  1. Topic 1
    • t1
    • t2
    2025

    Answer: \((c)\ 2,\ 2\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction sequence is:

    \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{CH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KMnO_4}} X \xrightarrow[\mathrm{573\,K}]{\mathrm{Cu}} Y\)

    1. Identify the starting compound

      \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{CH}\) is isobutane, that is, \(2\)-methylpropane.

    2. Reaction with \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\)

      Controlled oxidation converts isobutane into tert-butyl alcohol:

      \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{CH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KMnO_4}} (\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{COH}\)

      So, \(X = (\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{COH}\) (tert-butyl alcohol).

    3. Heating alcohol with copper at \(\mathrm{573\,K}\)

      Alcohol vapours passed over heated copper undergo characteristic reactions:

      • Primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
      • Secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
      • Tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) alkene by dehydration

      Since \(X\) is a tertiary alcohol, it gives alkene:

      \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{COH} \xrightarrow[\mathrm{573\,K}]{\mathrm{Cu}} (\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\)

      So, \(Y = (\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\), which is isobutene.

    4. Now count \(sp^3\) and \(sp^2\) carbons in \(Y\)

      Structure of \(Y\):

      \((\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\)

      • The two methyl carbons \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) are single-bonded carbons, so they are \(sp^3\)-hybridised.
      • The two carbons involved in the double bond \(\mathrm{C{=}C}\) are \(sp^2\)-hybridised.

      Therefore:

      • Number of \(sp^3\) carbons \(= 2\)
      • Number of \(sp^2\) carbons \(= 2\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(c)\ 2,\ 2}\)

    Helpful structure view:

    •       CH3
       \
        C = CH2
       /
     CH3

    Why this method works:

    • In organic chemistry, hybridisation is decided by the bonding around each carbon.
    • Carbon making four sigma bonds is usually \(sp^3\)-hybridised.
    • Carbon participating in a double bond is \(sp^2\)-hybridised.
    • So once \(Y\) is identified correctly, counting becomes very direct.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Tertiary alcohols do not have \(\alpha\)-hydrogen required for normal dehydrogenation over copper.
    • Hence, on heating with Cu at \(\mathrm{573\,K}\), tertiary alcohols usually undergo dehydration to form alkenes.
    • In alkenes, the two double-bonded carbons are \(sp^2\)-hybridised.
    • Alkyl carbons attached only through single bonds are generally \(sp^3\)-hybridised.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Do not try to count hybridisation at the first step.
    • First identify \(X\), then immediately recall: tertiary alcohol \(+\) Cu at \(\mathrm{573\,K}\) \(\rightarrow\) alkene.
    • Write \(Y\) quickly as \((\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\).
    • Then count:

      \(2\) methyl carbons \(\Rightarrow 2\ sp^3\)

      \(2\) double-bond carbons \(\Rightarrow 2\ sp^2\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Assuming Cu at \(\mathrm{573\,K}\) always gives dehydrogenation product. That is true for primary and secondary alcohols, but tertiary alcohols behave differently.
    2. Counting the central carbon of alkene as \(sp^3\). Any carbon in a \(\mathrm{C{=}C}\) bond is \(sp^2\).
    3. Forgetting that both carbons of the double bond are \(sp^2\), not just one.
    4. Mixing up isobutane, tert-butyl alcohol, and isobutene during the sequence.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Reactions of alcohols and hybridisation in organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Behaviour of tertiary alcohols over heated copper and counting \(sp^3/sp^2\) carbons

    Final exam-style answer:

    \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{CH}\) on oxidation with \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) gives \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{COH}\).

    This tertiary alcohol on heating with Cu at \(\mathrm{573\,K}\) gives \((\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\).

    In \((\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{C{=}CH_2}\), the two methyl carbons are \(sp^3\)-hybridised and the two double-bond carbons are \(sp^2\)-hybridised.

    Hence, the number of \(sp^3\) and \(sp^2\) carbons are respectively: \(2,\ 2\).

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Answer: \((d)\) position isomers


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:



    The reaction sequence is:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_2O/H^+]{(i)\ CH_3MgBr} (A) \xrightarrow[\Delta]{H_2SO_4} (B) \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_2O,\ H_2O_2/OH^-]{(i)\ B_2H_6} (C)}\)

    1. Find \(A\)

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) is ethanal. Grignard reagent \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) adds a methyl group to the carbonyl carbon.

      First, the alkoxide is formed:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO + CH_3MgBr \rightarrow CH_3CH(CH_3)OMgBr}\)

      On hydrolysis:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(CH_3)OMgBr \xrightarrow{H^+/H_2O} CH_3CH(CH_3)OH}\)

      So,

      \(A = \mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_3}\), which is propan-\(2\)-ol.

    2. Find \(B\)

      Propan-\(2\)-ol on heating with \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) undergoes dehydration to give propene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_3 \xrightarrow[\Delta]{H_2SO_4} CH_3CH{=}CH_2}\)

      Hence,

      \(B = \mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CH_2}\), that is, propene.

    3. Find \(C\)

      Propene undergoes hydroboration-oxidation with \(\mathrm{B_2H_6}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_2O_2/OH^-}\).

      This gives anti-Markovnikov addition of water, so \(-\mathrm{OH}\) goes to the terminal carbon:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_2O,\ H_2O_2/OH^-]{(i)\ B_2H_6} CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}\)

      Therefore,

      \(C = \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}\), which is propan-\(1\)-ol.

    4. Compare \(A\) and \(C\)
      • \(A = \mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_3}\) = propan-\(2\)-ol
      • \(C = \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}\) = propan-\(1\)-ol

      Both have the same molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_3H_8O}\) and the same functional group \((-\mathrm{OH})\), but the position of the \(-\mathrm{OH}\) group is different.

      Hence, \(A\) and \(C\) are position isomers.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(d)\ \text{position isomers}}\)

    Why this works:

    • Grignard reagents behave like carbon nucleophiles and add to aldehydes and ketones.
    • Aldehyde \(+\) Grignard reagent \(+\) hydrolysis gives an alcohol with one new carbon-carbon bond.
    • Concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) and heat convert alcohols to alkenes by dehydration.
    • Hydroboration-oxidation of an alkene gives alcohol in anti-Markovnikov orientation.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • \(\mathrm{RMgX}\) is a very important reagent for C-C bond formation.
    • Aldehydes except methanal give secondary alcohols with Grignard reagents.
    • Dehydration of alcohols with \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) is a standard elimination reaction.
    • Hydroboration-oxidation is a key method for preparing alcohols where \(-\mathrm{OH}\) appears at the less substituted carbon.
    • Position isomerism means:

      same carbon skeleton \(+\) same functional group \(+\) different position of that group.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO + CH_3MgBr}\) and immediately think:

      \(\mathrm{aldehyde + Grignard \rightarrow 2^\circ\ alcohol}\)

    • So \(A\) is propan-\(2\)-ol.
    • \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4,\Delta}\) means dehydration \(\rightarrow\) propene.
    • \(\mathrm{B_2H_6 / H_2O_2,OH^-}\) means anti-Markovnikov alcohol \(\rightarrow\) propan-\(1\)-ol.
    • Propan-\(2\)-ol and propan-\(1\)-ol differ only in position of \(-\mathrm{OH}\).
    • So mark position isomers.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking hydroboration-oxidation gives Markovnikov addition. It gives anti-Markovnikov product.
    2. Calling \(A\) and \(C\) functional isomers. They are not, because both are alcohols.
    3. Confusing position isomers with metamers. Metamerism is not applicable here.
    4. Missing that \(A\) is a secondary alcohol, not a primary alcohol.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Preparation and reactions of alcohols; isomerism
    • Subtopic: Grignard reagent addition, dehydration of alcohols, hydroboration-oxidation, position isomerism

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) reacts with \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) followed by hydrolysis to give \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_3}\), so \(A\) is propan-\(2\)-ol.

    On dehydration with \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) and heat, it gives propene.

    Propene on hydroboration-oxidation gives \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}\), so \(C\) is propan-\(1\)-ol.

    Since \(A\) and \(C\) have the same molecular formula and same functional group but differ in the position of the \(-\mathrm{OH}\) group, they are position isomers.

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Answer: \((c)\ \mathrm{I < II < III < IV}\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify the common framework

      All four compounds are para-substituted benzoic acids. So the acidic strength depends mainly on how the para-substituent affects the stability of the conjugate base \(\mathrm{ArCOO^-}\).

    2. Main idea: electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity

      A carboxylic acid becomes more acidic when the group attached to the ring withdraws electron density, because this stabilizes the conjugate base formed after loss of \(\mathrm{H^+}\).

      Electron-donating groups do the opposite: they push electron density toward the ring and the \(\mathrm{-COO^-}\) system, making the conjugate base less stable, so acidity decreases.

    3. Now compare the substituents
      • I: \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) is strongly electron-donating by resonance at para position \((+R)\). Although it has a weak \(-I\) effect, its resonance donation dominates here.

        So it decreases acidity the most.

      • II: \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) is electron-donating by \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation.

        So it also decreases acidity, but less than \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\).

      • III: \(\mathrm{-CN}\) is a strong electron-withdrawing group by \(-I\) effect and also withdraws through conjugation.

        So it increases acidity.

      • IV: \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) is one of the strongest electron-withdrawing groups by both \(-I\) and \(-R\) effects.

        So it increases acidity the most.

    4. Hence the acidity order is

      \(\mathrm{-OCH_3 < -CH_3 < -CN < -NO_2}\)

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{I < II < III < IV}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(c)\ \mathrm{I < II < III < IV}}\)

    Why this works:

    • Acidity depends on stability of the conjugate base.
    • In aromatic carboxylic acids, substituents on the benzene ring affect that stability through inductive effect and resonance effect.
    • Electron-withdrawing groups stabilize \(\mathrm{ArCOO^-}\), so acidity increases.
    • Electron-donating groups destabilize \(\mathrm{ArCOO^-}\), so acidity decreases.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • In carboxylic acids, acidity increases when the conjugate base is stabilized.
    • \(-I\) groups such as \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\), \(\mathrm{-CN}\), halogens increase acidity.
    • \(+I\) or \(+R\) groups such as alkyl and \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) usually decrease acidity.
    • For substituted benzoic acids, para substituents can show both inductive and resonance effects.
    • At para position, \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) is especially important because its resonance donation makes the acid weaker.

    Quick comparison memory line:

    • \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\): donating by resonance \(\Rightarrow\) weakest acid
    • \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\): donating by \(+I\) \(\Rightarrow\) weak acid
    • \(\mathrm{-CN}\): withdrawing \(\Rightarrow\) stronger acid
    • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\): very strongly withdrawing \(\Rightarrow\) strongest acid

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First ask only one thing:

      Which group withdraws electrons most strongly?

    • Stronger withdrawal \(\Rightarrow\) stronger acid.
    • Instantly rank the substituents:

      \(\mathrm{-OCH_3 < -CH_3 < -CN < -NO_2}\)

    • Then map that directly to compounds:

      \(\mathrm{I < II < III < IV}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) always withdraws because oxygen is electronegative.

      At para position in an aromatic ring, its resonance donation is very important and makes the acid weaker.

    2. Forgetting that acidity is decided by conjugate base stability, not by the acid molecule alone.
    3. Confusing \(\mathrm{-CN}\) and \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\).

      Both increase acidity, but \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) is generally stronger.

    4. Using only inductive effect without checking whether resonance operates at ortho/para positions.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Acidic strength of carboxylic acids
    • Subtopic: Effect of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents on substituted benzoic acids

    Final exam-style answer:

    Acidic strength of substituted benzoic acids depends on the effect of the substituent on stability of the conjugate base.

    Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity, while electron-donating groups decrease acidity.

    Here:

    \(\mathrm{-OCH_3 < -CH_3 < -CN < -NO_2}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\mathrm{I < II < III < IV}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)\ \mathrm{I < II < III < IV}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Acidity rule: more stable conjugate base \(\Rightarrow\) stronger acid
    • Electron-withdrawing group: acidity increases
    • Electron-donating group: acidity decreases
    • Strong withdrawing groups: \(\mathrm{-NO_2,\ -CN}\)
    • Donating groups here: \(\mathrm{-CH_3,\ -OCH_3}\)
    • Important para case: \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) donates by resonance, so it weakens benzoic acid

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{III < I < II < IV}\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The compounds are:

    • \(\mathrm{I = CH_3 - O - CH_3}\) (dimethyl ether)
    • \(\mathrm{II = CH_3CHO}\) (ethanal)
    • \(\mathrm{III = CH_3CH_2CH_3}\) (propane)
    • \(\mathrm{IV = CH_3CH_2OH}\) (ethanol)

    Boiling point mainly depends on the strength of intermolecular forces. Stronger intermolecular attraction means more energy is needed to separate molecules, so the boiling point becomes higher.

    1. Compare the type of intermolecular force in each compound
      • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_3}\) (III): This is an alkane. It is non-polar, so only London dispersion forces (van der Waals' forces) are present.

        These are the weakest among the four.

      • \(\mathrm{CH_3 - O - CH_3}\) (I): Ether is polar because of the \(\mathrm{C-O-C}\) bond angle and electronegative oxygen. So it has dipole-dipole attraction in addition to dispersion forces.

        Hence its boiling point is higher than propane.

      • \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) (II): Aldehyde contains the \(\mathrm{C=O}\) group, which is strongly polar. Therefore dipole-dipole attraction is stronger here than in ethers.

        So its boiling point is higher than dimethyl ether.

      • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}\) (IV): Alcohol contains an \(\mathrm{O-H}\) bond, so molecules can form hydrogen bonding.

        Hydrogen bonding is the strongest intermolecular force among these four cases.

    2. Hence arrange them from weakest to strongest intermolecular force

      \(\text{London dispersion} < \text{dipole-dipole} < \text{stronger dipole-dipole} < \text{hydrogen bonding}\)

      So the boiling point order becomes:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_3 < CH_3 - O - CH_3 < CH_3CHO < CH_3CH_2OH}\)

    3. Map back to the labels

      \(\mathrm{III < I < II < IV}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{III < I < II < IV}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Boiling involves separating molecules from one another, so it depends on intermolecular forces, not bond breaking inside the molecule.
    • A molecule with stronger attraction between its molecules needs more heat, hence has a higher boiling point.
    • For small organic molecules, a very useful order is:

      \(\text{van der Waals' forces} < \text{dipole-dipole attraction} < \text{hydrogen bonding}\)

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Alkanes are non-polar, so they mainly show London dispersion forces.
    • Ethers are polar, so they have dipole-dipole attraction, but they do not show intermolecular hydrogen bonding among themselves.
    • Aldehydes and ketones have a strongly polar \(\mathrm{C=O}\) bond, so their dipole-dipole attraction is significant.
    • Alcohols show intermolecular hydrogen bonding because of the \(\mathrm{O-H}\) group, so they usually have much higher boiling points than comparable ethers and hydrocarbons.
    • Boiling point trends often follow:

      \(\text{hydrogen bonding} > \text{polarity} > \text{non-polar character}\)

    Helpful comparison idea:

    • Propane: only temporary induced dipoles
    • Dimethyl ether: permanent dipole, but no \(\mathrm{O-H}\)
    • Ethanal: stronger permanent dipole because of \(\mathrm{C=O}\)
    • Ethanol: can form hydrogen bonds

    Text-only diagram:

    • Weakest                    Strongest
Propane   <   Ether   <   Aldehyde   <   Alcohol
LDF           dipole       stronger      H-bonding
                            dipole

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First check: Which one can form hydrogen bonding?

      That must have the highest boiling point. Here it is \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}\).

    • Then identify the non-polar molecule.

      That must have the lowest boiling point. Here it is \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_3}\).

    • Now compare the middle two:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) has a strongly polar \(\mathrm{C=O}\) group, so it boils higher than ether.

    • So directly:

      \(\mathrm{III < I < II < IV}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing molecular mass with the only deciding factor.

      Mass matters, but here the type of intermolecular force is more important.

    2. Thinking ether also shows hydrogen bonding among its own molecules.

      Ether oxygen can accept hydrogen bonds, but dimethyl ether has no \(\mathrm{O-H}\) bond, so it cannot form intermolecular hydrogen bonding with itself like alcohol does.

    3. Placing aldehyde below ether just because both contain oxygen.

      The \(\mathrm{C=O}\) group is more strongly polar than the ether linkage.

    4. Using bond strength inside the molecule instead of intermolecular attraction.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - Alcohols, Ethers and Carbonyl Compounds / General Organic Properties
    • Topic: Boiling point and intermolecular forces in organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Comparison of boiling points using van der Waals' forces, dipole-dipole forces and hydrogen bonding

    Final exam-style answer:

    Boiling point depends on the strength of intermolecular forces. \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_3}\) is non-polar and has only van der Waals' forces, so it has the lowest boiling point. Ether \(\mathrm{CH_3 - O - CH_3}\) is polar, so its boiling point is higher. Aldehyde \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) has a strongly polar \(\mathrm{C=O}\) group, so its boiling point is still higher. Alcohol \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}\) shows hydrogen bonding, so it has the highest boiling point.

    Therefore:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_3 < CH_3 - O - CH_3 < CH_3CHO < CH_3CH_2OH}\)

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{III < I < II < IV}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Boiling point depends on:

      strength of intermolecular forces

    • Force order:

      \(\text{London dispersion} < \text{dipole-dipole} < \text{hydrogen bonding}\)

    • Alkane:

      lowest among similar-sized molecules

    • Ether:

      polar, but no self hydrogen bonding

    • Aldehyde:

      strong \(\mathrm{C=O}\) dipole

    • Alcohol:

      highest because of \(\mathrm{O-H}\) hydrogen bonding

    • Direct order here:

      \(\mathrm{III < I < II < IV}\)

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{P = C_6H_5CH_2NH_2;\ Q = C_6H_5NH_2}\)


    Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The given compound is benzamide, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2}\).

    1. Formation of \(P\) with \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_2O}\)

      \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) is a strong reducing agent. It reduces amides \(\mathrm{RCONH_2}\) to amines \(\mathrm{RCH_2NH_2}\).

      So:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow[\ H_2O\ ]{LiAlH_4} C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\)

      Therefore, \(P\) is benzylamine, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\).

    2. Formation of \(Q\) with \(\mathrm{Br_2/NaOH}\)

      This is the Hofmann bromamide reaction (also called Hofmann degradation).

      In this reaction, a primary amide \(\mathrm{RCONH_2}\) gives a primary amine \(\mathrm{RNH_2}\) with loss of one carbon atom.

      So:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/NaOH} C_6H_5NH_2}\)

      Therefore, \(Q\) is aniline, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\).

    3. Hence the correct option is

      \(\mathrm{P = C_6H_5CH_2NH_2,\quad Q = C_6H_5NH_2}\)

    Reaction summary:

    • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow[\ H_2O\ ]{LiAlH_4} C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\)
    • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/NaOH} C_6H_5NH_2}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{P = C_6H_5CH_2NH_2;\ Q = C_6H_5NH_2}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) reduces the carbonyl group of an amide to a methylene group.
    • So the amide carbonyl carbon remains in the molecule, giving:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \rightarrow RCH_2NH_2}\)

    • In Hofmann bromamide reaction, the carbonyl carbon is removed from the chain.
    • So:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \rightarrow RNH_2}\)

    • That is why the two reagents give different amines from the same amide.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Reduction of amides:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \xrightarrow[\ H_2O\ ]{LiAlH_4} RCH_2NH_2}\)

    • Hofmann bromamide reaction:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/NaOH} RNH_2}\)

    • Hofmann bromamide reaction gives a primary amine with one carbon less than the amide.
    • \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) does not remove the carbonyl carbon; it reduces it.
    • This is a very common comparison in amines conversion questions.

    Helpful memory trick:

    • \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\): keeps the carbon chain, converts \(\mathrm{CO}\) into \(\mathrm{CH_2}\)
    • \(\mathrm{Br_2/NaOH}\): removes one carbon

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) on an amide \(\Rightarrow\) think

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \rightarrow RCH_2NH_2}\)

    • See \(\mathrm{Br_2/NaOH}\) on an amide \(\Rightarrow\) think

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \rightarrow RNH_2}\)

    • So from benzamide:

      \(P = \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\), \(Q = \mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\)

    • This directly gives option \((b)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing reduction of amide with Hofmann bromamide reaction.
    2. Forgetting that Hofmann reaction gives amine with one carbon less.
    3. Writing aniline for the \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) product.
    4. Thinking \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) removes the carbonyl carbon. It does not.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Amines
    • Topic: Preparation of amines
    • Subtopic: Reduction of amides and Hofmann bromamide degradation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Benzamide on reduction with \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) followed by hydrolysis gives benzylamine:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow[\ H_2O\ ]{LiAlH_4} C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\)

    Benzamide with \(\mathrm{Br_2/NaOH}\) undergoes Hofmann bromamide reaction and gives aniline:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/NaOH} C_6H_5NH_2}\)

    Hence:

    \(\mathrm{P = C_6H_5CH_2NH_2,\quad Q = C_6H_5NH_2}\)

    \(\boxed{(b)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Amide reduction:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \xrightarrow[\ H_2O\ ]{LiAlH_4} RCH_2NH_2}\)

    • Hofmann bromamide:

      \(\mathrm{RCONH_2 \xrightarrow{Br_2/NaOH} RNH_2}\)

    • Key idea:

      \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) keeps carbon count; Hofmann reduces carbon count by one

    • For benzamide:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\) with \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\)

    • For benzamide:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CONH_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5NH_2}\) with \(\mathrm{Br_2/NaOH}\)

    Answer: \((b)\ sp,\ \text{Neutral}\)

    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    p-Block Elements – Group 14 (Carbon Family) TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify the organic acid \(X\)

      The organic acid which gives a gas on dehydration with concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) at \(373\ \mathrm{K}\) is formic acid, \(\mathrm{HCOOH}\).

      The reaction is:

      \(\mathrm{HCOOH \xrightarrow[\ 373\ K\ ]{conc.\ H_2SO_4} CO\uparrow + H_2O}\)

      So, gas \(Y\) is carbon monoxide, \(\mathrm{CO}\).

    2. Find the hybridisation of carbon in \(Y\)

      In \(\mathrm{CO}\), carbon and oxygen are joined by a multiple bond and the carbon atom has two electron domains in the bonding picture used at this level.

      Therefore, carbon in \(\mathrm{CO}\) is \(sp\)-hybridised.

    3. Find the nature of \(Y\)

      \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a neutral oxide.

      It is neither acidic nor basic under normal conditions considered in NCERT/JEE chemistry.

    4. Hence the correct option

      Hybridisation of carbon in \(Y\) is \(sp\) and the nature of \(Y\) is neutral.

      Therefore, the correct answer is \(\boxed{(b)}\).

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{sp,\ \text{Neutral}}\)

    \(\boxed{(b)}\)

    Reaction summary:

    • Acid involved: \(\mathrm{HCOOH}\) (formic acid)
    • Dehydrating agent: concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\)
    • Gas formed: \(\mathrm{CO}\)
    • Water also formed: \(\mathrm{H_2O}\)

    Why \(sp\)-hybridisation comes here:

    • \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a linear molecule.
    • Linear geometry corresponds to \(sp\)-hybridisation.
    • One \(sp\) orbital of carbon participates in sigma bonding, and the remaining unhybridised \(p\)-orbitals take part in \(\pi\)-bonding.

    JEE / NCERT theory to remember:

    • Formic acid is the simplest carboxylic acid:

      \(\mathrm{HCOOH}\)

    • On heating with concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\), formic acid undergoes dehydration:

      \(\mathrm{HCOOH \xrightarrow[\ 373\ K\ ]{conc.\ H_2SO_4} CO + H_2O}\)

    • Concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) acts as a dehydrating agent.
    • \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a neutral oxide, just like \(\mathrm{NO}\) and \(\mathrm{N_2O}\) in standard classification.
    • \(\mathrm{CO}\) is also an important ligand in coordination chemistry, but in this question only its hybridisation and oxide nature matter.

    How to identify this instantly in exam:

    • See organic acid + conc. \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) + \(373\ \mathrm{K}\).
    • Recall the famous reaction of formic acid.
    • Formic acid gives \(\mathrm{CO}\), not \(\mathrm{CO_2}\).
    • Then immediately note:

      \(\mathrm{CO}\) is linear \(\Rightarrow sp\)

      \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a neutral oxide \(\Rightarrow\) neutral

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Memorize this direct reaction:

      \(\mathrm{HCOOH \xrightarrow{conc.\ H_2SO_4} CO + H_2O}\)

    • Once \(Y = \mathrm{CO}\), the rest is immediate:

      \(\mathrm{CO}\) is linear \(\Rightarrow sp\)

      \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a neutral oxide \(\Rightarrow\) neutral

    • So directly mark \((b)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing dehydration of formic acid with oxidation reactions of formic acid.
    2. Thinking the gas formed is \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) instead of \(\mathrm{CO}\).
    3. Marking \(\mathrm{CO}\) as acidic because it is an oxide of carbon.
    4. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) is acidic, but \(\mathrm{CO}\) is neutral.
    5. Mixing up \(sp\) and \(sp^2\) hybridisation.

    Quick comparison that helps:

    Compound Nature / key point
    \(\mathrm{CO}\) Neutral oxide, carbon is \(sp\)-hybridised
    \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) Acidic oxide, carbon is \(sp\)-hybridised
    \(\mathrm{SO_2}\) Acidic oxide, sulphur is \(sp^2\)-hybridised

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Compounds Containing Oxygen
    • Topic: Carboxylic acids
    • Subtopic: Dehydration reaction of formic acid and identification of product

    Extra NCERT-linked memory point:

    • Among carboxylic acids, formic acid shows some special behavior because it contains the \(\mathrm{-CHO}\)-like hydrogen.
    • Its dehydration with concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) is a very standard identification reaction.

    Final exam-style answer:

    The organic acid \(X\) is formic acid, \(\mathrm{HCOOH}\).

    On dehydration with concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) at \(373\ \mathrm{K}\), it gives:

    \(\mathrm{HCOOH \xrightarrow[\ 373\ K\ ]{conc.\ H_2SO_4} CO + H_2O}\)

    Thus, \(Y = \mathrm{CO}\).

    Carbon in \(\mathrm{CO}\) is \(sp\)-hybridised and \(\mathrm{CO}\) is a neutral oxide.

    \(\boxed{(b)\ sp,\ Neutral}\)

    Answer: \((a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify product \(X\)

      Ethane undergoes controlled oxidation in presence of manganese acetate \((\mathrm{CH_3COO})_2\mathrm{Mn}\) and heat to give acetic acid.

      \( \mathrm{C_2H_6 + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow[(\Delta)]{(CH_3COO)_2Mn} CH_3COOH} \)

      Hence, \(X = \mathrm{CH_3COOH}\) (ethanoic acid).

    2. Identify product \(Y\)

      Carboxylic acids react with sodium metal to form sodium carboxylate.

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COOH + Na \rightarrow CH_3COO^-Na^+ + \frac{1}{2}H_2} \)

      So, \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COONa}\) (sodium acetate).

    3. Now apply Kolbe’s electrolysis

      In aqueous solution, sodium acetate gives acetate ion:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COONa \rightarrow CH_3COO^- + Na^+} \)

      At the anode, acetate ion loses electron:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COO^- \rightarrow CH_3^\bullet + CO_2 + e^-} \)

      Two methyl radicals combine:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3^\bullet + CH_3^\bullet \rightarrow C_2H_6} \)

      Therefore overall anode reaction is:

      \( \mathrm{2CH_3COO^- \rightarrow C_2H_6 + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

      So the gases formed at anode are:

      • \(P = \mathrm{C_2H_6}\)
      • \(Q = \mathrm{CO_2}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2}\)

    Key theory you should know:

    • Kolbe’s electrolysis: Electrolysis of aqueous solution of sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids gives alkane at the anode.
    • General anode reaction:

      \( \mathrm{2RCOO^- \rightarrow R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

    • Thus:

      \( \mathrm{RCOO^- \xrightarrow{electrolysis} alkane\ with\ even\ number\ of\ carbon\ atoms} \)

    • For acetate ion \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}\), the radical formed is \(\mathrm{CH_3^\bullet}\), so coupling gives ethane.
    • The decarboxylation step explains why \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) is also produced.

    Very important JEE / NCERT pattern:

    • Sodium salt of carboxylic acid \(\rightarrow\) on Kolbe electrolysis gives alkane by radical coupling.
    • If the acid is:

      \(\mathrm{RCOOH}\)

      its sodium salt is:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa}\)

      and electrolysis gives:

      \(\mathrm{R-R}\)

    • Carbon count trick:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^- \rightarrow CH_3^\bullet \rightarrow C_2H_6}\)

    1-minute solving trick:

    • As soon as you see carboxylate salt + electrolysis, think Kolbe electrolysis.
    • Write instantly:

      \( \mathrm{2RCOO^- \rightarrow R-R + 2CO_2} \)

    • Here \(R=\mathrm{CH_3}\), so product alkane must be:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_3 = C_2H_6}\)

    • The second gas is automatically \(\mathrm{CO_2}\).
    • So no need to work through long mechanism in exam if you recognize the pattern fast.

    Quick intuitive view:

    • Acetate ion:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}\)

    • Loses \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) at anode and leaves methyl fragment:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3^\bullet}\)

    • Two methyl fragments join: 
      CH3•  +  •CH3  →  CH3—CH3

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking \(\mathrm{CH_4}\) forms at anode. In Kolbe electrolysis, radical coupling gives \(\mathrm{C_2H_6}\), not methane.
    2. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) is released due to decarboxylation.
    3. Confusing sodium metal reaction with soda lime decarboxylation. These are different reactions.
    4. Ignoring that the question asks gases at anode, not cathode.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carboxylic acids and their salts
    • Subtopic: Kolbe’s electrolysis of sodium carboxylates

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(X\) is ethanoic acid, \(\mathrm{CH_3COOH}\).

    On reaction with sodium, it gives sodium acetate, so \(Y=\mathrm{CH_3COONa}\).

    During Kolbe’s electrolysis:

    \( \mathrm{2CH_3COO^- \rightarrow C_2H_6 + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

    Hence, \(P=\mathrm{C_2H_6}\) and \(Q=\mathrm{CO_2}\).

    \(\boxed{(a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2}\)

    Answer: \((c)\)

    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    Reimer-Tiemann reaction is the ortho-formylation of phenol. In this reaction, phenol reacts with chloroform in aqueous sodium hydroxide, and after hydrolysis / acidification, the major product is o-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde).

    1. Identify the named reaction first

      Reimer-Tiemann reaction is:

      \(\text{Phenol} \xrightarrow[\text{aq. NaOH}]{\text{CHCl}_3} \text{o-hydroxybenzaldehyde (major)}\)

      So immediately:

      • \(Y = \text{CHCl}_3\)
      • \(Z = \text{aq. NaOH}\)
      • \(X = \text{salicylaldehyde}\)
    2. Match this with the options

      The correct option must contain:

      • Product \(X\) as 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde)
      • Reactants \(Y\) and \(Z\) as \(\text{CHCl}_3\) and aq. \(\text{NaOH}\)

      Only option \((c)\) matches this completely.

    3. Reaction idea from the mechanism

      In basic medium, chloroform produces dichlorocarbene:

      \(\text{CHCl}_3 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow :\text{CCl}_2\)

      Phenol in base forms phenoxide ion, which is highly activating and directs substitution mainly at the ortho position.

      Then after a sequence of steps, the \(-\text{CHCl}_2\) group formed on the ring is converted into the aldehyde group \(-\text{CHO}\).

    4. Major product formed

      Thus the major product is:

      \(\text{o-hydroxybenzaldehyde} = \text{salicylaldehyde}\)

      • Minor amount of para product may form
      • But the ortho product is major
    5. Useful reaction flow


    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Why this method works:

    • This question is a named reaction identification question.
    • Once you know Reimer-Tiemann reaction, the answer becomes direct.
    • Phenol with \(\text{CHCl}_3/\text{aq. NaOH}\) gives salicylaldehyde as the major product.
    • So instead of analyzing all four structures in detail, you can solve it by recalling the standard reaction pattern.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Named reaction: Reimer-Tiemann reaction
    • Substrate: Phenol / phenoxide ion
    • Reagents: \(\text{CHCl}_3\) and aqueous \(\text{NaOH}\)
    • Electrophile generated: dichlorocarbene, \(:\text{CCl}_2\)
    • Major product: ortho-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde)
    • Minor product: para-hydroxybenzaldehyde
    • Phenoxide ion is strongly activating and directs substitution at ortho/para positions.
    • After introduction of the carbene-derived group, hydrolysis converts it into the aldehyde group \(-\text{CHO}\).

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See Reimer-Tiemann \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think phenol + \(\text{CHCl}_3\) + aq. \(\text{NaOH}\).
    • Then think formylation of phenol.
    • Major product is ortho-hydroxybenzaldehyde.
    • So in one glance, pick the option containing:

      \(\text{salicylaldehyde},\ \text{CHCl}_3,\ \text{aq. NaOH}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Reimer-Tiemann reaction with Kolbe reaction.
    2. Choosing \(\text{COOH}\) instead of \(\text{CHO}\) as the product group.
    3. Forgetting that reagent is \(\text{CHCl}_3\), not \(\text{CCl}_4\).
    4. Forgetting that the major product is ortho, not para.
    5. Assuming benzene itself undergoes this reaction; the reacting substrate is phenol.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Chemical reactions of phenols
    • Subtopic: Reimer-Tiemann reaction

    Related comparison for quick revision:

    • Reimer-Tiemann reaction:

      \(\text{Phenol} \xrightarrow[\text{aq. NaOH}]{\text{CHCl}_3} \text{salicylaldehyde}\)

    • Kolbe reaction:

      \(\text{Sodium phenoxide} \xrightarrow[\text{pressure}]{\text{CO}_2} \text{salicylic acid}\)

    • So if product has \(-\text{CHO}\), think Reimer-Tiemann.
    • If product has \(-\text{COOH}\), think Kolbe.

    Final exam-style answer:

    In Reimer-Tiemann reaction, phenol reacts with \(\text{CHCl}_3\) and aqueous \(\text{NaOH}\) to give o-hydroxybenzaldehyde (salicylaldehyde) as the major product.

    Hence:

    • \(X =\) salicylaldehyde
    • \(Y = \text{CHCl}_3\)
    • \(Z = \text{aq. NaOH}\)

    Therefore, the correct option is \(\boxed{(c)}\).

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Reaction name: Reimer-Tiemann reaction
    • Substrate: Phenol
    • Reagents: \(\text{CHCl}_3\), aq. \(\text{NaOH}\)
    • Key intermediate: \(:\text{CCl}_2\)
    • Major product: salicylaldehyde
    • Functional group introduced: \(-\text{CHO}\)
    • Orientation: ortho major, para minor

    Answer: \((a)\ \text{B and C only}\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction sequence involves two standard reactions from aromatic aldehydes.

    1. Identify \(X\) from toluene

      Toluene on treatment with chromyl chloride in \(\mathrm{CS_2}\) followed by hydrolysis \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\) undergoes Etard reaction.

      So:

      \(\text{Toluene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{H_3O^+}]{(i)\ \mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}} \text{Benzaldehyde}\)

      Hence, \(X =\) benzaldehyde \(\left(\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO}\right)\).

    2. Now identify \(Y\) and \(Z\)

      Benzaldehyde does not have any \(\alpha\)-hydrogen. Therefore, with concentrated \(\mathrm{NaOH}\), it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction.

      In Cannizzaro reaction:

      \(2\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO} + \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH} + \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\)

      Therefore:

      • \(Y = \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH}\) = benzyl alcohol
      • \(Z = \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\) = sodium benzoate
    3. Check statement A

      Statement A says: Y is a secondary alcohol.

      But \(Y\) is benzyl alcohol, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH}\).

      The carbon bearing \(-\mathrm{OH}\) is attached to only one other carbon, so it is a primary alcohol, not secondary.

      Hence, A is false.

    4. Check statement B

      In Cannizzaro reaction, one molecule of aldehyde gets oxidized to carboxylate and the other gets reduced to alcohol.

      So benzaldehyde \((X)\) is reduced to benzyl alcohol \((Y)\).

      Hence, B is true.

    5. Check statement C

      \(Z\) is sodium benzoate, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\).

      On heating sodium carboxylates with sodalime, decarboxylation occurs:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[\Delta]{CaO} RH + Na_2CO_3}\)

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[\Delta]{CaO} C_6H_6 + Na_2CO_3}\)

      So sodium benzoate gives benzene on heating with sodalime.

      Hence, C is true.

    6. Check statement D

      Statement D says: Y does not give \(\mathrm{H_2}\) gas with Na metal.

      But alcohols do react with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas:

      \(2\mathrm{ROH} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{RONa} + \mathrm{H_2}\uparrow\)

      Since \(Y\) is benzyl alcohol, it does give \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with sodium.

      Hence, D is false.

    Therefore:

    Only statements B and C are correct.

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{B and C only}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • The first step is recognition of Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Toluene} \rightarrow \text{benzaldehyde}\)

    • The second step is recognition of Cannizzaro reaction, which is shown by:
      • an aldehyde
      • no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen
      • concentrated base
    • Once \(X =\) benzaldehyde is identified, \(Y\) and \(Z\) follow immediately as benzyl alcohol and sodium benzoate.
    • Then each statement can be tested using basic functional-group chemistry.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Ar-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2}} \text{Ar-CHO}\)

      Toluene is oxidized to benzaldehyde without full oxidation to benzoic acid.

    • Cannizzaro reaction:

      Aldehydes without \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo disproportionation in concentrated alkali.

      \(2\mathrm{RCHO} + \mathrm{OH^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCH_2OH} + \mathrm{RCOO^-}\)

    • Benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it gives Cannizzaro reaction.
    • Sodalime decarboxylation:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH/CaO}} \mathrm{RH}\)

    • Alcohols react with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}\) on toluene \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think Etard reaction \(\Rightarrow X=\) benzaldehyde.
    • See benzaldehyde + conc. \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think Cannizzaro reaction.
    • So directly write:

      \(Y=\) benzyl alcohol, \(Z=\) sodium benzoate

    • Then:
      • \(Y\) is primary, not secondary \(\Rightarrow A\) false
      • \(Y\) is reduction product of \(X\) \(\Rightarrow B\) true
      • \(Z\) gives benzene with sodalime \(\Rightarrow C\) true
      • Alcohol gives \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with Na \(\Rightarrow D\) false
    • Hence answer is \((a)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Etard oxidation with full side-chain oxidation to benzoic acid.
    2. Forgetting that benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    3. Thinking Cannizzaro gives two different alcohols; it gives one alcohol and one carboxylate salt.
    4. Calling benzyl alcohol a secondary alcohol. It is a primary alcohol.
    5. Forgetting that alcohols react with sodium metal to evolve \(\mathrm{H_2}\).
    6. Missing that sodium benzoate on sodalime decarboxylation gives benzene.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Chemical reactions of aldehydes
    • Subtopic: Cannizzaro reaction of benzaldehyde

    Related chapter link:

    • The first step also connects with side-chain oxidation of aromatic compounds and named reaction:

      Etard reaction

    • The statement about \(Z\) also connects with:

      Decarboxylation of carboxylate salts using sodalime

    Final exam-style answer:

    Toluene on treatment with \(\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\) gives benzaldehyde \((X)\) by Etard reaction.

    Benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so with concentrated \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction:

    \(2\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO} + \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH} + \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\)

    Thus:

    • \(Y =\) benzyl alcohol
    • \(Z =\) sodium benzoate

    Now, \(Y\) is a primary alcohol, so A is false.

    \(Y\) is the reduction product of \(X\), so B is true.

    \(Z\) on heating with sodalime gives benzene, so C is true.

    \(Y\) gives \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with Na metal, so D is false.

    Therefore, the correct statements are B and C only.

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{B and C only}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Ar-CH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ar-CHO}\)

    • Benzaldehyde + conc. NaOH:

      \(\Rightarrow\) Cannizzaro reaction

    • Cannizzaro products:

      \(\text{alcohol} + \text{carboxylate salt}\)

    • For benzaldehyde:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH + C_6H_5COONa}\)

    • Sodalime rule:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa \rightarrow RH}\)

    • Alcohol + Na:

      \(\mathrm{H_2}\) gas is evolved

    • Key clue:

      \(\text{No }\alpha\text{-H} \Rightarrow \text{Cannizzaro possible}\)

    Answer: \((c)\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction is solved in two clear steps: ozonolysis followed by aldol condensation.

    1. Step 1: Ozonolysis of the given alkene

      The alkene shown is tetramethylethylene, i.e. \(2,3\)-dimethyl-\(2\)-butene.

      On ozonolysis with reductive workup \(\mathrm{O_3 / Zn, H_2O}\), the double bond is cleaved and each alkene carbon becomes a carbonyl carbon.

      Since both alkene carbons are equivalent and each is attached to two methyl groups, the product formed is acetone on both sides.

      So:

      \(\text{Alkene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{Zn/H_2O}]{(i)\ \mathrm{O_3}} 2\,\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\)

      Hence, \(A =\) acetone.

    2. Step 2: Reaction of acetone with \(\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}\) and heat

      Acetone has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen atoms, so in basic medium it undergoes aldol condensation.

      First, two molecules of acetone combine to form the aldol addition product:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3 + CH_3COCH_3 \rightarrow CH_3COCH_2C(OH)(CH_3)_2}\)

      On heating, this undergoes dehydration to give the \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      This matches option \((c)\).

    3. Reaction flow in one line

      \(\text{Tetramethylethylene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{Zn/H_2O}]{(i)\ \mathrm{O_3}} 2\,\text{acetone} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \Delta]{(i)\ \mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}} \text{mesityl oxide}\)

    4. Helpful reaction image


    Therefore:

    \(P = \mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Ozonolysis always breaks the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond and converts the two alkene carbons into carbonyl compounds.
    • So first identify what carbonyl fragment each alkene carbon becomes.
    • Here both sides are identical, so only one carbonyl compound forms: acetone.
    • Then ask what acetone does with base and heat.
    • Ketones with \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo aldol condensation, and on heating give an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated carbonyl compound.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{R_1R_2C=CR_3R_4 \xrightarrow{O_3} carbonyl\ compounds}\)

    • Reductive workup \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\) stops at aldehydes/ketones and prevents further oxidation.
    • Aldol condensation:

      Aldehydes and ketones having \(\alpha\)-hydrogen react in dilute/base medium to form \(\beta\)-hydroxy carbonyl compounds.

    • On heating, aldol products dehydrate to give \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated aldehydes or ketones.
    • Acetone self-condensation gives first diacetone alcohol, then on dehydration gives mesityl oxide.

    Key theory behind the second step:

    1. Formation of enolate

      Base removes an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen from acetone to form an enolate ion.

    2. Nucleophilic addition

      This enolate attacks the carbonyl carbon of another acetone molecule.

    3. Aldol product

      A \(\beta\)-hydroxy ketone is formed.

    4. Dehydration on heating

      Loss of water gives the conjugated enone, which is more stable.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See a highly symmetrical alkene in ozonolysis \(\Rightarrow\) check whether both halves are identical.
    • Here both alkene carbons are the same, so ozonolysis must give two identical carbonyl compounds.
    • That carbonyl is acetone.
    • Then immediately recall:

      \(\text{acetone} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{base}} \text{mesityl oxide}\)

    • Among the options, choose the \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone, not the aldol addition product.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Stopping after ozonolysis and not using the second reagent step.
    2. Writing aldehyde instead of ketone after ozonolysis.
    3. Choosing the aldol addition product instead of the dehydrated aldol condensation product.
    4. Forgetting that heating favors dehydration and formation of conjugation.
    5. Missing the symmetry of the starting alkene and unnecessarily creating two different carbonyl compounds.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carbonyl compounds
    • Subtopic: Aldol condensation and ozonolysis-based product prediction

    Final exam-style answer:

    On ozonolysis, the given alkene undergoes cleavage of the double bond to give two molecules of acetone.

    \(\mathrm{O_3/Zn,H_2O}\) therefore forms \(A = \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).

    Acetone has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so with \(\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}\) it undergoes aldol condensation, and on heating the aldol product dehydrates to form:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    This is mesityl oxide.

    Hence, the correct product is option \((c)\).

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Ozonolysis: breaks \(\mathrm{C=C}\) into carbonyl compounds
    • Reductive workup: \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\)
    • Acetone + base: aldol reaction possible
    • Heating after aldol: dehydration occurs
    • Final product type: \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone
    • Name of final product: mesityl oxide
    • Correct option: \((c)\)

    Answer: \((a)\)

    Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    This question is solved by identifying the reaction at each step: Grignard reagent formation, carboxylation, and then sodalime decarboxylation.

    2. Step 1: Formation of \(A\)

      The starting compound is \(m\)-bromonitrobenzene \(\left(3\text{-bromonitrobenzene}\right)\).

      On treatment with magnesium in dry ether, aryl bromide forms the corresponding Grignard reagent.

      So:

      \(\mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4Br \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{Mg} m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4MgBr}\)

      Hence, \(A = \mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4MgBr}\).

    3. Step 2: Conversion of \(A\) into \(B\)

      Grignard reagents react with carbon dioxide to give magnesium carboxylates, which on acidic hydrolysis form carboxylic acids.

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4MgBr \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_3O^+]{(i)\ CO_2} m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4COOH}\)

      So, \(B = m\)-nitrobenzoic acid.

    4. Step 3: Conversion of \(B\) into \(C\)

      The reagent \(\mathrm{Na}\) first converts the carboxylic acid into its sodium salt:

      \(\mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4COOH + Na \rightarrow m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4COONa + \tfrac{1}{2}H_2}\)

      Now sodium carboxylate on heating with sodalime \(\left(\mathrm{NaOH + CaO}\right)\) undergoes decarboxylation.

      General reaction:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} RH}\)

      So here:

      \(\mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4COONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} C_6H_5NO_2}\)

      Thus, \(C =\) nitrobenzene.

    5. Match with the options

      Nitrobenzene corresponds to option \((a)\).

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{C = \text{nitrobenzene}}\)

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Why this method works:

    • An aryl halide with \(\mathrm{Mg/ether}\) generally forms an aryl magnesium halide.
    • Any Grignard reagent with \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) followed by hydrolysis gives a carboxylic acid having one extra carbon.
    • Carboxylic acid salts on heating with sodalime lose \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) and give the corresponding hydrocarbon.
    • So the sequence is:

      \(\text{aryl halide} \rightarrow \text{Grignard reagent} \rightarrow \text{carboxylic acid} \rightarrow \text{decarboxylated product}\)

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Grignard reagent formation:

      \(\mathrm{R\text{-}X \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{Mg} R\text{-}MgX}\)

    • Carboxylation of Grignard reagent:

      \(\mathrm{RMgX + CO_2 \rightarrow RCOOMgX \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} RCOOH}\)

    • This is a standard method for converting an organic halide into a carboxylic acid with one additional carbon atom.
    • Sodalime decarboxylation:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} RH}\)

    • In aromatic systems:

      \(\mathrm{ArCOONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} ArH}\)

    • So sodium benzoate gives benzene, and substituted sodium benzoates give substituted benzenes.

    Reaction map:

    • \(\mathrm{m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4Br \xrightarrow{Mg/ether} m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4MgBr}\)

      \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow{CO_2/H_3O^+} m\text{-}NO_2C_6H_4COOH}\)

      \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow[\Delta]{Na,\ NaOH+CaO} C_6H_5NO_2}\)

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{Mg/ether}\) on aryl bromide \(\Rightarrow\) think Grignard reagent.
    • See \(\mathrm{CO_2/H_3O^+}\) \(\Rightarrow\) think \(+1\) carbon as \(-\mathrm{COOH}\).
    • See \(\mathrm{NaOH + CaO}\) with a carboxylate \(\Rightarrow\) think decarboxylation.
    • So mentally:

      \(\mathrm{ArBr \rightarrow ArMgBr \rightarrow ArCOOH \rightarrow ArH}\)

    • Since the ring already has \(\mathrm{NO_2}\), the final product must be nitrobenzene.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) adds one carbon and forms a carboxylic acid after hydrolysis.
    2. Choosing the carboxylic acid itself as the final answer and missing the last decarboxylation step.
    3. Forgetting that sodalime acts on the sodium salt of the acid.
    4. Assuming the \(\mathrm{NO_2}\) group is removed during decarboxylation. It is not removed here.
    5. Confusing Grignard carboxylation with Friedel-Crafts or Kolbe-type reactions.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
    • Topic: Organometallic compounds and reactions of haloarenes
    • Subtopic: Grignard reagent carboxylation and sodalime decarboxylation

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(m\)-Bromonitrobenzene reacts with magnesium in dry ether to form the Grignard reagent \(m\text{-}\mathrm{NO_2C_6H_4MgBr}\).

    On treatment with \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\), it gives \(m\)-nitrobenzoic acid.

    This acid forms its sodium salt with sodium, and on heating with sodalime undergoes decarboxylation to give nitrobenzene.

    Hence, the product \(C\) is nitrobenzene.

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • \(\mathrm{Mg/ether}\): forms Grignard reagent
    • \(\mathrm{CO_2/H_3O^+}\): converts Grignard reagent into carboxylic acid
    • Carbon count change: \(+1\)
    • \(\mathrm{NaOH + CaO,\ \Delta}\): sodalime decarboxylation
    • Key pattern:

      \(\mathrm{ArX \rightarrow ArMgX \rightarrow ArCOOH \rightarrow ArH}\)

    • Final product here: nitrobenzene

    Answer: \((c)\ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO,\ CH_3CH_2CHO}\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify the product type

      The given product is a \(\beta\)-hydroxy aldehyde. Such compounds are formed in aldol addition reactions.

      In aldol addition, an aldehyde or ketone having at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen forms an enolate/enol, which attacks the carbonyl carbon of another molecule.

    2. Observe the product carefully

      The product shown is:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2-CH(OH)-CH(CH_3)-CHO}\)

      It contains:

      • one aldehyde group \(\mathrm{(-CHO)}\)
      • one hydroxyl group on the \(\beta\)-carbon
      • a total of 6 carbon atoms
    3. Use NCERT/JEE aldol rule

      In self-aldol of an aldehyde \(\mathrm{RCH_2CHO}\), the product is generally:

      \(\mathrm{RCH(OH)CH(R)CHO}\)

      Here, if \(\mathrm{R = CH_3CH_2}\), then the aldehyde is \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO}\) (propanal).

      Its self-aldol product becomes:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH(CH_3)CHO}\)

      This matches the given compound exactly.

    4. Therefore

      Both \(A\) and \(B\) must be:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO}\)

      So the correct option is:

      \(\boxed{(c)\ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO,\ CH_3CH_2CHO}}\)

    Reaction idea:



    How to think about it structurally:

    • One molecule gives the enolate from the \(\alpha\)-carbon.
    • That \(\alpha\)-carbon attacks the carbonyl carbon of another aldehyde molecule.
    • After protonation, the attacked carbonyl becomes \(\mathrm{-CH(OH)-}\).
    • The original enolate-containing molecule still keeps the \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) group.

    Simple reaction pattern:

    • For self-aldol of propanal:

      \(\mathrm{2\,CH_3CH_2CHO \xrightarrow{OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH(CH_3)CHO}\)

    Why other options are wrong:

    1. Option (a)

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH}\) is an alcohol, not a carbonyl compound. Alcohols do not undergo aldol addition like aldehydes/ketones.

    2. Option (b)

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) is acetone. If acetone participates, the product pattern would be a \(\beta\)-hydroxy ketone or mixed aldol product, not the exact aldehyde shown here.

    3. Option (d)

      This mixed combination does not generate the exact carbon skeleton of the given product.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Aldol reaction: aldehydes and ketones having at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo aldol addition in presence of dilute base.
    • The product is a \(\beta\)-hydroxy aldehyde or \(\beta\)-hydroxy ketone.
    • \(\alpha\)-hydrogen means hydrogen on the carbon adjacent to the carbonyl carbon.
    • Formaldehyde \(\mathrm{HCHO}\) does not undergo aldol because it has no \(\alpha\)-carbon and hence no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    • Compounds without \(\alpha\)-hydrogen usually do not give aldol reaction.

    Fast 1-minute solving trick:

    • In aldol addition, first look for \(\beta\)-OH and terminal \(\mathrm{-CHO}\).
    • Mentally split the product between:

      \(\mathrm{-CH(OH)-CH(-)-CHO}\)

    • If both left and right side fragments suggest the same aldehyde unit, it is usually self-aldol.
    • Here the 6-carbon product strongly suggests:

      \(\mathrm{3\ carbon\ aldehyde + 3\ carbon\ aldehyde}\)

      that is \(\mathrm{propanal + propanal}\).

    Intuitive carbon-count method:

    • Product has \(6\) carbons.
    • In aldol addition, carbon skeleton is often the sum of the two reactant carbonyl compounds.
    • \(3 + 3 = 6\), so two molecules of propanal fit perfectly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking every base-catalysed carbonyl reaction is Cannizzaro reaction.
    2. Forgetting that aldol needs at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    3. Missing that the product is an aldehyde, not a ketone.
    4. Not checking the total carbon count before choosing the option.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Chemical reactions of aldehydes and ketones
    • Subtopic: Aldol condensation / aldol addition

    Final exam-style answer:

    The given product is a \(\beta\)-hydroxy aldehyde, so it is an aldol addition product. The structure \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH(CH_3)CHO}\) is obtained by self-aldol of propanal.

    Therefore:

    \(\mathrm{A = CH_3CH_2CHO,\quad B = CH_3CH_2CHO}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Aldol condition: carbonyl compound must have \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    • Reagent: usually dilute \(\mathrm{OH^-}\)
    • Product: \(\beta\)-hydroxy aldehyde / \(\beta\)-hydroxy ketone
    • Self-aldol of propanal:

      \(\mathrm{2\,CH_3CH_2CHO \xrightarrow{OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH(CH_3)CHO}\)

    • No \(\alpha\)-H \(\Rightarrow\) no aldol reaction

    Answer: \((b)\) Catechol


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    Intramolecular hydrogen bonding means hydrogen bonding within the same molecule.

    For this, the molecule must have:

    • a hydrogen bond donor such as \(\mathrm{-OH}\), and
    • a nearby electronegative atom such as \(\mathrm{O}\) placed suitably inside the same molecule.
    1. Check the relative positions of the groups

      Catechol is \(o\)-dihydroxybenzene, that is, the two \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups are at ortho positions.

      Because they are adjacent, one \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group can form a hydrogen bond with the oxygen of the other \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group within the same molecule.

    2. Why this gives intramolecular hydrogen bonding

      In catechol, the two neighbouring \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups can bend toward each other, so a six-membered hydrogen-bonded arrangement becomes possible.

      • \(\mathrm{O-H \cdots O}\) interaction forms inside one molecule.
      • Hence hydrogen bonding is intramolecular.
    3. Why the other options are not correct
      • Resorcinol is \(m\)-dihydroxybenzene. The \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups are too far apart for effective intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
      • Quinol is \(p\)-dihydroxybenzene. The \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups are opposite to each other, so intramolecular hydrogen bonding is not possible.
      • \(o\)-cresol has only one \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group and one \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) group. Since \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) cannot act as a hydrogen bond acceptor, intramolecular hydrogen bonding is not possible.

    Helpful structure:



    Simple diagram idea:

    •    OH
    \
     ⌬
    / \
 HO···O

    Why this works conceptually:

    • Hydrogen bonding needs proper distance and orientation.
    • In aromatic compounds, ortho-substitution often allows intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
    • Meta and para positions usually do not allow the required closeness.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Intramolecular hydrogen bonding: hydrogen bonding within the same molecule.
    • It generally decreases association between molecules and often lowers boiling point compared with strong intermolecularly hydrogen-bonded compounds.
    • Intermolecular hydrogen bonding: hydrogen bonding between different molecules.
    • Ortho-substituted compounds with suitable donor and acceptor groups commonly show intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
    • Catechol is a standard example because its two neighbouring \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups can interact internally.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Look for ortho arrangement first.
    • Ask: are there both donor and acceptor atoms inside the same molecule?
    • Among the options, only catechol has two adjacent \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups.
    • So directly mark \((b)\).

    More intuitive elimination:

    • \(m\)- and \(p\)-dihydroxy compounds: usually too far apart.
    • Single \(\mathrm{-OH}\) with \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\): no hydrogen bond acceptor available.
    • Two adjacent \(\mathrm{-OH}\): strong clue for intramolecular H-bonding.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking every compound with \(\mathrm{-OH}\) shows intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
    2. Forgetting that correct geometry and nearby acceptor atom are necessary.
    3. Confusing intramolecular and intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
    4. Choosing \(o\)-cresol just because it is ortho-substituted.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – General Organic Chemistry / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Hydrogen bonding in organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Intramolecular vs intermolecular hydrogen bonding in phenols

    Final exam-style answer:

    Intramolecular hydrogen bonding is present in catechol because it is \(o\)-dihydroxybenzene and the two adjacent \(\mathrm{-OH}\) groups can form hydrogen bonding within the same molecule.

    Therefore, the correct answer is \(\boxed{(b)\ \text{Catechol}}\).

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Intramolecular H-bond: within the same molecule
    • Intermolecular H-bond: between different molecules
    • Best clue: ortho-positioned donor and acceptor groups
    • Catechol: \(o\)-dihydroxybenzene \(\Rightarrow\) intramolecular H-bonding
    • Resorcinol: \(m\)-dihydroxybenzene \(\Rightarrow\) not intramolecular
    • Quinol: \(p\)-dihydroxybenzene \(\Rightarrow\) not intramolecular
    • \(o\)-cresol: only one \(\mathrm{-OH}\), so not intramolecular

    Answer: \((c)\) Ethanal, propanone


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We first identify the starting carbonyl compound and then follow the reaction sequence step by step.

    1. Identify the starting ketone

      The given ketone is \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\), that is butan-2-one.

    2. Action of Grignard reagent \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\)

      Grignard reagents add an alkyl group to the carbonyl carbon.

      So, \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) adds a \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) group to butan-2-one, and after hydrolysis \(\mathrm{(H_3O^+)}\), a tertiary alcohol is formed.

      Thus, \(A\) is:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

      This is a tertiary alcohol.

    3. Dehydration of the alcohol

      On heating with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), the alcohol undergoes elimination to form an alkene.

      By Saytzeff rule, the more substituted alkene is the major product.

      Therefore, \(B\) is:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      This is the major alkene formed.

    4. Ozonolysis of the alkene

      Ozonolysis with \(\mathrm{O_3}\), followed by reductive workup \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\), cleaves the double bond and converts each doubly bonded carbon into a carbonyl compound.

      For:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      the cleavage gives:

      • Left carbon \(\mathrm{(CH_3CH=)}\) has one H, so it gives an aldehyde:

        \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) = ethanal

      • Right carbon \(\mathrm{(=C(CH_3)_2)}\) has two \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) groups, so it gives a ketone:

        \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\) = propanone

      Hence:

      \(C = \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) and \(D = \mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Reaction flow:



    Compact reaction summary:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow[\ (ii)\ H_3O^+]{\ (i)\ CH_3MgBr\ } CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

    \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow[\ 358\,K]{20\%\ H_3PO_4} CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow[\ Zn/H_2O]{O_3} CH_3CHO + (CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Grignard reagent \(\mathrm{RMgX}\) behaves like \(\mathrm{R^-}\) and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
    • Ketone \(+\) Grignard reagent \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
    • Acidic dehydration of alcohol usually gives the more substituted alkene as the major product.
    • Ozonolysis breaks the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond into carbonyl fragments.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Grignard reagent: \(\mathrm{RMgX}\) adds to aldehydes and ketones.
    • With ketone:

      \(\mathrm{R_2CO + R'MgX \xrightarrow{ether} R_2C(OMgX)R' \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} R_2C(OH)R'}\)

    • Alcohol dehydration: generally gives alkene; major product follows Saytzeff rule.
    • Ozonolysis: cleaves \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond.
    • Carbon of double bond having at least one H usually gives an aldehyde on reductive ozonolysis.
    • Carbon of double bond attached to two carbon groups gives a ketone.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First identify the starting ketone as butan-2-one: \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\).
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) adds one \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) to the carbonyl carbon \(\Rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol.
    • Dehydration of that alcohol gives the most substituted alkene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    • Now split the double bond directly in ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=} \Rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\)

      \(\mathrm{=C(CH_3)_2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    • So the answer is immediately:

      \((c)\) Ethanal, propanone

    Very fast carbonyl-fragment trick:

    • In ozonolysis, mentally cut the double bond:
    • CH3CH = C(CH3)2
  |         |
CHO      C=O
    • The left alkene carbon has one H \(\Rightarrow\) aldehyde.
    • The right alkene carbon has two alkyl groups \(\Rightarrow\) ketone.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that ketone \(+\) Grignard reagent gives a tertiary alcohol, not a secondary alcohol.
    2. Missing the major alkene during dehydration.
    3. Confusing oxidative ozonolysis with reductive ozonolysis.
    4. Writing ethanoic acid instead of ethanal. Here \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\) gives reductive ozonolysis, so aldehyde is obtained.
    5. Not checking whether the alkene carbon bears hydrogen before deciding aldehyde vs ketone.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carbonyl compounds and ozonolysis of alkenes
    • Subtopic: Grignard addition to ketones, dehydration of alcohols, reductive ozonolysis

    Final exam-style answer:

    The starting compound is butan-2-one, \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\).

    On reaction with \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\), it forms the tertiary alcohol:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

    On dehydration with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), the major alkene formed is:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    Reductive ozonolysis of this alkene gives:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) and \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Therefore, \(C =\) ethanal and \(D =\) propanone.

    \(\boxed{(c)\ \text{Ethanal, propanone}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Ketone \(+\) Grignard:

      \(\mathrm{R_2CO \rightarrow R_3COH}\)

    • Dehydration of alcohol:

      more substituted alkene is major

    • Reductive ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{C=C \rightarrow}\) aldehydes / ketones

    • Alkene carbon with H:

      gives aldehyde

    • Alkene carbon with two alkyl groups:

      gives ketone

    • Here:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2 \rightarrow CH_3CHO + (CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. We must find the incorrect reagent match

      The question asks which reagent cannot distinguish the given pair of compounds.

    2. Check option (a)

      \(CH_3OH\) and \(CH_3CH_2OH\) with \(I_2 + NaOH\)

      This is the iodoform test. Ethanol gives iodoform test, but methanol does not.

      • \(CH_3CH_2OH \rightarrow CH_3CHO\) on oxidation
      • Compounds containing or forming the group \(CH_3CO-\) or \(CH_3CH(OH)-\) give iodoform test
      • So this reagent can distinguish them
    3. Check option (b)

      \(CH_3CH_2OH\) and \((CH_3)_3COH\) with anhydrous \(ZnCl_2 +\) conc. \(HCl\)

      This is Lucas reagent, used to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols.

      • Primary alcohol: very slow / no turbidity at room temperature
      • Tertiary alcohol: immediate turbidity
      • Therefore ethanol and tert-butyl alcohol are easily distinguished
    4. Check option (c)

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}CH\) and \(CH_3-C{\equiv}C-CH_3\) with \(Na\)

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}CH\) is a terminal alkyne. It has an acidic hydrogen attached to the triple-bonded carbon.

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}C-CH_3\) is an internal alkyne. It does not have such acidic hydrogen.

      • Terminal alkynes react with active metals like \(Na\) due to acidic hydrogen
      • Internal alkynes do not show this behavior
      • So this reagent can distinguish them
    5. Check option (d)

      \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\) with \(2,4\)-dinitrophenylhydrazine

      \(2,4\)-DNP reagent tests for the presence of the carbonyl group, \(C{=}O\).

      • Aldehydes give the test
      • Ketones also give the test
      • Since both \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\) contain carbonyl group, both respond positively
      • Hence this reagent cannot distinguish these two compounds

      Therefore, this is the incorrect match.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Core theory to remember:

    • Iodoform test: Given by compounds containing \(CH_3CO-\) or \(CH_3CH(OH)-\), and also by ethanol because it gets oxidized to ethanal.
    • Lucas reagent: anhydrous \(ZnCl_2 +\) conc. \(HCl\); used to distinguish alcohol classes by rate of turbidity formation.
    • Terminal alkynes: contain acidic hydrogen and react with strong metals / suitable reagents, unlike internal alkynes.
    • \(2,4\)-DNP test: confirms presence of aldehyde or ketone carbonyl group, but does not distinguish aldehyde from ketone.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Questions on qualitative organic analysis are often solved by remembering what functional group the reagent detects.
    • If a reagent gives positive test for both compounds, it cannot be used for distinction.
    • \(2,4\)-DNP is a test for carbonyl compounds in general, not specifically aldehydes only.
    • To distinguish aldehydes and ketones, reagents like Tollens' reagent or Fehling's solution are usually more useful.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately spot option (d): aldehyde + ketone with \(2,4\)-DNP.
    • Ask: “Does \(2,4\)-DNP detect only one of them?” No, it detects both.
    • So without checking the full details, you can often mark \(\boxed{(d)}\) quickly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking \(2,4\)-DNP distinguishes aldehydes and ketones. It does not; it only confirms carbonyl presence.
    2. Forgetting that ethanol gives iodoform test.
    3. Forgetting that terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogen but internal alkynes do not.
    4. Mixing up Lucas reagent behavior of primary and tertiary alcohols.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Qualitative analysis of organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Distinguishing functional groups using named reagents and characteristic tests

    Quick reagent cheatsheet:

    • Iodoform test:

      \(CH_3CO-\), \(CH_3CH(OH)-\), ethanol

    • Lucas reagent:

      distinguishes \(1^\circ\), \(2^\circ\), \(3^\circ\) alcohols

    • Terminal alkyne test idea:

      presence of acidic hydrogen on \(sp\)-hybridized carbon

    • \(2,4\)-DNP:

      aldehydes + ketones \(\rightarrow\) positive test

    • Tollens' / Fehling's:

      generally useful to distinguish aldehydes from ketones

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(2,4\)-dinitrophenylhydrazine reacts with the carbonyl group \((C{=}O)\), which is present in both \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\). Therefore, it cannot distinguish the given pair of compounds.

    Hence, the incorrect match is \(\boxed{(d)}\).

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    The starting compound is anisole, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3}\). We have to find the final product \(Z\).

    1. Reaction of anisole with \(\mathrm{HI}\)

      Anisole is an aryl-alkyl ether. On heating with \(\mathrm{HI}\), ether cleavage occurs at the alkyl-oxygen bond, not at the aryl-oxygen bond.

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

      So:

      • \(X = \mathrm{C_6H_5OH}\) (phenol)
      • \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\) (methyl iodide)
    2. Why cleavage happens at \(\mathrm{O-CH_3}\) and not at \(\mathrm{O-C_6H_5}\)

      The \(\mathrm{C_6H_5-O}\) bond in anisole has partial double-bond character due to resonance, so it is difficult to break.

      Also, nucleophilic substitution at an \(\mathrm{sp^2}\)-hybridised aryl carbon does not occur easily.

      Hence \(\mathrm{I^-}\) attacks the methyl group and forms \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\).

    3. Reaction of \(X\) with Zn / \(\Delta\)

      Phenol on distillation with zinc dust gives benzene:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OH \xrightarrow{Zn,\ \Delta} C_6H_6}\)

      This matches the reaction shown, so our identification of \(X = \mathrm{phenol}\) is confirmed.

    4. Reaction of \(Y\) with benzene in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\)

      Since \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\), it undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation with benzene:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3I \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5CH_3 + HI}\)

      The product formed is toluene.

    Therefore, \(Z\) is:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    Hence, the correct option is \((d)\).

    Why this method works:

    • In aryl-alkyl ethers, cleavage with \(\mathrm{HI}\) or \(\mathrm{HBr}\) occurs at the alkyl side.
    • Phenol formation from anisole is a standard NCERT reaction.
    • Methyl iodide formed in the first step is an excellent substrate for Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
    • The Zn-dust step is a useful confirmation because \(\mathrm{phenol \rightarrow benzene}\) is a classic conversion.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Cleavage of ethers with \(\mathrm{HI}\):

      \(\mathrm{R-O-R' + HI \rightarrow R-OH + R'-I}\)

    • In anisole:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

    • Phenol with Zn dust:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OH \xrightarrow{Zn} C_6H_6}\)

    • Friedel-Crafts alkylation: alkyl halide + benzene \(\rightarrow\) alkylbenzene
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\) with benzene gives toluene.

    Reaction flow:

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately remember:

      \(\mathrm{anisole + HI \rightarrow phenol + CH_3I}\)

    • Then use the clue:

      \(\mathrm{phenol \xrightarrow{Zn} benzene}\)

    • So \(X\) must be phenol and \(Y\) must be methyl iodide.
    • Then:

      \(\mathrm{benzene + CH_3I / AlCl_3 \rightarrow toluene}\)

    • So choose the structure with a \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) group on benzene: option \((d)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking anisole gives phenyl iodide with \(\mathrm{HI}\).

      The aryl-oxygen bond is not cleaved easily.

    2. Confusing \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\) with \(\mathrm{C_2H_5I}\), leading to ethylbenzene instead of toluene.
    3. Forgetting the standard reaction:

      \(\mathrm{phenol \xrightarrow{Zn} benzene}\)

    4. Choosing benzyl chloride instead of toluene in Friedel-Crafts step.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Reactions of ethers and phenols
    • Subtopic: Cleavage of anisole by \(\mathrm{HI}\), phenol to benzene conversion, and Friedel-Crafts alkylation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Anisole reacts with \(\mathrm{HI}\) to give phenol and methyl iodide:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

    Thus \(X = \mathrm{phenol}\) and \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\).

    Since phenol on heating with zinc dust gives benzene, this confirms \(X\).

    Then methyl iodide reacts with benzene in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) to form toluene:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3I \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    Therefore \(Z = \mathrm{toluene}\).

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Anisole + \(\mathrm{HI}\):

      \(\mathrm{phenol + CH_3I}\)

    • Phenol + Zn dust:

      \(\mathrm{benzene}\)

    • \(\mathrm{CH_3I + benzene / AlCl_3}\):

      \(\mathrm{toluene}\)

    • Key bond cleavage rule: cleavage occurs at alkyl-O bond in anisole
    • Final product \(Z\):

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Clemmensen reduction converts the carbonyl group of an aldehyde or ketone into a methylene group in acidic medium.

      Reagent used:

      \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\)

      General transformation:

      \(\mathrm{R{-}CO{-}R' \xrightarrow[\text{conc. }HCl]{Zn{-}Hg} R{-}CH_2{-}R'}\)

    2. In option \((d)\), the ketone:

      \(\mathrm{R{-}CO{-}CH_3}\)

      is reduced to:

      \(\mathrm{R{-}CH_2{-}CH_3}\)

      using \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\), which is exactly the Clemmensen reduction.

      • Option \((a)\) is not Clemmensen reduction

        \(\mathrm{R{-}COCl + H_2 \xrightarrow{Pd/BaSO_4} R{-}CHO + HCl}\)

        This is Rosenmund reduction, where an acid chloride is reduced to an aldehyde.

      • Option \((b)\) is not Clemmensen reduction

        \(\mathrm{R{-}CHO \xrightarrow[(ii)\ KOH,\ ethylene\ glycol]{(i)\ NH_2NH_2} R{-}CH_3}\)

        This is Wolff–Kishner reduction, which also converts carbonyl to methylene, but it occurs in basic medium, not acidic medium.

      • Option \((c)\) is not Clemmensen reduction

        \(\mathrm{R{-}COOR' \xrightarrow[\ 2.\ H_2O\ ]{1.\ DIBAL{-}H} R{-}CHO}\)

        DIBAL-H reduces esters to aldehydes under controlled conditions.



    4. This reaction pattern is the key visual clue: \(C=O\) becomes \(\mathrm{-CH_2-}\) in the presence of \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\).

    Why \((d)\) is correct:
