    • Clemmensen reduction uses \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\).
    • It reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons.
    • So \(\mathrm{R{-}CO{-}CH_3 \rightarrow R{-}CH_2{-}CH_3}\) is the correct identification.

    JEE / NCERT theory takeaway:

    • Carbonyl compounds can be reduced in different ways depending on reagent:
      • \(\mathrm{NaBH_4/LiAlH_4}\): carbonyl to alcohol
      • \(\mathrm{Zn{-}Hg/HCl}\): carbonyl to methylene (Clemmensen)
      • \(\mathrm{NH_2NH_2/KOH,\ heat}\): carbonyl to methylene (Wolff–Kishner)
    • Clemmensen reduction is preferred when the compound can tolerate strongly acidic medium.
    • Wolff–Kishner reduction is preferred when the compound can tolerate strongly basic medium.
    • Both Clemmensen and Wolff–Kishner achieve the same net result for aldehydes/ketones:

      \(\mathrm{C=O \rightarrow CH_2}\)

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately scan for the reagent \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\).
    • Match it with the shortcut:

      \(\mathrm{C=O \rightarrow CH_2}\)

    • If reagent is \(\mathrm{NH_2NH_2/KOH}\), think Wolff–Kishner, not Clemmensen.
    • If reagent is \(\mathrm{Pd/BaSO_4}\), think Rosenmund.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Clemmensen reduction with Wolff–Kishner reduction.
    2. Remembering only the product change but forgetting the reaction medium.
    3. Assuming every carbonyl-to-hydrocarbon conversion is Clemmensen reduction.
    4. Missing that option \((a)\) starts from an acid chloride, not an aldehyde or ketone.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reduction reactions of carbonyl compounds
    • Subtopic: Clemmensen reduction and comparison with Wolff–Kishner reduction

    Final exam-style answer:

    Clemmensen reduction is the reduction of aldehydes or ketones to hydrocarbons using \(\mathrm{Zn\!-\!Hg/HCl}\).

    Option \((d)\):

    \(\mathrm{R{-}CO{-}CH_3 \xrightarrow{Zn{-}Hg/HCl} R{-}CH_2{-}CH_3}\)

    matches this reaction exactly.

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Clemmensen: \(\mathrm{Zn{-}Hg/HCl}\), acidic, \(\mathrm{C=O \rightarrow CH_2}\)
    • Wolff–Kishner: \(\mathrm{NH_2NH_2/KOH}\), basic, \(\mathrm{C=O \rightarrow CH_2}\)
    • Rosenmund: \(\mathrm{RCOCl \rightarrow RCHO}\)
    • DIBAL-H: controlled reduction of ester/nitrile to aldehyde

    Answer: \((b)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction is shown by carboxylic acids having at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

    In this reaction, the \(\alpha\)-hydrogen of a carboxylic acid is replaced by halogen in the presence of halogen and red phosphorus.

    General reaction:

    \(\mathrm{R-CH_2-COOH \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_2O]{(i)\ X_2/red\ P} R-CHX-COOH}\)

    where \(X = \mathrm{Cl}\) or \(\mathrm{Br}\).

    1. Key condition for HVZ reaction
      • The compound must be a carboxylic acid.
      • It must contain at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
      • The \(\alpha\)-carbon is the carbon directly next to the \(\mathrm{-COOH}\) group.
    2. Check each option
      • Option \((a)\): benzoic acid, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COOH}\)

        Here, the carbon next to \(\mathrm{-COOH}\) is part of the benzene ring system. There is no suitable \(\alpha\)-hydrogen for HVZ reaction.

        So, it does not undergo HVZ reaction.

      • Option \((b)\): \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COOH}\)

        This is a carboxylic acid and the \(\alpha\)-carbon is the \(\mathrm{CH_2}\) group next to \(\mathrm{-COOH}\).

        That \(\mathrm{CH_2}\) has \(\alpha\)-hydrogens, so it can undergo HVZ reaction.

        Reaction:

        \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COOH \xrightarrow[(ii)\ H_2O]{(i)\ X_2/red\ P} C_6H_5CHXCOOH}\)

      • Option \((c)\): \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CHO}\)

        This is an aldehyde, not a carboxylic acid.

        So, it does not undergo Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.

      • Option \((d)\): \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COCH_3}\)

        This is a ketone, not a carboxylic acid.

        So, it does not undergo Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.

    3. Therefore

      Only option \((b)\) is a carboxylic acid having \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

      Hence, the correct answer is:

      \(\boxed{(b)}\)



    Why this works:

    • HVZ reaction specifically belongs to \(\alpha\)-halogenation of carboxylic acids.
    • The acid must first be able to form the required reactive intermediate, which is possible only when an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen is present.
    • That is why simple aromatic acid like benzoic acid does not respond, but phenylacetic acid does.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction is used for halogenation at the \(\alpha\)-position of carboxylic acids.
    • Reagents used:

      \(\mathrm{X_2/red\ P}\), followed by \(\mathrm{H_2O}\)

    • Only carboxylic acids with \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo this reaction.
    • General change:

      \(\mathrm{-CH_2COOH \rightarrow -CHXCOOH}\)

    • Benzoic acid does not undergo HVZ because it has no suitable \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First ask: Is it a carboxylic acid?
    • If no, reject immediately.
    • If yes, then check: Is there an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen on the carbon next to \(\mathrm{-COOH}\)?
    • Only the structure \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COOH}\) passes both tests.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Choosing any compound with a \(\mathrm{CH_2}\) group without checking whether it is a carboxylic acid.
    2. Confusing benzoic acid with phenylacetic acid.
    3. Forgetting that HVZ is not a general halogenation of all carbonyl compounds.
    4. Checking only for hydrogen and forgetting that it must be specifically an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carboxylic acids
    • Subtopic: Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction and \(\alpha\)-halogenation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction is given by carboxylic acids containing \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

    Among the given compounds, only \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2COOH}\) has a carboxylic acid group and \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.

    Therefore, it undergoes HVZ reaction.

    \(\boxed{(b)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • HVZ reaction: \(\alpha\)-halogenation of carboxylic acids
    • Required condition: presence of \(\alpha\)-hydrogen
    • Reagents: \(\mathrm{Cl_2}\) or \(\mathrm{Br_2}\), red \(P\), then \(\mathrm{H_2O}\)
    • General reaction: \(\mathrm{RCH_2COOH \rightarrow RCHXCOOH}\)
    • Not shown by: acids without \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, aldehydes, ketones

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    Smaller the \(pK_a\) value, stronger is the acid.

    So we must identify the strongest acid among the given compounds.

    1. Basic idea: lower \(pK_a\) means greater acidity

      Acid strength depends on the stability of the conjugate base formed after loss of \(\mathrm{H^+}\).

      More stable conjugate base \(\Rightarrow\) stronger acid \(\Rightarrow\) lower \(pK_a\).

    2. Compare the four options
      • Option \((a)\): \(p\)-nitrophenol

        Phenols are acidic, but they are generally much weaker acids than benzoic acid derivatives.

      • Option \((b)\): benzoic acid

        Benzoic acid is a carboxylic acid, so it is much stronger than phenol.

      • Option \((c)\): \(p\)-methoxy benzoic acid

        The \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) group is an electron-donating group by resonance.

        Electron-donating groups decrease acidity of benzoic acid because they destabilize the carboxylate ion.

        So this is less acidic than benzoic acid.

      • Option \((d)\): \(p\)-nitro benzoic acid

        The \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) group is a strong electron-withdrawing group.

        It stabilizes the carboxylate ion by \(-I\) effect and also helps withdraw electron density from the ring.

        Hence this compound is the most acidic among the given options.

    3. Therefore

      Since option \((d)\) is the strongest acid, it will have the lowest \(pK_a\) value.

      \(\boxed{(d)}\)



    Why this method works:

    • Acidity increases when the conjugate base becomes more stable.
    • In aromatic carboxylic acids, electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity.
    • Electron-donating groups decrease acidity.
    • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) is strongly electron-withdrawing, while \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) is electron-donating by resonance.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Carboxylic acids are stronger acids than phenols.
    • Electron-withdrawing substituents increase acidity of carboxylic acids.
    • Electron-donating substituents decrease acidity of carboxylic acids.
    • For substituted benzoic acids:

      \(\mathrm{EWG} \Rightarrow\) acidity increases \(\Rightarrow pK_a\) decreases

      \(\mathrm{EDG} \Rightarrow\) acidity decreases \(\Rightarrow pK_a\) increases

    Helpful comparison here:

    • \(p\)-nitro benzoic acid \(>\) benzoic acid \(>\) \(p\)-methoxy benzoic acid in acidity
    • \(p\)-nitrophenol is acidic, but still weaker than these benzoic acid derivatives

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First separate phenol from carboxylic acids.
    • Carboxylic acids are usually much stronger acids than phenols.
    • Among benzoic acid derivatives, look for the strongest electron-withdrawing group.
    • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) immediately signals stronger acid and hence lower \(pK_a\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that lower \(pK_a\) means stronger acid.
    2. Choosing phenol derivative just because it also has \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\).
    3. Ignoring the fact that \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\) donates electrons and reduces acidity.
    4. Comparing substituent effects without first identifying the main functional group.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Acidic strength of carboxylic acids and phenols
    • Subtopic: Effect of substituents on acidity and \(pK_a\)

    Final exam-style answer:

    Smaller the \(pK_a\) value, stronger is the acid.

    Among the given compounds, \(p\)-nitro benzoic acid is the strongest acid because the \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) group is strongly electron-withdrawing and stabilizes the conjugate base.

    Hence it has the lowest \(pK_a\) value.

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Lower \(pK_a\): stronger acid
    • Carboxylic acid vs phenol: carboxylic acid is stronger
    • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\): electron-withdrawing, increases acidity
    • \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\): electron-donating, decreases acidity
    • Key shortcut: among benzoic acids, stronger EWG \(\Rightarrow\) lower \(pK_a\)
    • Correct answer: (c) \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\)
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Cumene on oxidation in air forms cumene hydroperoxide. On acidic cleavage, this gives phenol and acetone. Since phenol reacts with sodium metal but acetone does not, \(Y\) must be phenol and \(Z\) must be acetone \((\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3})\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • This is the standard cumene process.
      • First, cumene undergoes oxidation in air to form cumene hydroperoxide:
        • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(CH_3)_2 + O_2 \rightarrow C_6H_5C(CH_3)_2OOH}\)
      • That compound \(X\) on treatment with dilute acid breaks into:
        • phenol
        • acetone
      • So the products are:
        • \(Y=\) phenol
        • \(Z=\) acetone
      • The clue given is:
        • \(Y\) reacts with sodium metal
        • \(Z\) does not react with sodium metal
      • Phenol reacts with sodium metal because it has acidic hydrogen on \(\mathrm{-OH}\):
        • \(\mathrm{2C_6H_5OH + 2Na \rightarrow 2C_6H_5ONa + H_2}\)
      • Acetone does not react with sodium metal in this way.
      • Therefore \(Z\) is acetone:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Cumene process is an industrial method for preparing phenol.
      • The sequence is:
        • cumene \(\rightarrow\) cumene hydroperoxide \(\rightarrow\) phenol + acetone
      • The question also uses a functional-group clue:
        • phenol reacts with sodium metal due to the presence of \(\mathrm{O-H}\) hydrogen
        • ketones like acetone do not show that reaction
      • So this is both a named-process and reactivity-based question.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • The acidic cleavage product pair is phenol and acetone.
      • Among the options, acetone is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\)
      • That is option (c).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\): This is acetaldehyde, not formed in the cumene process.
      • (b) \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_3}\): This is butan-2-one, not the product here.
      • (d) \(\mathrm{CH_3COOCH_3}\): This is methyl acetate, an ester, not formed in this reaction.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see:
        • cumene
        • oxidation in air
        • dilute acid cleavage
        immediately recall:
        • phenol + acetone
      • If the question says one product reacts with sodium metal, that identifies phenol. The other product must then be acetone.
    • Final result: \(Z\) is acetone, \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Preparation of phenol / Cumene process
    • Correct answer: (c) Benzoyl chloride \(\xrightarrow{\mathrm{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4}}\) benzaldehyde.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene reacts with CO and HCl in the presence of anhydrous AlCl\(_3\) by the Gattermann–Koch formylation reaction to give benzaldehyde \((C_6H_5CHO)\). So we must identify the option that also gives benzaldehyde.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • In the given reaction, benzene is treated with CO and HCl in the presence of anhydrous AlCl\(_3\).
      • This is the Gattermann–Koch reaction, whose main purpose is to introduce a formyl group \((\mathrm{-CHO})\) on benzene.
      • Therefore, compound X is benzaldehyde, that is \(C_6H_5CHO\).
      • Now check which option also forms \(C_6H_5CHO\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Gattermann–Koch formylation: \(C_6H_6 \xrightarrow[\mathrm{AlCl_3/CuCl}]{\mathrm{CO + HCl}} C_6H_5CHO\)
      • Rosenmund reduction: Acid chlorides on catalytic hydrogenation with poisoned catalyst \((\mathrm{Pd/BaSO_4})\) get reduced to aldehydes, not all the way to alcohols.
      • This is a standard named-reaction pattern: acyl chloride \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) starts with benzoyl chloride \((C_6H_5COCl)\).
      • On treatment with \(\mathrm{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4}\), it undergoes Rosenmund reduction.
      • The \(\mathrm{-COCl}\) group is reduced to \(\mathrm{-CHO}\).
      • So, \(C_6H_5COCl \rightarrow C_6H_5CHO\), which is exactly benzaldehyde.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) Nitriles with \(LiAlH_4\) get reduced to primary amines, so benzonitrile gives benzylamine, not benzaldehyde.
      • (b) Toluene with alkaline \(KMnO_4\) on heating gets oxidized strongly to benzoic acid \((C_6H_5COOH)\), not benzaldehyde.
      • (d) Benzyl alcohol with \(\mathrm{CrO_3/H_2SO_4}\) is oxidized under strong conditions to benzoic acid, not stopped at aldehyde stage.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorize this direct pattern: \(\mathrm{RCOCl \xrightarrow{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4} RCHO}\) = Rosenmund reduction.
      • If the product in the question is an aldehyde, quickly look for an acid chloride + Rosenmund option.
      • Also remember: strong oxidation of alkyl benzenes usually goes to carboxylic acid, not aldehyde.
    • Final result
      • Compound X is benzaldehyde.
      • Benzaldehyde is also obtained from benzoyl chloride by Rosenmund reduction.
      • Hence, option (c) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Preparation of aldehydes; named reactions (Gattermann–Koch, Rosenmund reduction)
    • Correct answer: (b) 2-acetoxybenzoic acid.
      Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Aniline first gets converted into phenol through diazotization followed by hydrolysis. Phenol then undergoes Kolbe reaction to give salicylic acid \((o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid), and finally acetylation with acetic anhydride converts the phenolic \(\mathrm{-OH}\) into \(\mathrm{-OCOCH_3}\), giving 2-acetoxybenzoic acid.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Step 1: Aniline reacts with \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at \(273\text{–}278\,K\) to form benzene diazonium chloride.
      • On warming with water, the diazonium salt is hydrolyzed to phenol. So \(X =\) phenol.
      • Step 2: Phenol with \(NaOH\) first forms sodium phenoxide.
      • Reaction with \(CO_2\) followed by acidification \((H^+)\) gives salicylic acid \((o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid). So \(Y =\) salicylic acid.
      • Step 3: Salicylic acid reacts with acetic anhydride \((CH_3CO)_2O\).
      • The phenolic \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group gets acetylated to form \(\mathrm{-OCOCH_3}\).
      • Thus \(Z =\) 2-acetoxybenzoic acid, which is option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Diazotization: Primary aromatic amines form diazonium salts with \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at low temperature.
      • Hydrolysis of diazonium salt: Aromatic diazonium salts on warming with water give phenols.
      • Kolbe–Schmitt reaction: Sodium phenoxide reacts with \(CO_2\), and after acidification gives mainly \(o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid.
      • Acetylation: Phenolic \(\mathrm{-OH}\) reacts with acetic anhydride to give acetate ester.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) shows a benzene ring with both \(\mathrm{-COOH}\) and \(\mathrm{-OCOCH_3}\) groups in ortho positions.
      • This exactly matches the product formed by acetylation of salicylic acid.
      • This compound is commonly known as acetylsalicylic acid.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) has \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-OCOCH_3}\), but no \(\mathrm{-COOH}\) group, so it cannot come from Kolbe reaction followed by acetylation.
      • (c) contains \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\), which would require methylation, not acetylation.
      • (d) contains \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\), which is an acyl group directly attached to the ring, not the ester group formed here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see aniline \(\rightarrow\) diazotization \(\rightarrow\) warm water, think phenol.
      • Whenever phenol is followed by \(NaOH\), \(CO_2\), \(H^+\), think salicylic acid (Kolbe reaction).
      • Acetic anhydride usually acetylates \(\mathrm{-OH}\) or \(\mathrm{-NH_2}\), so the final group becomes \(\mathrm{-OCOCH_3}\).
    • Final result
      • \(X =\) phenol
      • \(Y =\) salicylic acid
      • \(Z =\) 2-acetoxybenzoic acid
      • Hence, option (b) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Amines and Phenols
      • Subtopic: Diazotization, hydrolysis of diazonium salts, Kolbe–Schmitt reaction, acetylation of phenols
    • Correct answer: (a) \(3:1\).
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Benzenesulphonic acid on fusion with NaOH followed by acidification gives phenol, and oxidation of phenol with \(Na_2Cr_2O_7/H_2SO_4\) gives \(p\)-benzoquinone. In this compound Q, the total number of \(\sigma\) bonds is 12 and the number of \(\pi\) bonds is 4, so the ratio is \(12:4 = 3:1\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is benzenesulphonic acid.
      • On treatment with \(NaOH\) and then \(H^+\), the \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\) group is replaced by \(\mathrm{-OH}\), giving phenol. So \(P =\) phenol.
      • Phenol on oxidation with \(Na_2Cr_2O_7/H_2SO_4\) gives \(p\)-benzoquinone. So \(Q =\) \(p\)-benzoquinone.
      • Now we count \(\sigma\) and \(\pi\) bonds in \(p\)-benzoquinone.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Conversion of benzenesulphonic acid to phenol: This is a standard aromatic substitution sequence used in preparation of phenol.
      • Oxidation of phenol: Strong oxidizing agents can convert phenol into quinones.
      • \(\sigma\) and \(\pi\) bond counting rule: Every single bond is one \(\sigma\) bond. Every double bond contains one \(\sigma\) and one \(\pi\) bond.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Structure of \(Q\) is \(p\)-benzoquinone.
      • It contains two \(C=O\) double bonds and two \(C=C\) double bonds, so total \(\pi\) bonds \(= 4\).
      • For \(\sigma\) bonds:
        • 6 bonds around the six-membered ring contribute 6 \(\sigma\) bonds.
        • 2 \(C=O\) double bonds contribute 2 \(\sigma\) bonds.
        • 4 \(C-H\) bonds contribute 4 \(\sigma\) bonds.
      • So total \(\sigma\) bonds \(= 6 + 2 + 4 = 12\).
      • Hence ratio \(= 12:4 = 3:1\).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (b) \(1:3\) would mean \(\pi\) bonds exceed \(\sigma\) bonds, which is impossible here.
      • (c) \(4:1\) would require 16 \(\sigma\) bonds for 4 \(\pi\) bonds, which is not the count for quinone.
      • (d) \(2:1\) would require 8 \(\sigma\) bonds for 4 \(\pi\) bonds, again incorrect.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Do not count only the visible single bonds; remember each double bond also contributes one \(\sigma\) bond.
      • For cyclic conjugated molecules, first identify the exact product structure, then count ring bonds, double bonds, and \(C-H\) bonds separately.
    • Final result
      • \(Q = p\)-benzoquinone
      • \(\sigma\) bonds \(= 12\)
      • \(\pi\) bonds \(= 4\)
      • Ratio of \(\sigma : \pi = 3:1\)
      • Hence, option (a) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Phenols
      • Subtopic: Preparation of phenol, oxidation to quinones, counting \(\sigma\) and \(\pi\) bonds
    • Correct answer: (c) X is 2-methyl-2-butanol and Y is 2-methyl-2-butene.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Oxidation of isopentane under the given condition gives the corresponding tertiary alcohol, 2-methyl-2-butanol. On dehydration with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), this alcohol gives the more substituted alkene as major product, namely 2-methyl-2-butene.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Start with isopentane, whose structure is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)
      • The carbon at position 2 is a tertiary-type center for oxidation/substitution context in this branched framework, and the product shown in the key is the tertiary alcohol:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)(OH)-CH_2-CH_3}\)
      • So:
        • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
      • Now treat this alcohol with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\).
      • This is a dehydration reaction, meaning water is removed and an alkene is formed.
      • From 2-methyl-2-butanol, possible alkenes can form by removing a \(\beta\)-hydrogen.
      • According to Saytzeff rule, the more substituted alkene is the major product.
      • Therefore the major alkene is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_3}\)
      • This is 2-methyl-2-butene.
      • So:
        • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This question combines oxidation/substitution at branched alkane center with dehydration of alcohol.
      • Tertiary alcohols on dehydration generally form alkenes through elimination.
      • Saytzeff rule says the major alkene is usually the more substituted, more stable alkene.
      • So once \(X\) is recognized as 2-methyl-2-butanol, the major product of dehydration is expected to be 2-methyl-2-butene.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) gives:
        • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
        • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
      • This exactly matches the reaction sequence shown in the solution key.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) gives the wrong alcohol and a less appropriate alkene product.
      • (b) has the correct alcohol but wrong alkene; dehydration major product is not 2-methyl-1-butene here.
      • (d) gives a primary alcohol, which does not match the given transformation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • When acid-catalyzed dehydration of an alcohol is asked, look for the more substituted alkene as major product.
      • So among 2-methyl-1-butene and 2-methyl-2-butene, the latter is more substituted and more stable.
      • That helps eliminate options quickly.
    • Final result
      • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
      • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
      • Hence, option (c) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Alcohols
      • Topic: Oxidation and dehydration reactions
      • Subtopic: Dehydration of alcohols and alkene major-product prediction
    • Correct answer: (b).
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Instant turbidity with Lucas reagent \((\)conc. \(HCl / ZnCl_2)\) shows that \(X\) is a tertiary alcohol. Among the given \(C_5H_{12}O\) isomers, the correct tertiary alcohol is \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butanol. Its isomer \(Y\), which on dehydration gives the corresponding alkene, is \(2\)-methyl-\(1\)-butanol. So the correct pair is option (b).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The phrase produces turbidity instantly with Lucas reagent is the main clue.
      • Lucas reagent is:
        • conc. \(HCl + ZnCl_2\)
      • Its test is used to distinguish alcohols.
      • General observation:
        • tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) turbidity immediately
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) turbidity in a few minutes
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) no turbidity at room temperature for a long time
      • So \(X\) must be a tertiary alcohol.
      • Among the given \(C_5H_{12}O\) structures, the tertiary alcohol is:
        • \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butanol
      • Now the question asks for an isomer \(Y\) of \(X\) that undergoes dehydration with conc. \(H_2SO_4\) at \(443\ K\).
      • The matching isomer shown in the correct option is:
        • \(2\)-methyl-\(1\)-butanol
      • On dehydration, it forms the alkene:
        • \(2\)-methyl-but-\(2\)-ene
      • Therefore the pair \((X, Y)\) is:
        • \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butanol,\ 2\)-methyl-\(1\)-butanol
      • This corresponds to option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is an alcohols question combining:
        • Lucas test
        • classification of alcohols
        • dehydration of alcohols
      • Lucas test depends on how easily the alcohol forms a carbocation-like intermediate during substitution.
      • Tertiary alcohols react fastest because the tertiary carbocation is most stable.
      • That is why tertiary alcohols show immediate turbidity.
      • Dehydration with conc. \(H_2SO_4\) at high temperature converts alcohols into alkenes.
      • In elimination reactions, the more substituted alkene is often favoured \((\)Saytzeff product\().\)
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) contains \(X\) as a tertiary alcohol, matching the Lucas test clue.
      • Its isomer \(Y\) is a suitable alcohol that dehydrates under the stated conditions.
      • So option (b) satisfies both conditions of the question.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Any option with \(X\) as a primary alcohol is wrong because primary alcohols do not give instant turbidity with Lucas reagent.
      • Any option with the wrong tertiary alcohol placement or an inconsistent isomer pair fails the test condition given in the question.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you read:
        • instant turbidity with Lucas reagent
      • immediately mark the alcohol as tertiary.
      • Then among isomers, identify the tertiary one first.
      • That alone eliminates most wrong options.
    • Final result
      • \(X\) and \(Y\) respectively are \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butanol and \(2\)-methyl-\(1\)-butanol.
      • Hence the correct option is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Lucas test and dehydration of alcohols
      • Subtopic: Identification of tertiary alcohol and elimination to alkene
    • Correct answer: (d) CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+; KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: \(X\) must be benzaldehyde because it gives 2,4-DNP test and also reduces ammoniacal silver nitrate solution (Tollen’s reagent). \(Y\) must be benzoic acid because it reacts with NaHCO3 giving CO2. So reagent \(A\) must convert toluene into benzaldehyde, and reagent \(B\) must oxidise toluene fully into benzoic acid.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The product \(X\) forms a 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone. This tells us \(X\) contains a carbonyl group, so it must be either an aldehyde or a ketone.
      • \(X\) also reduces ammoniacal silver nitrate solution. That is the Tollen’s test, which is given by aldehydes, not ordinary ketones.
      • So \(X\) is an aldehyde derived from toluene, namely benzaldehyde, \(C_6H_5CHO\).
      • Toluene is converted to benzaldehyde by controlled side-chain oxidation using chromyl reagent conditions, here represented by CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, followed by hydrolysis.
      • Now look at \(Y\): it dissolves in NaHCO3 with evolution of CO2. That is the characteristic reaction of a carboxylic acid.
      • Oxidation of toluene with strong oxidising agent KMnO4 in alkaline medium on heating, followed by acidification, converts the side chain completely to benzoic acid, \(C_6H_5COOH\).
      • Hence \(A\) is CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+ and \(B\) is KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • 2,4-DNP test identifies aldehydes and ketones because both have the \(>C=O\) group.
      • Tollen’s reagent distinguishes aldehydes from ketones. Aldehydes are easily oxidised to acids, so Ag+ is reduced to metallic silver.
      • NaHCO3 test distinguishes carboxylic acids from many other organic compounds because acids stronger than carbonic acid release CO2.
      • Alkyl side chains on benzene having benzylic hydrogen usually undergo complete oxidation with hot alkaline KMnO4 to give benzoic acid.
      • Controlled oxidation of toluene to benzaldehyde is a standard exam pattern. Full oxidation gives benzoic acid; controlled oxidation stops at aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Its first reagent set gives benzaldehyde, which fits both 2,4-DNP and Tollen’s test.
      • Its second reagent set gives benzoic acid, which reacts with NaHCO3 to evolve CO2.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Any option that uses KMnO4 for making \(X\) is wrong because hot permanganate does not stop at aldehyde; it usually oxidises the side chain fully to benzoic acid.
      • Any option that uses the benzaldehyde-forming reagent for \(Y\) is wrong because \(Y\) must be a carboxylic acid, not an aldehyde.
      • So the two reagent roles are not interchangeable here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • 2,4-DNP + Tollen’s together almost immediately means aldehyde.
      • NaHCO3 with CO2 almost immediately means carboxylic acid.
      • For alkyl benzene side chain oxidation, KMnO4 usually means benzoic acid, not aldehyde.
    • Final result:
      • \(X =\) benzaldehyde
      • \(Y =\) benzoic acid
      • \(A =\) CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+
      • \(B =\) KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons and Aldehydes, Ketones, Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzene, qualitative tests of aldehydes and carboxylic acids
    • Correct answer: (a) \(X =\) propanone and \(Y =\) ethanal.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: \(C_5H_{12}O\) on passing over Cu at 573 K must first give an alkene \(C_5H_{10}\). The alkene formed is 2-methylbut-2-ene, and on ozonolysis it splits into propanone and ethanal.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound has molecular formula \(C_5H_{12}O\).
      • On heating alcohol vapours with copper at 573 K:
        • primary alcohols usually give aldehydes,
        • secondary alcohols usually give ketones,
        • tertiary alcohols cannot be dehydrogenated, so they undergo dehydration to give alkenes.
      • Since the product given is \(C_5H_{10}\), it must be an alkene. So the alcohol is a tertiary alcohol.
      • The suitable tertiary alcohol with formula \(C_5H_{12}O\) is 2-methylbutan-2-ol.
      • On heating with Cu at 573 K, it gives 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • Now apply ozonolysis:
        • Ozone breaks the \(C=C\) double bond.
        • Each doubly bonded carbon becomes a carbonyl carbon.
      • For 2-methylbut-2-ene, the structure can be written as \(CH_3 - C(CH_3)=CH - CH_3\).
      • On cleavage of the double bond:
        • the carbon attached to two \(CH_3\) groups gives propanone, \(CH_3COCH_3\),
        • the other carbon attached to \(H\) and \(CH_3\) gives ethanal, \(CH_3CHO\).
      • Therefore, \(X =\) propanone and \(Y =\) ethanal.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Copper at 573 K is a standard reagent used in alcohol conversions.
      • Tertiary alcohols lack the necessary hydrogen on the carbon bearing OH for dehydrogenation, so they preferentially undergo elimination.
      • Ozonolysis is a very important method for locating the position of the double bond, because the alkene splits into smaller carbonyl compounds.
      • If a double-bond carbon has:
        • two alkyl groups, it gives a ketone,
        • one alkyl group and one H, it gives an aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The alkene produced is 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • Its ozonolysis products are exactly propanone + ethanal.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (b) gives products that do not arise from cleavage of 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • (c) would require both fragments to become aldehydes, which is not possible here because one double-bond carbon has no hydrogen.
      • (d) is wrong because ozonolysis with reductive workup \((Zn + H_2O)\) gives aldehydes/ketones, not carboxylic acids like methanoic acid.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In ozonolysis, first identify how many alkyl groups are attached to each double-bond carbon.
      • Two carbon groups \(\rightarrow\) ketone; one H present \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde.
      • Also remember: \(Zn + H_2O\) is reductive workup, so do not expect acids.
    • Final result:
      • \(X = CH_3COCH_3\) (propanone)
      • \(Y = CH_3CHO\) (ethanal)
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Alcohols and Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Behaviour of alcohols with Cu at 573 K, ozonolysis of alkenes
    • Correct answer: (c) H2\(|\)Pd - BaSO4; (CH3)2Cd; \(C_6H_5CH = CHCOC_6H_5\).
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: \(C_6H_5COCl\) must first be converted into benzaldehyde and acetophenone. These then undergo base-catalysed crossed aldol condensation to give the major product \(Z\), which is benzalacetophenone, \(C_6H_5CH = CHCOC_6H_5\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound in both lines is benzoyl chloride, \(C_6H_5COCl\).
      • For the upper conversion, benzoyl chloride is converted to benzaldehyde.
      • The reagent that converts an acid chloride into an aldehyde is Rosenmund reduction, that is H2\(|\)Pd - BaSO4.
      • So \(X =\) H2\(|\)Pd - BaSO4.
      • For the lower conversion, benzoyl chloride is converted to acetophenone, \(C_6H_5COCH_3\).
      • Acid chlorides react with dialkyl cadmium reagents to give ketones without further overreaction. Therefore \(Y =\) (CH3)2Cd.
      • Now benzaldehyde and acetophenone are treated with \(OH^-\) at 293 K.
      • Acetophenone has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it forms an enolate in base.
      • Benzaldehyde does not have \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it acts only as the electrophile.
      • This is the classic crossed aldol condensation / Claisen-Schmidt condensation.
      • The enolate of acetophenone attacks benzaldehyde, and after dehydration the major product formed is:
        • \(C_6H_5CH = CHCOC_6H_5\)
      • So \(Z =\) benzalacetophenone.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Rosenmund reduction is selective reduction of acid chlorides to aldehydes using poisoned palladium catalyst.
      • Dialkyl cadmium reagents are less reactive than Grignard reagents and stop at the ketone stage when reacting with acid chlorides.
      • Cross aldol condensation is easiest to predict when one carbonyl compound has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen and the other does not.
      • Benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, which avoids self-aldol and makes the crossed product the dominant one.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • \(X\) correctly gives benzaldehyde from benzoyl chloride.
      • \(Y\) correctly gives acetophenone from benzoyl chloride.
      • The product \(Z\) shown in option (c) is exactly the expected condensation product of benzaldehyde and acetophenone.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) uses H2\(|\)Pd without poisoned catalyst, which is not the standard selective method here for stopping at aldehyde.
      • (b) LiAlH4 would reduce acid chloride too far, not give the required aldehyde selectively, and CH3MgBr would not stop cleanly at ketone in the required way.
      • (d) has correct \(X\) but wrong \(Y\); CH3MgBr is too reactive and does not serve the required acid chloride to ketone conversion selectively.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Acid chloride \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde usually points to Rosenmund reduction.
      • Acid chloride \(\rightarrow\) ketone in named-reagent questions often points to dialkyl cadmium.
      • Benzaldehyde + methyl aryl ketone + base is a strong clue for Claisen-Schmidt condensation.
    • Final result:
      • \(X =\) H2\(|\)Pd - BaSO4
      • \(Y =\) (CH3)2Cd
      • \(Z = C_6H_5CH = CHCOC_6H_5\)
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
      • Subtopic: Rosenmund reduction, preparation of ketones from acid chlorides, crossed aldol condensation
    • Correct answer: (b) Na \(/\)Hg, C2H5OH; DIBAL - H, H2O.
      Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: From benzonitrile, product \(X\) dissolves in dilute HCl, so \(X\) must be a basic amine, namely benzylamine. Product \(Y\) reacts with 2,4-DNP, so \(Y\) must be a carbonyl compound, specifically benzaldehyde. Therefore \(A\) must reduce nitrile to amine, and \(B\) must partially reduce nitrile to aldehyde.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is benzonitrile, \(C_6H_5CN\).
      • First identify \(X\):
        • \(X\) dissolves in dilute HCl.
        • Compounds that dissolve in dilute HCl are usually basic nitrogen compounds, especially amines, because they form soluble ammonium salts.
        • So \(X\) must be benzylamine, \(C_6H_5CH_2NH_2\).
      • Now identify \(Y\):
        • \(Y\) reacts with 2,4-DNP.
        • That means \(Y\) must be an aldehyde or ketone.
        • From benzonitrile, the relevant partial reduction product is benzaldehyde, \(C_6H_5CHO\).
      • Therefore reagent \(B\) must be the one that converts nitrile to aldehyde. That reagent is DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis.
      • Reagent \(A\) must convert nitrile to primary amine. In this question, that is represented by Na/Hg in ethanol, which reduces benzonitrile to benzylamine.
      • So \(A =\) Na \(/\)Hg, C2H5OH and \(B =\) DIBAL-H, H2O.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Nitriles can undergo reduction in different ways depending on reagent.
      • Strong reduction gives primary amines.
      • Controlled partial reduction gives aldehydes.
      • DIBAL-H is a famous selective reducing agent used to stop nitrile reduction at the aldehyde stage after hydrolysis.
      • 2,4-DNP reagent confirms the presence of a carbonyl group.
      • Dilute HCl solubility is a useful practical clue for amines because amines form salts with acids.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Its first reagent set gives the amine \(X\), which dissolves in dilute HCl.
      • Its second reagent set gives benzaldehyde \(Y\), which reacts with 2,4-DNP.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) LiAlH4 can reduce nitriles to amines, but H2\(/\)Ni does not fit the required selective aldehyde formation for \(Y\).
      • (c) places DIBAL-H first for \(A\), but \(A\) must give an amine, not an aldehyde.
      • (d) uses H2\(/\)Ni for \(B\), which again does not match the needed aldehyde product that gives 2,4-DNP test.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Nitrile \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde is a very strong clue for DIBAL-H.
      • Dissolves in dilute HCl usually points to a basic amine.
      • 2,4-DNP always pushes you toward an aldehyde or ketone.
    • Final result:
      • \(X = C_6H_5CH_2NH_2\) (benzylamine)
      • \(Y = C_6H_5CHO\) (benzaldehyde)
      • \(A =\) Na \(/\)Hg, C2H5OH
      • \(B =\) DIBAL-H, H2O
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Amines and Carbonyl Compounds
      • Subtopic: Reduction of nitriles to amines and aldehydes, identification by chemical tests

    2024

    Answer: \((a)\ \mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\)

    So
    Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTRlution:

    1. First understand what hydrolysis gives

      A halogen compound \(\mathrm{C_4H_9Br}\) on hydrolysis gives an alcohol \(\mathrm{Y}\). So, in general:

      \(\mathrm{R-Br \xrightarrow{hydrolysis} R-OH}\)

      Now this alcohol \(\mathrm{Y}\) undergoes dehydration with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\).

      Dehydration of alcohol is most characteristic and easiest for tertiary alcohols, because they form the most stable carbocation.

    2. Check which alkyl bromide can give a tertiary alcohol on hydrolysis
      • \(\mathrm{(a)\ (CH_3)_3CBr}\) gives \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH}\), which is tert-butyl alcohol, a tertiary alcohol.
      • \(\mathrm{(b)\ (CH_3)_2CHCH_2Br}\) gives a primary alcohol.
      • \(\mathrm{(c)\ CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2Br}\) gives a primary alcohol.
      • \(\mathrm{(d)\ CH_3CH(Br)CH_2CH_3}\) gives a secondary alcohol.

      Since the question points toward easy dehydration under acidic conditions, the alcohol formed should be tertiary.

      Therefore \(X\) must be:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\)

    3. Reaction sequence

      Hydrolysis:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr \xrightarrow{H_2O/OH^-} (CH_3)_3COH}\)

      Dehydration:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH \xrightarrow[358\ K]{20\%\ H_3PO_4} (CH_3)_2C{=}CH_2 + H_2O}\)

      So the alcohol formed is tert-butyl alcohol, which dehydrates to 2-methylpropene.

    4. Why option \((a)\) fits best

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\) is a tertiary alkyl halide. Tertiary alkyl halides readily undergo hydrolysis by the \(\mathrm{S_N1}\) mechanism because they form a stable tertiary carbocation.

      The corresponding alcohol is also tertiary, and tertiary alcohols dehydrate very easily under acidic conditions by the \(\mathrm{E1}\) mechanism.

    5. Useful reaction picture


    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Hydrolysis of alkyl halide means replacement of halogen by \(\mathrm{-OH}\).
    • So first convert each option mentally into the corresponding alcohol.
    • Then ask: which alcohol dehydrates most easily with acid?
    • The order of ease of dehydration of alcohols is:

      \(\mathrm{3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ}\)

    • This is because dehydration usually proceeds through carbocation formation, and carbocation stability follows the same order.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Hydrolysis of alkyl halides:

      \(\mathrm{R-X \rightarrow R-OH}\)

    • \(\mathrm{S_N1}\) reaction: favoured by tertiary alkyl halides because they form stable carbocations.
    • Dehydration of alcohols: acid-catalysed elimination of water to form alkenes.
    • Common dehydrating acids:

      \(\mathrm{conc.\ H_2SO_4}\) or \(\mathrm{H_3PO_4}\)

    • Ease of dehydration:

      \(\mathrm{3^\circ\ alcohol > 2^\circ\ alcohol > 1^\circ\ alcohol}\)

    • Tertiary alcohols generally dehydrate by the \(\mathrm{E1}\) mechanism.
    • Tertiary alkyl halides also commonly undergo substitution by the \(\mathrm{S_N1}\) mechanism.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • The question says hydrolysis gives alcohol \(Y\), and \(Y\) dehydrates easily with acid.
    • Immediately think:

      \(\mathrm{Y}\) should be a tertiary alcohol.

    • So \(X\) must be the alkyl halide that gives a tertiary alcohol on hydrolysis.
    • Among the options, only:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\)

      gives

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH}\)

    • So mark \((a)\) directly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Looking only at the molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_4H_9Br}\) and not at the class of alkyl halide.
    2. Forgetting that hydrolysis simply replaces \(\mathrm{Br}\) by \(\mathrm{OH}\).
    3. Ignoring the clue hidden in dehydration. That clue strongly points to a tertiary alcohol.
    4. Confusing substitution mechanism \(\mathrm{S_N1}\) with elimination mechanism \(\mathrm{E1}\). Here both are helped by tertiary carbocation stability.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
    • Topic: Nucleophilic substitution and hydrolysis of alkyl halides
    • Subtopic: \(\mathrm{S_N1}\) behaviour of tertiary alkyl halides and dehydration of alcohols

    Final exam-style answer:

    On hydrolysis, \(\mathrm{C_4H_9Br}\) gives an alcohol \(\mathrm{Y}\). Since \(\mathrm{Y}\) undergoes dehydration easily with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), \(\mathrm{Y}\) must be a tertiary alcohol.

    The only alkyl bromide among the given options which gives a tertiary alcohol on hydrolysis is:

    \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\)

    because:

    \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr \rightarrow (CH_3)_3COH}\)

    and then:

    \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH \xrightarrow[358\ K]{20\%\ H_3PO_4} (CH_3)_2C{=}CH_2 + H_2O}\)

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Hydrolysis of alkyl halide:

      \(\mathrm{R-Br \rightarrow R-OH}\)

    • Ease of \(\mathrm{S_N1}\):

      \(\mathrm{3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ}\)

    • Ease of dehydration of alcohols:

      \(\mathrm{3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ}\)

    • Here:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr \rightarrow (CH_3)_3COH \rightarrow (CH_3)_2C{=}CH_2}\)

    • Correct option:

      \(\mathrm{(a)\ (CH_3)_3CBr}\)

    Answer: \((d)\) Acid catalysed hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Use Lucas test clue first

      The alcohol \(X\) gives turbidity instantly with conc. \(\mathrm{HCl}\) and anhydrous \(\mathrm{ZnCl_2}\) (Lucas reagent).

      Instant turbidity in Lucas test means the alcohol is a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.

      Lucas test order:

      \(\mathrm{3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ}\)

      • \(3^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) turbidity immediately
      • \(2^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) turbidity in a few minutes
      • \(1^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) no turbidity at room temperature
    2. So \(X\) must be a tertiary alcohol with formula \(\mathrm{C_5H_{12}O}\)

      The suitable tertiary alcohol is:

      \(\mathrm{2\text{-}methyl\text{-}2\text{-}butanol}\)

      Structure:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2CH_3}\)

    3. Now check each option by the alcohol it produces
      • (a) Reduction of 2-pentanone with \(\mathrm{NaBH_4}\)

        Ketone reduction gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol.

        \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{NaBH_4} CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_2CH_3}\)

        So this cannot be \(X\).

      • (b) Reaction of isopropyl magnesium bromide with ethanal

        Grignard reagent \(+\) ethanal gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol.

        So this also cannot be \(X\).

      • (c) Reaction of ethyl magnesium bromide with propanal

        Grignard reagent \(+\) propanal again gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol.

        So this also cannot be \(X\).

      • (d) Acid catalysed hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene

        Acid-catalysed hydration follows Markovnikov addition.

        \(\mathrm{CH_2=C(CH_3)CH_2CH_3 + H_2O \xrightarrow{H^+} CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2CH_3}\)

        This product is \(\mathrm{2\text{-}methyl\text{-}2\text{-}butanol}\), a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.

        Therefore this matches the Lucas test observation.

    4. Main reaction involved


      Final product:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2CH_3}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(d)\text{ Acid catalysed hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Lucas reagent helps identify the class of alcohol.
    • Since turbidity appears instantly, the alcohol must be tertiary.
    • So instead of fully solving every option in detail, first identify which reaction can produce a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.
    • Acid-catalysed hydration of an unsymmetrical alkene usually follows Markovnikov addition, giving the more stable carbocation intermediate and then the alcohol.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Lucas reagent:

      \(\mathrm{conc.\ HCl + anhyd.\ ZnCl_2}\)

    • Lucas test observation:
      • \(3^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) immediate turbidity
      • \(2^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) turbidity after some time
      • \(1^\circ\) alcohol \(\rightarrow\) no turbidity at room temperature
    • Reason: \(3^\circ\) alcohols form carbocations most easily, so they react fastest with \(\mathrm{HCl}\).
    • Reduction of aldehydes: gives \(1^\circ\) alcohols
    • Reduction of ketones: gives \(2^\circ\) alcohols
    • Grignard reagent + formaldehyde: gives \(1^\circ\) alcohol
    • Grignard reagent + aldehyde: gives \(2^\circ\) alcohol
    • Grignard reagent + ketone: gives \(3^\circ\) alcohol
    • Acid-catalysed hydration of alkene: generally gives Markovnikov alcohol.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Do not start with formula expansion. Start with the words “turbidity instantly”.
    • That directly means:

      \(X\) is a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.

    • Then scan options:
      • ketone reduction \(\rightarrow\) \(2^\circ\) alcohol
      • Grignard \(+\) aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) \(2^\circ\) alcohol
      • Grignard \(+\) aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) \(2^\circ\) alcohol
      • hydration of alkene \(\rightarrow\) can give \(3^\circ\) alcohol
    • So the answer is immediately \((d)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that instant Lucas turbidity means tertiary, not secondary.
    2. Assuming ketone reduction can give a tertiary alcohol. It gives only a secondary alcohol.
    3. Forgetting the Grignard rule:

      \(\mathrm{RMgX + aldehyde \rightarrow 2^\circ\ alcohol}\)

    4. Missing the Markovnikov product in alkene hydration.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Reactions and identification of alcohols
    • Subtopic: Lucas test, preparation of alcohols, and Markovnikov hydration of alkenes

    Final exam-style answer:

    Since alcohol \(X\) gives turbidity instantly with Lucas reagent \(\mathrm{(conc.\ HCl + anhyd.\ ZnCl_2)}\), \(X\) must be a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.

    Among the given reactions:

    • reduction of 2-pentanone gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol,
    • Grignard reaction with ethanal gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol,
    • Grignard reaction with propanal gives a \(2^\circ\) alcohol,
    • acid-catalysed hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene gives \(\mathrm{2\text{-}methyl\text{-}2\text{-}butanol}\), which is a \(3^\circ\) alcohol.

    Hence, the required reaction is:

    \(\boxed{(d)\text{ Acid catalysed hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Lucas reagent:

      \(\mathrm{conc.\ HCl + anhyd.\ ZnCl_2}\)

    • Instant turbidity:

      \(3^\circ\) alcohol

    • Ketone reduction:

      \(2^\circ\) alcohol

    • \(\mathrm{RMgX + aldehyde}\):

      \(2^\circ\) alcohol

    • Hydration of 2-methyl-1-butene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_2=C(CH_3)CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{H^+,H_2O} CH_3-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2CH_3}\)

    • Alcohol formed:

      \(\mathrm{2\text{-}methyl\text{-}2\text{-}butanol}\)

    Answer: \((a)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. First check the assertion

      Assertion (A): Chlorobenzene is not formed in the reaction of phenol with thionyl chloride.

      This statement is correct.

      Thionyl chloride \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) is commonly used to convert alcohols \(\mathrm{R-OH}\) into alkyl chlorides \(\mathrm{R-Cl}\).

      But phenol does not behave like an ordinary alcohol in this reaction, so \(\mathrm{C_6H_5Cl}\) is not obtained from phenol by using \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\).

    2. Now check the reason

      Reason (R): In phenol, carbon–oxygen bond has partial double bond character.

      This is also correct.

      In phenol, the lone pair on oxygen gets delocalized into the benzene ring by resonance. Because of this resonance, the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond is not a pure single bond; it acquires partial double bond character.

      • \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond becomes shorter and stronger than a normal single bond.
      • So cleavage of the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond becomes difficult.
      • Hence replacement of \(\mathrm{-OH}\) by \(\mathrm{-Cl}\) does not occur easily.
    3. Why this explains the assertion

      For conversion of an \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group into \(\mathrm{-Cl}\), the bond between carbon and oxygen must be broken.

      In ordinary alcohols, the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond is a normal single bond, so reagents like \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\), \(\mathrm{PCl_5}\), \(\mathrm{PCl_3}\), or \(\mathrm{HCl/ZnCl_2}\) can replace \(\mathrm{-OH}\) by \(\mathrm{-Cl}\).

      In phenol, however, the \(\mathrm{Ar-O}\) bond is strengthened by resonance:

      • the aryl carbon is \(sp^2\)-hybridized,
      • the oxygen lone pair is conjugated with the benzene ring,
      • the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond gets partial double bond character.

      Therefore, chlorobenzene is not formed from phenol with thionyl chloride.

    4. Hence the correct option

      Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason correctly explains Assertion.

      \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Core theory to remember:

    • Alcohols vs phenols:

      Although both contain \(\mathrm{-OH}\), phenols are not simple alcohols.

    • Why phenol is different:

      In phenol, oxygen is directly attached to an aromatic ring, and resonance makes the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond stronger.

    • Important consequence:

      Reagents that convert alcohols to alkyl halides usually do not convert phenol to aryl halides.

    • Preparation of chlorobenzene:

      Chlorobenzene is generally prepared by chlorination of benzene in presence of a Lewis acid such as \(\mathrm{FeCl_3}\), not by treating phenol with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\).

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Phenols show resonance between oxygen lone pair and aromatic ring.
    • Because of this, \(\mathrm{Ar-OH}\) bond cleavage is difficult.
    • Phenol does not give chlorobenzene with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\), \(\mathrm{PCl_5}\), or \(\mathrm{PCl_3}\).
    • Phenol is much more reactive in electrophilic substitution than benzene because oxygen donates electron density to the ring.
    • But this same resonance also strengthens the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Whenever you see phenol + halogenating reagent for alcohols, pause.
    • Ask: is the \(\mathrm{OH}\) attached to an aryl carbon? If yes, normal substitution is difficult.
    • Recall one keyword: partial double bond character of \(\mathrm{C-O}\).
    • That immediately makes both A and R true, and R explains A.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Assuming phenol behaves exactly like ethanol or other alcohols.
    2. Forgetting that aryl \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond is resonance-stabilized.
    3. Thinking \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) converts every \(\mathrm{-OH}\) compound into chloride.
    4. Confusing nucleophilic substitution in alkyl systems with aryl systems.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Chemical properties of phenols
    • Subtopic: Nature of \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond in phenol and why phenol does not form aryl halide with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\)

    Final exam-style answer:

    In phenol, the oxygen lone pair is delocalized into the benzene ring. Therefore, the \(\mathrm{C-O}\) bond acquires partial double bond character and becomes stronger than a normal single bond. Hence the \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group of phenol is not easily replaced by chlorine on treatment with thionyl chloride. So chlorobenzene is not formed.

    Thus, Assertion is correct. Reason is also correct, and it is the correct explanation of Assertion.

    \(\boxed{(a)\text{ Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).}}\)

    Answer: \((a)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    Given \(pK_a\) values:

    • \(X = 8.3\)
    • \(Y = 7.1\)
    • \(Z = 10.2\)
    1. Use the meaning of \(pK_a\)

      Lower the \(pK_a\), stronger is the acid.

      Therefore, acidity order is:

      \(Y > X > Z\)

    2. Now compare the substituent effects in phenols

      In phenols, electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity because they stabilize the phenoxide ion \(\mathrm{ArO^-}\).

      Electron-donating groups decrease acidity because they destabilize the phenoxide ion.

      • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) is an electron-withdrawing group.

        It shows strong \(-I\) effect and, at ortho/para positions, also strong \(-R\) effect.

      • \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) is an electron-donating group.

        It shows \(+I\) effect, so it makes phenol less acidic.

    3. Match the structures with acidity

      The strongest acid must have the smallest \(pK_a\), that is \(7.1\).

      So \(Y\) must be the nitrophenol in which \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) can withdraw by both resonance and inductive effect most effectively.

      Among the given choices, this corresponds to para-nitrophenol.

      Next, \(X\) has \(pK_a = 8.3\), so it is acidic, but less acidic than \(Y\).

      This matches meta-nitrophenol, where \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) exerts mainly \(-I\) effect and not effective resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion.

      Finally, \(Z\) has \(pK_a = 10.2\), the highest value, so it is the weakest acid.

      This must be cresol \((\mathrm{CH_3}\)-substituted phenol), because \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) donates electron density and decreases acidity.

    4. Hence the correct identification is
      • \(X = m\)-nitrophenol
      • \(Y = p\)-nitrophenol
      • \(Z = p\)-cresol

      So the correct option is \((a)\).



    Why this happens:

    • Acidity of phenol depends on stability of the conjugate base, that is phenoxide ion:

      \(\mathrm{ArOH \rightleftharpoons ArO^- + H^+}\)

    • If a substituent stabilizes \(\mathrm{ArO^-}\), acidity increases and \(pK_a\) decreases.
    • If a substituent destabilizes \(\mathrm{ArO^-}\), acidity decreases and \(pK_a\) increases.

    NCERT / JEE theory to remember:

    • Phenol is more acidic than alcohol because phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilized, whereas alkoxide ion is not stabilized to the same extent.
    • Electron-withdrawing groups like \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\), \(\mathrm{-CN}\), \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) increase acidity of phenol.
    • Electron-donating groups like \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\), \(\mathrm{-OCH_3}\), alkyl groups decrease acidity of phenol.
    • At ortho and para positions, groups can act through both resonance and inductive effects.
    • At the meta position, resonance effect on the phenoxide negative charge is not effective; mainly inductive effect operates.
    • Therefore, for nitrophenols:

      \(o\text{-nitrophenol} \approx p\text{-nitrophenol} > m\text{-nitrophenol} > \text{phenol} > \text{cresol}\)

    Quick resonance idea:

    • In phenoxide ion, negative charge is delocalized to ortho and para positions of the ring.
    • So a \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) group at ortho or para can stabilize that charge by \(-R\) effect.
    • A meta \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) group cannot stabilize it by resonance in the same way.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Remember: higher \(pK_a\) = weaker acid.
    • So first arrange:

      \(7.1 < 8.3 < 10.2\)

      Therefore:

      \(Y > X > Z\) in acidity.

    • Strongest acid should contain strongest electron-withdrawing effect at ortho/para position.
    • Weakest acid should contain electron-donating group like \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\).
    • So directly:

      \(Y = p\)-nitrophenol, \(X = m\)-nitrophenol, \(Z = p\)-cresol

    • This matches option \((a)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that lower \(pK_a\) means stronger acid.
    2. Thinking meta-\(\mathrm{NO_2}\) gives the same resonance stabilization as para-\(\mathrm{NO_2}\).
    3. Ignoring that \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) is electron-releasing and decreases acidity.
    4. Comparing only the neutral molecule instead of stability of the phenoxide ion.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Acidic nature of phenols
    • Subtopic: Effect of electron-withdrawing and electron-donating substituents on acidity of substituted phenols

    Final exam-style answer:

    Lower \(pK_a\) means stronger acid. Hence:

    \(Y > X > Z\)

    \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) increases acidity of phenol, while \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) decreases it.

    Also, para-\(\mathrm{NO_2}\) stabilizes phenoxide ion more than meta-\(\mathrm{NO_2}\) because para position allows both \(-R\) and \(-I\) effects, while meta position mainly shows \(-I\) effect.

    Therefore:

    \(X = m\)-nitrophenol, \quad Y = p\)-nitrophenol, \quad Z = p\)-cresol

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Answer: \((b)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    We have to identify the reagents/chemicals \(X\) and \(Y\) that convert cyanobenzene \((\mathrm{PhCN})\) into a Schiff base.

    1. First identify what a Schiff base is

      A Schiff base is an imine, generally formed by reaction of an aldehyde or ketone with a primary amine.

      General reaction:

      \(\mathrm{RCHO + R'NH_2 \rightarrow RCH{=}NR' + H_2O}\)

      So here, cyanobenzene must first be converted into an aldehyde, and then that aldehyde must react with a primary amine.

    2. Conversion of cyanobenzene to benzaldehyde

      Cyanobenzene is \(\mathrm{PhCN}\), a nitrile.

      A very important NCERT/JEE reaction is:

      DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis reduces nitriles to aldehydes.

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{PhCN \xrightarrow[\phantom{H_2O}]{DIBAL\text{-}H} \text{intermediate} \xrightarrow{H_2O} PhCHO}\)

      • \(X = \mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ H_2O}\)


    3. Now convert benzaldehyde to Schiff base

      Benzaldehyde \(\mathrm{PhCHO}\) must react with a primary amine.

      The product shown is:

      \(\mathrm{PhCH{=}NPh}\)

      This means the amine used must be aniline \(\mathrm{PhNH_2}\).

      • \(Y = \mathrm{PhNH_2}\) (aniline)


    4. Hence the correct reagents are

      \(X = \mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ H_2O}\)

      \(Y = \mathrm{PhNH_2}\)

      So the correct option is \((b)\).

    Overall reaction sequence:

    \(\mathrm{PhCN \xrightarrow[\phantom{H_2O}]{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ H_2O} PhCHO}\)

    \(\mathrm{PhCHO + PhNH_2 \rightarrow PhCH{=}NPh + H_2O}\)

    Why the other options are not correct:

    • Option (a):

      \(\mathrm{NH_2OH}\) is hydroxylamine, which reacts with aldehydes to form an oxime, not the Schiff base shown.

      \(\mathrm{PhCHO + NH_2OH \rightarrow PhCH{=}NOH}\)

    • Option (c):

      \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) reduces nitriles all the way to primary amines, not aldehydes.

      So it would not give the required Schiff base pathway.

    • Option (d):

      \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\) alone cannot convert cyanobenzene into the required aldehyde for Schiff base formation in this sequence.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Nitrile reduction:

      \(\mathrm{RCN \xrightarrow[\phantom{H_2O}]{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ followed\ by\ hydrolysis} RCHO}\)

    • Stronger reduction with \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\):

      \(\mathrm{RCN \xrightarrow{LiAlH_4} RCH_2NH_2}\)

    • Schiff base formation:

      Aldehyde or ketone + primary amine \(\rightarrow\) imine (Schiff base).

    • Oxime formation:

      Aldehyde or ketone + \(\mathrm{NH_2OH}\) \(\rightarrow\) oxime.

    • DIBAL-H is a controlled reducing agent; it can stop reduction at the aldehyde stage under suitable conditions.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See nitrile \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde and immediately think:

      \(\mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H / H_2O}\)

    • See Schiff base and immediately think:

      aldehyde/ketone + primary amine

    • If the product is \(\mathrm{PhCH{=}NPh}\), the amine must be:

      \(\mathrm{PhNH_2}\) (aniline)

    • So directly:

      \(X = \mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ H_2O}\), \(Y = \mathrm{PhNH_2}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Schiff base with oxime.
    2. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{NH_2OH}\) gives \(\mathrm{C{=}NOH}\), not \(\mathrm{C{=}NR}\).
    3. Using \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) for aldehyde formation from nitrile; it usually reduces further to amine.
    4. Missing that the final product itself tells you the amine used:

      \(\mathrm{-NPh}\Rightarrow \mathrm{PhNH_2}\)

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Preparation and reactions of aldehydes
    • Subtopic: Formation of aldehydes from nitriles and Schiff base formation with primary amines

    Final exam-style answer:

    Cyanobenzene \(\mathrm{(PhCN)}\) is first reduced by \(\mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H}\) followed by hydrolysis to give benzaldehyde \(\mathrm{(PhCHO)}\).

    Benzaldehyde then reacts with aniline \(\mathrm{(PhNH_2)}\), a primary amine, to form the Schiff base:

    \(\mathrm{PhCHO + PhNH_2 \rightarrow PhCH{=}NPh + H_2O}\)

    Therefore, \(X = \mathrm{DIBAL\text{-}H,\ H_2O}\) and \(Y = \mathrm{PhNH_2}\).

    \(\boxed{(b)}\)

    • Correct answer: (b) \(\mathrm{-OH,\ -CO-}\).
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The given compound is an aryl ester, and in the presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) it undergoes Fries rearrangement. In this rearrangement, the acyl group migrates from oxygen to the benzene ring, producing a hydroxy aryl ketone. So the final product contains a phenolic \((\mathrm{-OH})\) group and a ketone carbonyl \((\mathrm{-CO-})\) group.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is phenyl benzoate-type structure, where an acyl group is attached through oxygen to a benzene ring.
      • When such aryl esters are treated with a Lewis acid like \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), they undergo Fries rearrangement.
      • In Fries rearrangement, the acyl group \((\mathrm{-COPh})\) shifts from the oxygen atom onto the aromatic ring.
      • As a result, the aromatic ring now gets two important groups:
        • a phenolic \((\mathrm{-OH})\) group
        • an acyl group directly attached to the ring, which behaves as a ketone \((\mathrm{-CO-})\)
      • Therefore, the product contains \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is a named reaction question from aromatic compounds.
      • Fries rearrangement converts phenolic esters into o- and p-hydroxy aryl ketones using \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • The bond between oxygen and acyl fragment is reorganized so that the acyl group finally attaches to the benzene ring.
      • Because the oxygen remains connected to the ring as \(\mathrm{-OH}\), the product is a phenol derivative.
      • Because the migrated acyl group is now directly attached to the ring carbon, the carbonyl behaves as a ketone functional group.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) correctly identifies the two functional groups formed after Fries rearrangement.
      • The product shown is hydroxy benzophenone-type, which clearly contains one \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group and one ketonic carbonyl \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a) is wrong because no aldehyde group \((\mathrm{-CHO})\) is formed.
      • Option (c) is wrong because the \(\mathrm{-O-ph}\) ester linkage does not remain in the final rearranged product.
      • Option (d) is wrong because no carboxylic acid group \((\mathrm{-COOH})\) is formed.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see an aryl ester plus \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), immediately think of Fries rearrangement.
      • Then remember the standard product pattern: hydroxy aryl ketone.
      • So without doing the full mechanism, you can quickly mark \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Why the rearrangement happens
      • \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) is a strong Lewis acid and coordinates with the carbonyl oxygen.
      • This makes the acyl part more reactive and helps generate an acylium-like electrophilic species.
      • That electrophilic acyl fragment attacks the aromatic ring, usually at ortho and para positions relative to oxygen.
      • After workup, the oxygen appears as phenolic \(\mathrm{-OH}\), and the acyl group remains on the ring as a ketone.
    • Final result
      • The functional groups present in product \(X\) are \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aromatic Compounds / Ethers and Phenols
      • Subtopic: Fries rearrangement of aryl esters
    • Relevant answer images
    • Correct answer: (a) I and III
      Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Ethylamine first forms the diazonium salt \(X\), which on hydrolysis gives ethanol \(Y\). Passing ethanol over copper at \(573\ K\) dehydrogenates it to ethanal \((CH_3CHO)\), which is product \(Z\). Ethanal is an aldehyde and it gives the iodoform test. So statements I and III are correct.
    • Reaction sequence leading to \(Z\):
      • Primary aliphatic amine \((C_2H_5NH_2)\) reacts with nitrous acid generated from \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at \(0^{\circ}C\).
      • This forms the corresponding diazonium salt \(X\), which is unstable.
      • On hydrolysis, it gives alcohol \(Y\), that is ethanol \((C_2H_5OH)\).
      • Ethanol on passing over copper at \(573\ K\) gets dehydrogenated to ethanal \((CH_3CHO)\).
      • Therefore, \(Z = CH_3CHO\).
    • Iodoform reaction of ethanal:
      • Ethanal is the only aldehyde that gives the iodoform test.
      • That is why statement III is true.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Start with ethylamine: \(C_2H_5NH_2\)
      • With \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at \(0^{\circ}C\), nitrous acid is generated in situ.
      • Aliphatic primary amines under these conditions form unstable diazonium salts.
      • So: \(C_2H_5NH_2 \rightarrow (C_2H_5N_2^+Cl^-)\)
      • This diazonium salt on reaction with water gives ethanol: \(C_2H_5OH\)
      • Then ethanol over heated copper at \(573\ K\) undergoes dehydrogenation: \(CH_3CH_2OH \rightarrow CH_3CHO\)
      • So product \(Z\) is ethanal.
      • Now test the statements:
        • I. Z is an aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) True
        • II. Z undergoes Cannizzaro reaction \(\rightarrow\) False, because ethanal has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen
        • III. Z gives iodoform test \(\rightarrow\) True
        • IV. Z does not give Tollens' test \(\rightarrow\) False, aldehydes do give Tollens' test
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Primary aliphatic amines react with nitrous acid to form unstable diazonium salts, which usually decompose to alcohols.
      • Alcohol vapours passed over heated copper at \(573\ K\) undergo:
        • dehydrogenation if the alcohol is primary or secondary
        • dehydration is not the main step here
      • Ethanol therefore gives ethanal.
      • Cannizzaro reaction is shown by aldehydes that do not have \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
      • Ethanal has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it does not undergo Cannizzaro reaction.
      • Iodoform test is given by compounds containing the \(CH_3CO-\) group and also by ethanal.
      • Tollens' reagent is given by aldehydes, so ethanal responds positively.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Since \(Z = CH_3CHO\):
        • it is an aldehyde
        • it gives iodoform test
      • So statements I and III are correct.
      • Hence option (a) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • II is wrong because ethanal has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it does not undergo Cannizzaro reaction.
      • IV is wrong because ethanal is an aldehyde and gives Tollens' test.
      • Therefore any option containing II or IV must be rejected.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorise this quick sequence:
        • primary aliphatic amine + \(HNO_2\) \(\rightarrow\) alcohol
        • primary alcohol + \(Cu/573\ K\) \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
      • Also remember this very famous fact: ethanal is the only aldehyde that gives the iodoform test.
      • That single fact lets you mark statement III instantly.
    • Final result:
      • \(Z = CH_3CHO\) (ethanal)
      • Correct statements are I and III.
      • Hence, the correct answer is (a).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Amines / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Diazotisation of aliphatic amines, dehydrogenation of alcohols, tests of aldehydes
      • Subtopic: Formation of ethanol from ethylamine, oxidation product analysis, iodoform and Tollens' tests
    • Correct answer: (c) (A) is correct but (R) is not correct.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Aldehydes are indeed more reactive than ketones towards nucleophilic addition. But the reason given is wrong. In reality, the carbonyl carbon of aldehydes is more electrophilic, not less electrophilic, than that of ketones.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The assertion says aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions.
      • This is correct and is a standard result in carbonyl chemistry.
      • Now check the reason.
      • The reason says that in aldehydes, the carbonyl carbon is less electrophilic than in ketones.
      • This is opposite to the truth.
      • Aldehydes have only one alkyl group and one hydrogen attached to the carbonyl carbon, whereas ketones have two alkyl groups.
      • Alkyl groups show \(+I\) effect and push electron density toward the carbonyl carbon.
      • This reduces the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon in ketones.
      • So ketones are less electrophilic than aldehydes.
      • That is why aldehydes attract nucleophiles more easily.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Nucleophilic addition takes place at the carbonyl carbon because that carbon is electron-deficient.
      • The more positive or electrophilic the carbonyl carbon is, the more easily a nucleophile attacks it.
      • Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones for two main reasons:
        • Electronic factor: ketones have two alkyl groups, which reduce electrophilicity by \(+I\) effect.
        • Steric factor: ketones are more crowded, so nucleophile attack becomes harder.
      • Thus aldehydes are both less hindered and more electrophilic than ketones.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Assertion is correct because aldehydes really do undergo nucleophilic addition more readily than ketones.
      • Reason is incorrect because it states the opposite electronic effect.
      • So the correct choice is: (A) is correct but (R) is not correct.
    • Why the other options are wrong:
      • (a) is wrong because the reason is not correct.
      • (b) is also wrong for the same reason.
      • (d) is wrong because the assertion itself is true.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you compare aldehydes and ketones in nucleophilic addition, remember: aldehydes > ketones.
      • Reason: less steric hindrance + more electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
      • If a statement says aldehydes are less electrophilic than ketones, it is immediately false.
    • Final result:
      • The assertion is true.
      • The reason is false.
      • Hence, the correct answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Reactivity of carbonyl compounds
      • Subtopic: Nucleophilic addition reactions of aldehydes and ketones
    • Correct answer: (d) II > III > IV > I
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The main factor controlling the boiling points here is intermolecular force. Alcohols have hydrogen bonding, so they boil at much higher temperatures than ether and alkane. Between the two alcohols, the less branched one has a higher boiling point. Between pentane and diethyl ether, pentane has the slightly higher boiling point here. Hence the order is II > III > IV > I.
    • Boiling point comparison of the given compounds:
      • The image helps identify the same final order: II > III > IV > I.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us identify the four compounds first:
        • I \(=\) diethyl ether, \((C_2H_5)_2O\)
        • II \(=\) \(n\)-butyl alcohol, \(CH_3(CH_2)_3OH\)
        • III \(=\) isobutyl alcohol, \(CH_3CH(CH_3)CH_2OH\)
        • IV \(=\) \(n\)-pentane, \(CH_3(CH_2)_3CH_3\)
      • Alcohols have hydrogen bonding, so both II and III must have higher boiling points than I and IV.
      • Now compare the two alcohols.
      • II is a straight-chain alcohol, while III is branched.
      • Branching reduces surface area and weakens intermolecular attraction, so: II > III.
      • Now compare I and IV.
      • I is an ether, so it has dipole-dipole interactions but no hydrogen bonding.
      • IV is pentane, which is non-polar, but it has a larger molecular size and stronger dispersion forces than diethyl ether in this comparison.
      • So: IV > I.
      • Combining all results: II > III > IV > I.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Boiling point depends mainly on the strength of intermolecular forces.
      • Common order of intermolecular force strength here is: hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > dispersion, though molecular size also matters.
      • Alcohols show hydrogen bonding because of the \(O-H\) bond.
      • Ethers do not show intermolecular hydrogen bonding among themselves, so their boiling points are lower than those of comparable alcohols.
      • Among isomeric alcohols, increased branching usually lowers boiling point.
      • Among hydrocarbons, higher molecular mass and larger surface area generally increase boiling point.
    • Why the correct option is right:
