      • Because the \(-OH\) group already present on phenol is an ortho-para directing group, substitution occurs mainly at the ortho position.
      • So the major product is \(o\)-hydroxy benzaldehyde, also called salicylaldehyde.
      • In the Kolbe reaction (more precisely Kolbe-Schmitt reaction), sodium phenoxide reacts with \(CO_2\) under pressure and then on acidification gives a carboxylic acid.
      • Again, because the \(-OH\) group activates the ring and directs incoming substitution to ortho and para positions, the ortho product predominates.
      • Thus the major product is \(o\)-hydroxy benzoic acid, also called salicylic acid.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Phenol contains an \(-OH\) group attached directly to the benzene ring.
      • The oxygen lone pair donates electron density into the ring by resonance, making the ring more reactive toward electrophilic substitution.
      • This resonance donation increases electron density particularly at the ortho and para positions.
      • That is why many reactions of phenol give ortho/para substituted products.
      • In both these named reactions, the ortho product is especially important and commonly asked in entrance exams.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) gives salicylaldehyde as the major product of Reimer-Tiemann reaction and salicylic acid as the major product of Kolbe’s reaction.
      • That matches the standard NCERT/JEE chemistry facts exactly.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a): Phenol itself and benzaldehyde are not the required products of these named reactions.
      • Option (c): Reimer-Tiemann reaction does not give \(o\)-cresol; it gives a formyl-substituted phenol, not a methyl-substituted phenol.
      • Option (d): These compounds are unrelated to the standard products of Reimer-Tiemann and Kolbe reactions.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Remember this easy memory link:
      • Reimer-Tiemann \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde on phenol
      • Kolbe-Schmitt \(\rightarrow\) carboxylic acid on phenol
      • If the product has \(-CHO\), think Reimer-Tiemann.
      • If the product has \(-COOH\), think Kolbe-Schmitt.
      • For both, the major product is usually the ortho derivative.
    • Final result
      • Major products are \(o\)-hydroxy benzaldehyde and \(o\)-hydroxy benzoic acid.
      • Hence, option (b) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Subtopic: Named reactions of phenol (Reimer-Tiemann reaction and Kolbe-Schmitt reaction)
    • Correct answer: (c) Etard reaction.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Sodium benzoate first gives benzene on decarboxylation, benzene gives toluene by Friedel-Crafts alkylation, and toluene on oxidation with chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\) in \(CS_2\) followed by hydrolysis gives benzaldehyde. This oxidation of an aromatic methyl group to an aldehyde is called the Etard reaction.
    • Reaction sequence
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Step 1: Sodium benzoate on heating with soda lime \((NaOH + CaO)\) undergoes decarboxylation.
      • That removes the \(-COO^-Na^+\) group and gives benzene. So \(A =\) benzene.
      • Step 2: Benzene reacts with \(CH_3Cl\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) by Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
      • This introduces a methyl group into the ring and forms toluene. So \(B =\) toluene.
      • Step 3: Toluene reacts with chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\) in \(CS_2\), and after hydrolysis \((H_3O^+)\), it forms benzaldehyde. So \(C =\) benzaldehyde.
      • The named reaction for converting an aromatic \(-CH_3\) group into \(-CHO\) using chromyl chloride is called the Etard reaction.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Soda lime decarboxylation converts sodium salt of a carboxylic acid into a hydrocarbon having one carbon less.
      • Friedel-Crafts alkylation introduces an alkyl group into benzene using alkyl halide and \(AlCl_3\).
      • Etard reaction is a controlled oxidation of the side-chain methyl group of aromatic compounds to aldehyde, not all the way to carboxylic acid.
      • This is important because many oxidizing agents would take toluene directly to benzoic acid, but chromyl chloride gives the aldehyde stage.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • \(B\) is toluene and \(C\) is benzaldehyde.
      • The reagent set \((CrO_2Cl_2 / CS_2)\) followed by hydrolysis is the classic clue for Etard oxidation.
      • Therefore conversion of \(B\) to \(C\) is Etard reaction.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Stephen’s reaction: Converts nitriles into aldehydes, so it does not fit this sequence.
      • Rosenmund reduction: Converts acid chlorides to aldehydes using poisoned catalyst, not methyl side-chain oxidation.
      • Gatterman reaction: Refers to formylation/halogenation-related named reactions depending on context, but not this chromyl chloride oxidation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see \(CrO_2Cl_2\) acting on toluene or an alkyl benzene to form an aldehyde, immediately think Etard reaction.
      • Also remember:
      • Stephen \(\rightarrow\) nitrile to aldehyde
      • Rosenmund \(\rightarrow\) acid chloride to aldehyde
      • Etard \(\rightarrow\) alkyl benzene to aldehyde
    • Final result
      • \(A =\) benzene, \(B =\) toluene, \(C =\) benzaldehyde.
      • Thus \(B \rightarrow C\) is Etard reaction.
      • Hence, option (c) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Named reactions involving aromatic compounds and side-chain oxidation
    • Correct answer: (c) Lucas test.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Lucas test is used to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols. Since 1-propanol is a primary alcohol and 2-propanol is a secondary alcohol, they can be differentiated by this test.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • First identify the type of alcohols given in the question.
      • 1-propanol has the \(-OH\) group on a primary carbon, so it is a primary alcohol.
      • 2-propanol has the \(-OH\) group on a carbon attached to two other carbons, so it is a secondary alcohol.
      • Lucas test is specifically designed to classify alcohols as primary, secondary, or tertiary.
      • Hence it can distinguish between these two compounds.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Lucas reagent is a mixture of conc. HCl and anhydrous \(ZnCl_2\).
      • This reagent converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides.
      • The rate of reaction depends on how easily the intermediate carbocation forms.
      • Tertiary alcohols react immediately and show turbidity at once.
      • Secondary alcohols react more slowly and show turbidity after a few minutes.
      • Primary alcohols do not react easily at room temperature, so turbidity appears very slowly or not at all under test conditions.
      • Therefore:
        • tertiary alcohol → immediate turbidity
        • secondary alcohol → turbidity after some time
        • primary alcohol → no turbidity at room temperature for a long time
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • 1-propanol and 2-propanol belong to different classes of alcohols.
      • Lucas test directly distinguishes alcohols based on this classification.
      • So it is the correct test here.
    • Why the other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) 2, 4-DNP test is used for aldehydes and ketones, not for alcohols.
      • (b) Tollen's test is mainly used to detect aldehydes.
      • (d) Fehling's test is also used for aldehydes, especially aliphatic aldehydes.
      • None of these three is meant for distinguishing primary and secondary alcohols.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever the question says differentiate 1°, 2°, and 3° alcohols, think Lucas test.
      • Whenever you see 2, 4-DNP, Tollen's, or Fehling's, think carbonyl compounds rather than alcohols.
    • Final result: 1-propanol can be distinguished from 2-propanol by Lucas test.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Distinction tests for alcohols.
    • Correct answer: (b) m-chlorobenzoate and m-chlorobenzyl alcohol.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: m-chlorobenzaldehyde does not have an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so in concentrated NaOH it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction. One molecule is oxidized to m-chlorobenzoate and the other is reduced to m-chlorobenzyl alcohol.
    • Reaction scheme:
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The compound given is m-chlorobenzaldehyde, which is an aromatic aldehyde.
      • For aldol condensation to occur, the aldehyde must contain at least one \(\alpha\)-hydrogen on the carbon next to the carbonyl group.
      • In benzaldehyde and substituted benzaldehydes like m-chlorobenzaldehyde, the carbonyl carbon is attached directly to the benzene ring.
      • So there is no \(\alpha\)-carbon carrying hydrogen in the required sense, and hence no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen is present.
      • Therefore, in concentrated NaOH, such aldehydes undergo Cannizzaro reaction instead of aldol reaction.
      • In Cannizzaro reaction, two molecules of the aldehyde react together.
      • One molecule gets oxidized to the carboxylate salt.
      • The other molecule gets reduced to the corresponding alcohol.
      • Hence the products are:
        • m-chlorobenzoate \((\text{as sodium salt in NaOH medium})\)
        • m-chlorobenzyl alcohol
      • This matches option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Cannizzaro reaction is shown by aldehydes that do not contain \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
      • Under strong base such as concentrated NaOH, disproportionation occurs.
      • That means the same aldehyde undergoes both oxidation and reduction at the same time.
      • The general reaction is: \(2RCHO + OH^- \rightarrow RCOO^- + RCH_2OH\)
      • Aromatic aldehydes such as benzaldehyde and ring-substituted benzaldehydes commonly show this reaction.
      • The chlorine substituent at meta position remains unchanged during the reaction.
      • Only the \(-CHO\) group participates in conversion to \(-COO^-\) and \(-CH_2OH\).
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) correctly shows one product with \(-COO^-Na^+\) and the other with \(-CH_2OH\), while keeping the chlorine substituent at the meta position.
      • That is exactly what Cannizzaro reaction predicts for m-chlorobenzaldehyde.
    • Why the other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) shows products with \(-OH\) substituent on the ring, but the starting compound contains chlorine, not hydroxyl. So this is structurally incorrect.
      • (c) and (d) represent diol-type coupled products, which are not formed in this standard reaction with concentrated NaOH.
      • The reaction here is not pinacol coupling or any such coupling process. It is Cannizzaro disproportionation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • See an aldehyde + concentrated NaOH?
      • First ask: Does it have \(\alpha\)-hydrogen?
      • If no, quickly think Cannizzaro reaction.
      • Then write:
        • one molecule → acid salt
        • one molecule → alcohol
      • Also be careful that ring substituents like \(Cl\), \(NO_2\), \(CH_3\) usually stay where they are unless the question specifically involves substitution on the ring.
    • Final result: On heating with concentrated NaOH, m-chlorobenzaldehyde gives sodium m-chlorobenzoate and m-chlorobenzyl alcohol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids / Cannizzaro reaction of aldehydes without \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    • Correct answer: (b) both statements I and II are true.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Aldehydes undergo nucleophilic addition with HCN to form cyanohydrins, and in a cyanohydrin both the \(OH\) group and the \(CN\) group are attached to the same carbon atom.
    • Cyanohydrin formation:
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • This is a statement-based question from aldehydes and ketones.
      • Whenever an aldehyde reacts with HCN, the \(CN^-\) ion attacks the carbonyl carbon.
      • After this nucleophilic addition, the oxygen gets protonated and becomes an \(OH\) group.
      • So the final product contains both \(OH\) and \(CN\) attached to the same carbon atom.
      • That product is called a cyanohydrin.
      • Therefore, Statement I is correct.
      • Statement II is also correct because it gives the correct structural meaning of cyanohydrin.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Aldehydes and ketones have a polar carbonyl group \((C=O)\).
      • The carbonyl carbon is electrophilic, so nucleophiles attack it.
      • HCN provides the nucleophile \(CN^-\), which adds to the carbonyl carbon.
      • The general reaction for an aldehyde is: \(RCHO + HCN \rightarrow RCH(OH)CN\)
      • In the product, one carbon bears both:
        • \(OH\) group
        • \(CN\) group
      • This is why the name cyanohydrin makes sense:
        • cyano for \(CN\)
        • hydrin indicating \(OH\)
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Statement I matches the standard reaction of aldehydes with HCN.
      • Statement II correctly defines the product formed.
      • So both statements are true, making option (b) correct.
    • Why the other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) is wrong because both statements are not false.
      • (c) is wrong because Statement II is not false; it is a correct definition.
      • (d) is wrong because Statement I is also true.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see carbonyl compound + HCN, immediately think cyanohydrin formation.
      • Remember the product pattern: \(C=O \rightarrow C(OH)(CN)\)
      • If both \(OH\) and \(CN\) are on the same carbon, it is cyanohydrin.
    • Final result: Both Statement I and Statement II are true.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids / Nucleophilic addition reactions of aldehydes and ketones.
    • Correct answer: (d)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The first reagent set \((CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2,\ H_3O^+)\) converts the benzylic methyl group into an aldehyde group by Etard reaction, giving benzaldehyde. Then nitration occurs. Since the \(-CHO\) group is strongly electron withdrawing and meta-directing, the nitro group enters the meta position. So \(X\) is 3-nitrobenzaldehyde, which is option (d).
    • Reaction sequence
      • The image shows the key two-step logic: first form benzaldehyde, then nitrate it to obtain the meta-substituted product.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Step 1: Identify the first reaction. \(CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2\) followed by hydrolysis is the Etard reaction.
      • This reaction oxidizes the benzylic methyl group of toluene to an aldehyde group, not all the way to acid under these conditions.
      • So after step (i), the intermediate is:
        • benzaldehyde, \(C_6H_5CHO\)
      • Step 2: Apply nitration. Now benzaldehyde is treated with \(HNO_3/H_2SO_4\), which introduces a nitro group by electrophilic aromatic substitution.
      • The \(-CHO\) group is:
        • electron withdrawing
        • deactivating
        • meta-directing
      • Therefore the nitro group enters at the meta position with respect to \(-CHO\).
      • So the final product is:
        • 3-nitrobenzaldehyde
      • This matches option (d).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Etard reaction: Oxidation of the side-chain methyl group of an alkyl benzene to an aldehyde using chromyl chloride.
      • Directive influence of \(-CHO\): The aldehyde group withdraws electron density by both \(-I\) effect and \(-M\) effect.
      • Because of this, ortho and para positions become relatively less favorable in the sigma-complex formed during electrophilic substitution.
      • Hence electrophiles prefer the meta position in benzaldehyde.
      • This is a very standard directing-effect question from aromatic compounds.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) shows benzaldehyde carrying the nitro group at the meta position.
      • That is exactly what is expected after Etard oxidation followed by nitration.
    • Why the other options are wrong
      • (a) shows the acid group \(-CO_2H\), but Etard oxidation stops at aldehyde, not carboxylic acid.
      • (b) shows \(-CH_2Cl\), which is not the product of this reagent sequence.
      • (c) shows \(-COCl\), an acid chloride, again unrelated to the given reagents.
      • Only (d) has the correct functional group and the correct meta orientation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorize this high-yield fact:
        • \(CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2,\ H_3O^+\) on an alkyl benzene \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde at the benzylic carbon
      • Then remember the directing rule:
        • \(-CHO,\ -COOH,\ -COR,\ -NO_2,\ -CN,\ -SO_3H\) are generally meta directors
      • So once benzaldehyde is formed, nitration at meta becomes the immediate answer.
    • Final result
      • \(X =\) 3-nitrobenzaldehyde.
      • Answer: (d)
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Compounds Containing Oxygen
      • Topic: Aromatic substitution and named reactions
      • Subtopic: Etard reaction and directive influence of \(-CHO\)
    • Correct answer: (d) A is false but R is true
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The assertion has the \(pK_a\) values reversed. Actually, phenol has \(pK_a \approx 10\), while benzoic acid has \(pK_a \approx 4.19\). The reason statement is correct because benzoate ion is more resonance-stabilised by equivalent structures, whereas phenoxide ion has non-equivalent resonance forms. This greater stabilisation makes benzoic acid stronger than phenol.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First check the numerical fact in the assertion.
      • The standard acidity values are:
        • Phenol: \(pK_a \approx 10\)
        • Benzoic acid: \(pK_a \approx 4.19\)
      • But the assertion says:
        • phenol \(= 4.19\)
        • benzoic acid \(= 10\)
      • So the assertion is false.
      • Now examine the reason.
      • Acid strength depends on the stability of the conjugate base.
      • Benzoic acid forms benzoate ion after losing \(\mathrm{H^+}\).
      • In benzoate ion, the negative charge is delocalised over two oxygen atoms through equivalent resonance structures.
      • This gives strong stabilisation.
      • Phenol forms phenoxide ion after losing \(\mathrm{H^+}\).
      • In phenoxide ion, the negative charge can resonate into the aromatic ring, but these resonance structures are non-equivalent and place charge partly on less electronegative carbon atoms.
      • So phenoxide ion is less stabilised than benzoate ion.
      • Therefore the reason statement is true.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Lower \(pK_a\) means stronger acid.
      • So benzoic acid with \(pK_a \approx 4.19\) is much stronger than phenol with \(pK_a \approx 10\).
      • The key idea is conjugate base stability.
      • Benzoate ion is strongly stabilised because the negative charge is delocalised mainly over oxygen atoms, and the important resonance forms are equivalent.
      • Phenoxide ion is resonance-stabilised too, but some resonance forms place negative charge on carbon, which is less electronegative than oxygen. So the stabilisation is less effective.
      • Hence benzoic acid is more acidic than phenol.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Assertion is false because the \(pK_a\) values are interchanged.
      • Reason is true because the resonance comparison given is chemically correct.
      • Therefore the correct choice is (d).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) is wrong because the assertion is not true.
      • (b) is wrong because again the assertion is false.
      • (c) is wrong because the reason is not false; it is true.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Remember one quick acidity benchmark:
        • carboxylic acids are much stronger acids than phenols
      • So if a question says phenol has lower \(pK_a\) than benzoic acid, it is immediately suspicious.
      • Also remember:
        • equivalent resonance over oxygen atoms \(\rightarrow\) stronger conjugate base stabilisation
    • Final result
      • Phenol has \(pK_a \approx 10\), benzoic acid has \(pK_a \approx 4.19\).
      • So Assertion is false, Reason is true.
      • Hence the answer is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Acidity and resonance stabilisation
      • Subtopic: Comparison of phenol and benzoic acid acidity
    • Correct answer: (c) A and D only
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: We must check which reactant–reagent pairs actually give benzaldehyde \((\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO})\). Pair A is correct because benzoyl chloride on Rosenmund reduction gives benzaldehyde. Pair D is also correct because benzonitrile on partial reduction with \(\mathrm{AlH(t-Bu)_2}\) followed by hydrolysis gives benzaldehyde. Pairs B and C do not give benzaldehyde.
    • Key reaction paths for the given pairs
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • This question is best solved by checking each reaction separately.
      • Pair A: Benzoyl chloride \((\mathrm{C_6H_5COCl})\) with \(\mathrm{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4}\)
        • This is Rosenmund reduction.
        • Acid chlorides on Rosenmund reduction are converted to aldehydes.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COCl \xrightarrow{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4} C_6H_5CHO}\)
        • Hence A gives benzaldehyde.
      • Pair B: Ethyl benzoate \((\mathrm{C_6H_5COOCH_2CH_3})\) with \((i)\ \mathrm{LAH},\ (ii)\ \mathrm{H_2O}\)
        • \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) reduces esters completely to alcohols.
        • The acyl part becomes benzyl alcohol, not benzaldehyde.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COOCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{LAH/H_2O} C_6H_5CH_2OH + CH_3CH_2OH}\)
        • Hence B does not give benzaldehyde.
      • Pair C: Toluene \((\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3})\) with \(\mathrm{KMnO_4/H^+}\)
        • \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) is a strong oxidising agent.
        • It oxidises the alkyl side chain of toluene all the way to carboxylic acid.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3 \xrightarrow{KMnO_4/H^+} C_6H_5COOH}\)
        • Product is benzoic acid, not benzaldehyde.
      • Pair D: Benzonitrile \((\mathrm{C_6H_5CN})\) with \((i)\ \mathrm{AlH(t-Bu)_2},\ (ii)\ \mathrm{H_2O}\)
        • \(\mathrm{AlH(t-Bu)_2}\) is diisobutylaluminium hydride-type selective partial reduction reagent in this syllabus context.
        • Nitriles under controlled partial reduction followed by hydrolysis give aldehydes.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CN \xrightarrow{(i)\ AlH(t-Bu)_2\ (ii)\ H_2O} C_6H_5CHO}\)
        • Hence D gives benzaldehyde.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This question tests named reactions and selective reductions / oxidations.
      • Rosenmund reduction:
        • Acid chloride \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde using \(\mathrm{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4}\)
      • \(\mathrm{LiAlH_4}\) reduction of ester:
        • Ester \(\rightarrow\) primary alcohol(s)
      • Oxidation of side chain by \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\):
        • Alkyl benzene \(\rightarrow\) benzoic acid
      • Partial reduction of nitriles:
        • Nitrile \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde after controlled reduction and hydrolysis
      • So the trick is not just “reduction gives aldehyde”; it depends on the substrate and reagent pair.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • A gives benzaldehyde by Rosenmund reduction.
      • D gives benzaldehyde by partial reduction of nitrile followed by hydrolysis.
      • Therefore the correct pair is A and D only, which is option (c).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) A and C only is wrong because C gives benzoic acid, not benzaldehyde.
      • (b) C and D only is wrong because C is incorrect, though D is correct.
      • (d) B and C only is wrong because both B and C fail to give benzaldehyde.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorise these high-yield conversions:
        • \(\mathrm{RCOCl \xrightarrow{H_2/Pd-BaSO_4} RCHO}\)
        • \(\mathrm{RCOOR' \xrightarrow{LiAlH_4} RCH_2OH}\)
        • \(\mathrm{Ar-CH_3 \xrightarrow{KMnO_4} Ar-COOH}\)
        • \(\mathrm{RCN \xrightarrow{controlled\ reduction} RCHO}\)
      • If you know these four patterns, this whole question becomes a straight elimination problem.
    • Final result
      • Only pairs A and D form benzaldehyde.
      • Hence the correct answer is (c) A and D only.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids / Organic Conversions
      • Topic: Preparation of benzaldehyde
      • Subtopic: Rosenmund reduction, ester reduction, side-chain oxidation, nitrile partial reduction
    • Correct answer: (c) \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+ ,\ Cu/573\ K}\)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The alkene \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\) that does not show geometrical isomerism is but-1-ene, \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3}\). On hydration with \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+}\), it gives butan-2-ol, and on passing its vapours over Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\), the secondary alcohol is dehydrogenated to butan-2-one. Since butan-2-one is a methyl ketone \((\mathrm{CH_3CO-})\), it gives the iodoform test. Hence option (c) is correct.
    • Conversion of \(X\) into the iodoform-positive product \(Y\)
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify the alkene \(X\).
      • The molecular formula is \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\), so possible alkenes are:
        • but-1-ene \(\mathrm{(CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3)}\)
        • but-2-ene \(\mathrm{(CH_3CH{=}CHCH_3)}\)
        • 2-methylpropene \(\mathrm{((CH_3)_2C{=}CH_2)}\)
      • The question says \(X\) does not exhibit geometrical isomerism.
      • But-2-ene shows cis-trans isomerism, so it is ruled out.
      • From the attached solution and the reaction flow, the intended alkene is but-1-ene:
        • \(\mathrm{X = CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3}\)
      • Now test the reagent sequences.
      • Option (c): \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+}\) followed by \(\mathrm{Cu/573\ K}\)
        • Acid-catalysed hydration of but-1-ene follows Markovnikov addition.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+} CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3}\)
        • The product is butan-2-ol, a secondary alcohol.
        • Secondary alcohols on heating with copper at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\) undergo dehydrogenation to ketones.
        • Thus:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{Cu/573\,K} CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\)
        • The final product \(Y\) is butan-2-one.
        • Butan-2-one contains the \(\mathrm{CH_3CO-}\) group, so it gives the iodoform test.
      • Why the other options fail
        • Option (a): Hydroboration-oxidation gives butan-1-ol from but-1-ene. Oxidation with PCC converts primary alcohol into butanal:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{BH_3,\ H_2O_2/OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow{PCC} CH_3CH_2CH_2CHO}\)
        • Butanal does not give the iodoform test.
        • Option (b): Hydration gives butan-2-ol, but \(\mathrm{ZnCl_2/HCl}\) converts alcohol into alkyl chloride:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{ZnCl_2/HCl} CH_3CHClCH_2CH_3}\)
        • An alkyl chloride does not give the iodoform test.
        • Option (d): Hydroboration-oxidation again gives butan-1-ol, and Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\) converts a primary alcohol into an aldehyde:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow{Cu/573\,K} CH_3CH_2CH_2CHO}\)
        • Butanal does not give the iodoform test.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Iodoform test is given by:
        • methyl ketones \(\mathrm{(RCOCH_3)}\)
        • and alcohols of the type \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)R}\), because they can be oxidised to methyl ketones
      • Acid-catalysed hydration of an unsymmetrical alkene follows Markovnikov’s rule.
      • Hydroboration-oxidation follows anti-Markovnikov addition and gives the alcohol at the less substituted carbon.
      • Alcohols with Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\):
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) generally dehydration
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Only option (c) converts but-1-ene into butan-2-one.
      • Butan-2-one is a methyl ketone.
      • Therefore \(Y\) gives the iodoform test.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Options (a) and (d) give butanal, not a methyl ketone.
      • Option (b) gives an alkyl chloride, which does not satisfy the iodoform clue.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see “gives iodoform test,” quickly look for formation of:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CO-}\) group, or
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)-}\) group
      • Here, the fastest route is:
        • alkene \(\rightarrow\) secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) methyl ketone
      • That immediately points to hydration followed by Cu/\(573\ \mathrm{K}\).
    • Final result
      • \(\mathrm{X = CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3}\)
      • \(\mathrm{Y = CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\), which gives the iodoform test.
      • So the correct sequence is (c) \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+ ,\ Cu/573\ K}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Topic: Alkene reactions and functional group interconversion
      • Subtopic: Hydration of alkenes, dehydrogenation of alcohols, iodoform test
    • Correct answer: (b)
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Option (b) is feasible because PCC oxidises a primary alcohol to an aldehyde without over-oxidation. Thus \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CHCH_2OH}\) is converted into \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CHCHO}\). The other reactions are not feasible for the reasons given below.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • We must examine each option separately and ask whether the shown reagent can really give the shown product.
      • Option (a)
        • The reactant is phenol reacting with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) to form chlorobenzene.
        • This conversion does not occur normally in this way.
        • \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides mainly through a substitution process that works at an \(\mathrm{sp^3}\)-hybridised carbon bearing the \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group.
        • In phenol, the carbon attached to \(\mathrm{-OH}\) is part of an aromatic ring and is \(\mathrm{sp^2}\)-hybridised.
        • So phenol does not give chlorobenzene with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\).
      • Option (b)
        • The reactant is \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CHCH_2OH}\), an unsaturated primary alcohol.
        • PCC \((\)pyridinium chlorochromate\()\) is a mild oxidising agent.
        • It oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes and usually stops there.
        • Therefore:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CHCH_2OH \xrightarrow{PCC} CH_3CH{=}CHCHO}\)
        • This exactly matches the given product.
        • Hence option (b) is feasible.
      • Option (c)
        • The reactant is a primary alcohol being oxidised with acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) to an aldehyde.
        • This is not feasible as written.
        • Acidified \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) is a strong oxidising agent.
        • It does not usually stop at the aldehyde stage for primary alcohols.
        • It further oxidises the aldehyde to the corresponding carboxylic acid.
      • Option (d)
        • The reaction shows a primary alcohol giving an alkene with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(273\ \mathrm{K}\).
        • These are mild conditions and are not suitable for dehydration of a primary alcohol to alkene in the normal entrance-exam sense.
        • Primary alcohol dehydration generally needs stronger conditions, such as concentrated acid and higher temperature.
        • So the shown conversion is not feasible.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • PCC oxidation:
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • it avoids over-oxidation under suitable conditions
      • Acidified \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\):
        • strong oxidising agent
        • primary alcohols usually go all the way to carboxylic acids
      • \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\):
        • works well for converting aliphatic alcohols into alkyl chlorides
        • does not directly convert phenol into chlorobenzene
      • Dehydration of alcohols:
        • requires sufficiently strong acidic conditions and heat
        • mild conditions are usually insufficient, especially for primary alcohols
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) is exactly the standard oxidation of a primary allylic alcohol to the corresponding aldehyde by PCC.
      • No over-oxidation is expected under these conditions.
      • So the product drawn in option (b) is chemically correct.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) phenol does not form aryl chloride with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) in this way.
      • (c) acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) would oxidise further to carboxylic acid, not stop at aldehyde.
      • (d) the dehydration conditions are too mild for the shown primary alcohol to give alkene as written.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorise these three high-yield rules:
        • PCC: primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • \(\mathrm{KMnO_4/H^+}\): primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) carboxylic acid
        • Phenol does not behave like a simple aliphatic alcohol with \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\)
      • If you spot PCC with a primary alcohol and the product is an aldehyde, that is often the feasible option.
    • Final result
      • The feasible reaction is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Organic Reagents
      • Topic: Oxidation and substitution reactions of alcohols and phenols
      • Subtopic: PCC oxidation, \(\mathrm{SOCl_2}\) reaction, dehydration conditions
    • Correct answer: (b) It undergoes Cannizzaro reaction.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The final product \(R\) is ethanal \((\mathrm{CH_3CHO})\). Ethanal contains an \( \alpha \)-hydrogen, so it does not undergo Cannizzaro reaction. Therefore the statement saying that it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction is incorrect.
    • Reaction sequence and product map
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Start with ethene, \( \mathrm{C_2H_4} \).
      • On ozonolysis followed by reductive workup \( (\mathrm{O_3}/\mathrm{Zn},\mathrm{H_2O}) \), the double bond is cleaved.
      • Ethene is symmetric, so each carbon becomes the same carbonyl compound.
      • Thus, \(P\) is methanal, \( \mathrm{HCHO} \).
      • Now methanal reacts with \( \mathrm{CH_3MgBr} \) followed by hydrolysis.
      • Grignard reagent adds a methyl group to the carbonyl carbon of methanal, giving the primary alcohol ethanol.
      • So, \(Q = \mathrm{CH_3CH_2OH}\).
      • Oxidation of ethanol with PCC in \( \mathrm{CH_2Cl_2} \) gives the aldehyde ethanal.
      • Therefore, \(R = \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\).
    • Now test each statement for ethanal
      • (a) It gives Tollen’s test. True, because aldehydes reduce Tollen’s reagent.
      • (b) It undergoes Cannizzaro reaction. False, because Cannizzaro reaction is given by aldehydes without \( \alpha \)-hydrogen. Ethanal has \( \alpha \)-hydrogen.
      • (c) It gives iodoform test. True, because ethanal contains the \( \mathrm{CH_3CO{-}} \)-equivalent pattern needed for iodoform test.
      • (d) It undergoes aldol condensation. True, because ethanal has \( \alpha \)-hydrogen.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond and converts alkene carbons into carbonyl carbons.
      • Grignard reagents add carbon groups to carbonyl compounds, increasing carbon chain length.
      • PCC is a mild oxidising agent that converts primary alcohols to aldehydes without further oxidation to acids.
      • Cannizzaro reaction is shown only by aldehydes lacking \( \alpha \)-hydrogen, such as formaldehyde and benzaldehyde.
      • Aldol condensation requires \( \alpha \)-hydrogen, so ethanal readily undergoes it.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) is the incorrect statement because ethanal cannot give Cannizzaro reaction.
      • The presence of \( \alpha \)-hydrogen prevents ethanal from falling into the Cannizzaro category.
    • Why the other options are right
      • (a) Aldehydes like ethanal give Tollen’s test.
      • (c) Ethanal gives iodoform test.
      • (d) Ethanal undergoes aldol condensation because it has \( \alpha \)-hydrogen.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever a question asks about Cannizzaro reaction, immediately check for \( \alpha \)-hydrogen.
      • No \( \alpha \)-H \(\Rightarrow\) Cannizzaro possible.
      • Has \( \alpha \)-H \(\Rightarrow\) Cannizzaro not possible, aldol more likely.
      • Also remember:
        • \( \mathrm{HCHO} \) gives Cannizzaro.
        • \( \mathrm{CH_3CHO} \) gives aldol, not Cannizzaro.
    • Final result
      • \(R = \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) (ethanal).
      • The incorrect statement is (b): “It undergoes Cannizzaro reaction.”
    • Chapter / Topic / Subtopic
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Reactions of aldehydes and ketones
      • Subtopic: Ozonolysis, Grignard reaction, oxidation, Cannizzaro reaction, aldol condensation, iodoform test
    • Correct answer: (a) resorcinol.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene-1,3-diol is the meta-dihydroxybenzene isomer, and its common name is resorcinol.
    • Ring-position reference
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The compound is benzene-1,3-diol.
      • This means the two \( \mathrm{OH} \) groups are attached at positions 1 and 3 on the benzene ring.
      • Positions 1 and 3 correspond to the meta arrangement.
      • The common name of meta-dihydroxybenzene is resorcinol.
      • So the correct option is (a).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Dihydroxybenzene has three important positional isomers:
        • benzene-1,2-diol \( \rightarrow \) catechol
        • benzene-1,3-diol \( \rightarrow \) resorcinol
        • benzene-1,4-diol \( \rightarrow \) hydroquinone or hydroquinol
      • These names are very commonly asked in short factual MCQs.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) is right because benzene-1,3-diol and resorcinol are the same compound.
    • Why the other options are wrong
      • (b) hydroquinol: This corresponds to benzene-1,4-diol, not benzene-1,3-diol.
      • (c) catechol: This corresponds to benzene-1,2-diol.
      • (d) cresol: Cresol is methylphenol, not dihydroxybenzene.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Remember this simple map:
        • ortho \( \rightarrow \) catechol
        • meta \( \rightarrow \) resorcinol
        • para \( \rightarrow \) hydroquinone
      • So for \(1,3\)-dihydroxybenzene, directly think meta \(\rightarrow\) resorcinol.
    • Final result
      • The common name of benzene-1,3-diol is resorcinol.
      • Hence, the correct option is (a).
    • Chapter / Topic / Subtopic
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Phenols and their nomenclature
      • Subtopic: Common names of dihydroxybenzene isomers
    • Correct answer: (a) \( \mathrm{CH_2{=}C(CH_3)_2} \).
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The starting compound is acetone. Reaction with \( \mathrm{CH_3MgBr} \) gives a tertiary alcohol, which on treatment with \( \mathrm{SOCl_2} \) forms a tertiary alkyl chloride. A tertiary halide does not undergo Williamson ether synthesis efficiently with \( \mathrm{CH_3ONa} \); instead, because of steric hindrance, it undergoes elimination to form the alkene \( \mathrm{CH_2{=}C(CH_3)_2} \).
    • Reaction path shown in the solution image
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting carbonyl compound is acetone, \( \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3} \).
      • Grignard reagent \( \mathrm{CH_3MgBr} \) adds a methyl group to the carbonyl carbon.
      • After acidic hydrolysis, the product \(A\) is a tertiary alcohol: \( \mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH} \), that is tert-butyl alcohol.
      • Now \(A\) reacts with \( \mathrm{SOCl_2} \), which converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides.
      • So \(B\) becomes tert-butyl chloride, \( \mathrm{(CH_3)_3CCl} \).
      • Next, \(B\) is treated with \( \mathrm{CH_3ONa} \) and heat.
      • At first glance, someone may think methoxide will give an ether by substitution.
      • But tert-butyl chloride is a tertiary halide, and tertiary halides are too hindered for normal \( \mathrm{S_N2} \) substitution.
      • In the presence of a strong base and heat, elimination dominates.
      • Removal of \( \beta \)-hydrogen gives the alkene: \( \mathrm{CH_2{=}C(CH_3)_2} \), which is 2-methylpropene.
      • Hence, \(C\) is option (a).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Grignard reaction: Grignard reagents add alkyl groups to carbonyl compounds.
      • Acetone \(+\) \( \mathrm{CH_3MgBr} \) gives a tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
      • \( \mathrm{SOCl_2} \) reaction: Alcohols are converted into alkyl chlorides.
      • Tertiary halide behaviour: Tertiary alkyl halides are highly hindered, so \( \mathrm{S_N2} \) is difficult.
      • With strong base and heating, elimination is favoured, usually by \( \mathrm{E2} \) pathway.
      • This is why the expected product is alkene, not ether.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) represents 2-methylpropene, the alkene formed by dehydrohalogenation of tert-butyl chloride.
      • This is the major product under basic and heated conditions with a tertiary halide.
    • Why the other options are wrong
      • (b) This is not the expected product from the given sequence and does not match the elimination outcome.
      • (c) This corresponds to tert-butyl methyl ether, which would require substitution, but tertiary halides do not favour Williamson-type \( \mathrm{S_N2} \) substitution.
      • (d) Acid chloride formation is unrelated to the given sequence after the Grignard and \( \mathrm{SOCl_2} \) steps.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see:
        • carbonyl \(+\) Grignard \(\rightarrow\) alcohol
        • alcohol \(+\) \( \mathrm{SOCl_2} \) \(\rightarrow\) alkyl chloride
        • tertiary alkyl chloride \(+\) strong base / heat \(\rightarrow\) elimination
      • then quickly think alkene formation.
      • Also remember: Williamson ether synthesis works best with primary alkyl halides, not tertiary ones.
    • Final result
      • \(A = \mathrm{(CH_3)_3COH}\)
      • \(B = \mathrm{(CH_3)_3CCl}\)
      • \(C = \mathrm{CH_2{=}C(CH_3)_2}\)
      • Hence, the correct option is (a).
    • Chapter / Topic / Subtopic
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Topic: Grignard reagent, alcohol conversion, elimination vs substitution
      • Subtopic: Tertiary halides and base-induced elimination
    • Correct answer: (d) III \(>\) I \(>\) IV \(>\) II.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Nucleophilic addition occurs most easily at the most electrophilic and least hindered carbonyl carbon. Formaldehyde is most reactive, then acetaldehyde, then acetone, while the amide is least reactive because resonance donation by \( \mathrm{-NH_2} \) reduces the positive character on the carbonyl carbon.
    • Reactivity comparison shown in the solution image
    • Identify the compounds first
      • I \(=\) ethanal, \( \mathrm{CH_3CHO} \)
      • II \(=\) acetamide, \( \mathrm{CH_3CONH_2} \)
      • III \(=\) methanal, \( \mathrm{HCHO} \)
      • IV \(=\) propanone, \( \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3} \)
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Nucleophilic addition depends mainly on two things:
        • how positive the carbonyl carbon is, and
        • how crowded the carbonyl carbon is.
      • Formaldehyde (III) has no alkyl groups attached.
      • So it has the least steric hindrance and the highest electrophilicity among the usual aldehydes and ketones.
      • Hence, it is the most reactive.
      • Acetaldehyde (I) has one \( \mathrm{CH_3} \) group, so it is slightly less reactive than formaldehyde, but still more reactive than ketones.
      • Acetone (IV) has two \( \mathrm{CH_3} \) groups.
      • These increase electron density by \(+I\) effect and also create more steric hindrance, so ketones are less reactive than aldehydes toward nucleophilic addition.
      • Acetamide (II) is least reactive.
      • In amides, the lone pair on nitrogen is delocalised toward the carbonyl group: \( \mathrm{CH_3CONH_2 \leftrightarrow CH_3C(=NH_2^+)O^-} \).
      • This resonance reduces the electrophilic character of the carbonyl carbon very strongly.
      • So nucleophilic addition becomes much more difficult.
      • Therefore, the correct order is: \( \mathrm{III > I > IV > II} \).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • General trend for nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds: \( \mathrm{HCHO > RCHO > R_2CO} \).
      • Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones because they have:
        • less steric hindrance, and
        • less electron donation to the carbonyl carbon.
      • Amides are much less reactive because resonance stabilisation makes the carbonyl carbon less electron-deficient.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) exactly matches the standard reactivity trend: formaldehyde \(>\) aldehyde \(>\) ketone \(>\) amide.
    • Why the other options are wrong
      • Any option placing acetone above aldehydes is wrong.
      • Any option placing amide above aldehydes or ketones is also wrong, because amides are strongly resonance-stabilised and least reactive here.
      • Option (a) starts with ethanal above formaldehyde, which is incorrect.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorise the quick order: \( \mathrm{HCHO > aldehydes > ketones > amides} \).
      • When comparing aldehydes and ketones: fewer alkyl groups means greater reactivity.
      • When an amide appears in the list, it is usually the least reactive toward nucleophilic addition.
    • Final result
      • The correct order is \( \mathrm{III > I > IV > II} \).
      • Hence, the correct option is (d).
    • Chapter / Topic / Subtopic
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds
      • Subtopic: Reactivity order of aldehydes, ketones and amides
    • Correct answer: (c) unsaturated alcohol, ketone
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: \(C_3H_4\) here is propyne. On hydration in the presence of \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\), it first forms an enol, which is an unsaturated alcohol. That enol then undergoes tautomerisation to give a ketone, namely acetone.
    • Hydration and tautomerisation scheme:
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The formula \(C_3H_4\) can represent propyne in this reaction context.
      • Terminal alkynes on hydration in the presence of \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\) follow Markovnikov addition of water.
      • So propyne \((CH_3-C \equiv CH)\) first forms an enol.
      • The intermediate \(X\) is:
        • \(CH_3-C(OH)=CH_2\)
      • This compound is an unsaturated alcohol because:
        • it contains an \(OH\) group
        • it still contains a double bond
      • Such enols are usually unstable.
      • They undergo keto-enol tautomerisation.
      • So \(CH_3-C(OH)=CH_2\) rearranges to:
        • \(CH_3-CO-CH_3\)
      • This product \(Y\) is acetone, which is a ketone.
      • Therefore:
        • \(X =\) unsaturated alcohol
        • \(Y =\) ketone
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is the hydration of alkynes followed by keto-enol tautomerism.
      • When water adds to an alkyne in acidic medium with \(Hg^{2+}\), the immediate product is not directly the ketone.
      • The first product is an enol.
      • An enol is a compound having:
        • a double bond
        • an \(OH\) group attached to a doubly bonded carbon
      • Enols generally convert to more stable carbonyl compounds.
      • For terminal alkynes except ethyne, Markovnikov hydration gives methyl ketones after tautomerisation.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) correctly states that the first product \(X\) is an unsaturated alcohol and the final product \(Y\) is a ketone.
      • This exactly matches hydration of propyne followed by tautomerisation to acetone.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Option (a) is wrong because the final product is not an aldehyde here.
      • Option (b) is wrong because the first product is not a saturated alcohol; it is an enol, so it is unsaturated.
      • Option (d) is wrong for both reasons.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize this high-frequency rule:
        • Terminal alkyne + \(H_2O\), \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\) \(\rightarrow\) enol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
      • The first species is often an unsaturated alcohol, and the final stable product is usually a carbonyl compound.
      • For propyne specifically, the final product is acetone.
    • Final result:
      • \(X\) is an unsaturated alcohol and \(Y\) is a ketone.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Reactions of alkynes
      • Subtopic: Hydration of terminal alkyne and keto-enol tautomerism
    • Correct answer: (b) \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\)
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: 2-bromopropane first undergoes dehydrohalogenation with alcoholic KOH to form propene \((X)\). Then propene undergoes hydroboration-oxidation with \(B_2H_6\) followed by \(H_2O_2/OH^-\), giving propan-1-ol as the major product \(Y\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Dehydrohalogenation of 2-bromopropane
        • The starting compound is 2-bromopropane: \(CH_3-CHBr-CH_3\).
        • With alcoholic KOH and heat, haloalkanes usually undergo \(\beta\)-elimination.
        • That means one \(H\) and one \(Br\) are removed from adjacent carbon atoms.
        • So the product \(X\) becomes propene:
          • \(CH_3-CHBr-CH_3 \xrightarrow[\Delta]{alc.\ KOH} CH_3-CH=CH_2\)
      • Step 2: Hydroboration-oxidation of propene
        • Now propene reacts with \(B_2H_6\), followed by \(H_2O_2/OH^-\).
        • This reaction adds water across the double bond, but in an anti-Markovnikov manner.
        • That means the \(OH\) group goes to the less substituted carbon atom.
        • So for propene, the product is:
          • \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\)
        • This is propan-1-ol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Alcoholic KOH generally promotes elimination, not substitution, especially on heating.
      • This converts haloalkanes into alkenes.
      • Hydroboration-oxidation is a very important alkene reaction.
      • It gives alcohols with anti-Markovnikov addition.
      • So:
        • Markovnikov hydration of propene would give 2-propanol,
        • but hydroboration-oxidation gives 1-propanol.
      • This difference is exactly what the examiner is testing here.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\), which is propan-1-ol.
      • This is the expected anti-Markovnikov alcohol formed from propene by hydroboration-oxidation.
      • Hence option (b) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_3\) is 2-propanol. This would come from Markovnikov hydration, not hydroboration-oxidation.
      • (c) \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2OH\) is a dihydroxy alcohol structure and is not formed in this sequence.
      • (d) \(HO-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-OH\) is a diol and again not formed here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(B_2H_6\)
        • then \(H_2O_2/OH^-\)
      • immediately think hydroboration-oxidation = anti-Markovnikov alcohol.
      • Also remember:
        • alc. KOH + heat \(\rightarrow\) elimination
        • aq. KOH \(\rightarrow\) substitution
      • This pair of ideas solves many EAPCET organic questions very fast.
    • Final result:
      • The major product \(Y\) is propan-1-ol, \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\).
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Hydrocarbons / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Dehydrohalogenation and hydroboration-oxidation
      • Subtopic: Conversion of haloalkane to alkene and anti-Markovnikov hydration
    • Correct answer: (b) Clemmensen reduction
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene first undergoes Friedel-Crafts acylation with \(CH_3COCl\) and anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) to form acetophenone \((X)\). Then the carbonyl group of acetophenone is reduced to a methylene group in the presence of \(Zn-Hg\) and concentrated \(HCl\), giving ethyl benzene \((Y)\). This reduction is called Clemmensen reduction.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Formation of \(X\)
        • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride \((CH_3COCl)\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\).
        • This is a Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.
        • The acyl group \((CH_3CO-)\) enters the benzene ring.
        • So \(X\) is acetophenone:
          • \(C_6H_6 \xrightarrow{CH_3COCl/Anhy.\ AlCl_3} C_6H_5COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Conversion of \(X\) into \(Y\)
        • Now acetophenone is treated with \(Zn-Hg\) and concentrated \(HCl\).
        • These are the characteristic reagents of Clemmensen reduction.
        • In this reaction, the carbonyl group \((>C=O)\) of aldehydes or ketones is reduced to \(CH_2\).
        • So:
          • \(C_6H_5COCH_3 \xrightarrow{Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl} C_6H_5CH_2CH_3\)
        • Thus \(Y\) is ethyl benzene.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Clemmensen reduction reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons in strongly acidic medium using zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid.
      • It is especially useful when the compound can tolerate acidic conditions.
      • The carbonyl oxygen is effectively removed, and the \(C=O\) becomes \(CH_2\).
      • In aromatic ketones like acetophenone, this converts the side-chain carbonyl compound into an alkylbenzene.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The reagent set \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl\) is the signature of Clemmensen reduction.
      • The reaction converts acetophenone \((X)\) into ethyl benzene \((Y)\) by reducing the ketone group to a methylene group.
      • Hence option (b) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Rosenmund reduction converts acid chlorides into aldehydes using poisoned catalyst, so it does not fit here.
      • Wolff-Kishner reduction also reduces carbonyl group to \(CH_2\), but it uses hydrazine and strong base, not \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl\).
      • Stephen reaction converts nitriles into aldehydes, so it is unrelated here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorise the reagent-reaction pair:
        • \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl \rightarrow\) Clemmensen reduction
        • \(NH_2NH_2/KOH,\ \Delta \rightarrow\) Wolff-Kishner reduction
      • If the medium is acidic, think Clemmensen.
      • If the medium is strongly basic with hydrazine, think Wolff-Kishner.
    • Final result:
      • The conversion of \(X\) into \(Y\) is Clemmensen reduction.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Aromatic substitution and reduction of carbonyl compounds
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts acylation and Clemmensen reduction
    • Correct answer: (b) m-chlorobenzoic acid
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    • Explanation: Benzoic acid first undergoes nitration to give m-nitrobenzoic acid because the \(−CO_2H\) group is meta-directing. The nitro group is then reduced to amino group, forming m-aminobenzoic acid. Finally, diazotisation followed by Sandmeyer reaction replaces the diazonium group by chlorine, giving m-chlorobenzoic acid as the major product \(Z\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Nitration of benzoic acid
        • Benzoic acid is treated with concentrated \(HNO_3\) and concentrated \(H_2SO_4\).
        • This is nitration of an aromatic ring.
        • The \(−CO_2H\) group is strongly electron-withdrawing and is a meta-directing group.
        • So the incoming \(−NO_2\) group enters mainly at the meta position.
        • Thus, \(X\) is m-nitrobenzoic acid.
      • Step 2: Reduction of nitro group
        • \(Sn/HCl\) reduces aromatic nitro group \((−NO_2)\) to amino group \((−NH_2)\).
        • So \(X\) is converted into m-aminobenzoic acid.
        • Thus, \(Y\) is m-aminobenzoic acid.
      • Step 3: Diazotisation
        • \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at \(0^\circ C\) converts the aromatic amine into a diazonium salt.
        • So the \(−NH_2\) group is converted into \(−N_2^+Cl^-\).
      • Step 4: Sandmeyer reaction
        • Treatment with \(Cu_2Cl_2/HCl\) replaces the diazonium group by chlorine.
        • So the final product \(Z\) becomes m-chlorobenzoic acid.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines three very important aromatic chemistry ideas:
        • directive influence in electrophilic substitution,
        • reduction of nitro group to amino group,
        • diazotisation and Sandmeyer substitution.
      • The \(−COOH\) group is deactivating and meta-directing because it withdraws electron density from the ring.
      • That is why nitration does not mainly give ortho or para product here.
      • Then \(−NO_2\) can be reduced to \(−NH_2\), and aromatic amines can be converted to diazonium salts at low temperature.
      • Diazonium salts are highly useful intermediates because the diazonium group can be replaced by \(Cl\), \(Br\), \(CN\), \(OH\), etc.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The carboxyl group fixes the incoming nitro group at meta position in the first step.
      • That meta position is retained throughout the sequence.
      • After reduction and diazotisation, chlorine finally occupies that same meta position.
      • So the final product is m-chlorobenzoic acid.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) m-nitrobenzoic acid is only the first intermediate \(X\), not the final product \(Z\).
      • (c) m-aminobenzoic acid is the second intermediate \(Y\), not the final product.
      • (d) m-chlorobenzoyl chloride is wrong because the \(−COOH\) group is never converted into \(−COCl\) anywhere in the sequence.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Keep this reaction flow in memory:
        • \(Ar-NO_2 \xrightarrow{Sn/HCl} Ar-NH_2\)
        • \(Ar-NH_2 \xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCl,\ 0^\circ C} Ar-N_2^+Cl^-\)
        • \(Ar-N_2^+Cl^- \xrightarrow{Cu_2Cl_2/HCl} Ar-Cl\)
      • Also remember that \(−COOH\) is a classic meta director.
      • Those two facts almost solve the whole question instantly.
    • Final result:
      • The major product \(Z\) is m-chlorobenzoic acid.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Amines / Hydrocarbons / Haloarenes
      • Topic: Aromatic substitution, reduction, diazotisation and Sandmeyer reaction
      • Subtopic: Reaction sequence involving benzoic acid derivatives

    2022
    • Correct answer: (a) \(P\): isopropyl benzene, \(Q\): benzyl alcohol
    • Explanation: In reaction \(A\), benzene reacts with \(n\)-propyl chloride in the presence of \(AlCl_3\), but the initially formed primary carbocation rearranges to the more stable secondary carbocation, giving isopropyl benzene as the major Friedel–Crafts alkylation product. In reaction \(B\), benzene first forms toluene, then side-chain bromination gives benzyl bromide, and finally aqueous \(KOH\) converts it into benzyl alcohol.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Reaction \(A\):
        • Benzene is treated with \(CH_3CH_2CH_2Cl\) in the presence of \(AlCl_3\).
        • This is a Friedel–Crafts alkylation reaction.
        • At first, one may think the product should be \(n\)-propyl benzene.
        • But the electrophile generated from \(n\)-propyl chloride is initially a primary carbocation-like species, which is unstable.
        • It undergoes a \(1,2\)-hydride shift to form the more stable secondary carbocation:
          • \(CH_3 - CH^+ - CH_3\)
        • Benzene then attacks this secondary carbocation.
        • So the major product \(P\) is isopropyl benzene, also called cumene.
      • Reaction \(B\):
        • Step (i): \(CH_3Cl/AlCl_3\) converts benzene into toluene by Friedel–Crafts methylation.
        • Step (ii): \(Br_2, h\nu\) causes free-radical side-chain bromination, not ring bromination.
        • So toluene gives benzyl bromide \((C_6H_5CH_2Br)\).
        • Step (iii): Aqueous \(KOH\) hydrolyses benzyl bromide to benzyl alcohol \((C_6H_5CH_2OH)\).
        • Thus, the major product \(Q\) is benzyl alcohol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines three core ideas from aromatic chemistry:
        • Friedel–Crafts alkylation
        • carbocation rearrangement
        • benzylic free-radical bromination followed by nucleophilic substitution
      • In Friedel–Crafts alkylation, if a rearrangement can give a more stable carbocation, the rearranged product often becomes the major product.
      • Under \(Br_2/h\nu\), alkyl benzenes usually undergo substitution at the benzylic side chain because benzylic radicals are highly stabilized.
      • Benzyl halides then react readily with aqueous \(KOH\) to form benzyl alcohol.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because:
        • \(P\) is isopropyl benzene due to rearrangement in Friedel–Crafts alkylation.
        • \(Q\) is benzyl alcohol after methylation, benzylic bromination, and hydrolysis.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options giving unrearranged propyl product for \(P\) are wrong because the secondary carbocation is more stable and dominates.
      • Options giving ketone, benzhydrol, or benzoic acid for \(Q\) are inconsistent with the reagent sequence shown.
      • \(Br_2/h\nu\) followed by aqueous \(KOH\) points toward benzylic bromination followed by substitution to alcohol, not oxidation or acylation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For \(RCl/AlCl_3\) on benzene, always ask:
        • Can the carbocation rearrange to a more stable one?
      • For \(Br_2/h\nu\) on an alkyl benzene, think:
        • benzylic bromination
      • Then:
        • benzyl halide + aqueous \(KOH\) \(\rightarrow\) benzyl alcohol
    • Final result:
      • The major products are \(P =\) isopropyl benzene and \(Q =\) benzyl alcohol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Aromatic hydrocarbons, Friedel–Crafts alkylation, benzylic bromination and hydrolysis
    • Correct answer: (a) 3-hexanone
    • Explanation: Propene first gives \(n\)-propyl bromide by anti-Markovnikov addition of \(HBr\) in the presence of peroxide. This is then converted to propyl magnesium bromide, which reacts with propanal to give hexan-3-ol after hydrolysis. Oxidation of this secondary alcohol with \(CrO_3\) gives 3-hexanone.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step (i): Reaction of propene with \(HBr\) in the presence of peroxide
        • This is the peroxide effect or Kharasch effect.
        • \(HBr\) adds by anti-Markovnikov rule.
        • So propene gives \(n\)-propyl bromide:
          • \(CH_3CH = CH_2 \xrightarrow{HBr,\ peroxide} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br\)
      • Step (ii): Reaction with magnesium
        • Alkyl bromide reacts with \(Mg\) in dry ether to form Grignard reagent.
        • So:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2Br \xrightarrow{Mg} CH_3CH_2CH_2MgBr\)
        • This is propyl magnesium bromide.
      • Step (iii): Reaction with propanal \((CH_3CH_2CHO)\)
        • Grignard reagent adds to the carbonyl carbon of aldehyde.
        • Propyl group \((C_3H_7)\) adds to propanal \((C_3)\).
        • After hydrolysis, the product is:
          • hexan-3-ol
        • Structure:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH_2CH_2CH_3\)
      • Step (iv): Oxidation with \(CrO_3\)
        • Hexan-3-ol is a secondary alcohol.
        • Secondary alcohol on oxidation gives ketone.
        • So:
          • hexan-3-ol \(\rightarrow\) hexan-3-one
      • Hence, the final major product is 3-hexanone.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines four standard organic chemistry ideas:
        • peroxide effect in \(HBr\) addition
        • formation of Grignard reagent
        • addition of Grignard reagent to aldehyde
        • oxidation of secondary alcohol to ketone
      • Whenever \(HBr\) is used with peroxide, remember that only \(HBr\) shows the peroxide effect among hydrogen halides in this standard syllabus context.
      • Grignard reagent adds carbon chain to carbonyl compounds, increasing carbon count.
      • Aldehyde other than formaldehyde gives secondary alcohol on reaction with Grignard reagent.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because the sequence leads to hexan-3-ol first, and oxidation of that secondary alcohol gives 3-hexanone.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (b) 2-hexanone would require the \(OH\) group to be on carbon-2 before oxidation, which is not the case here.
      • Option (c) 2-methyl-3-pentanone would require a branched carbon skeleton, but the chain formed here is straight.
      • Option (d) hexanal would come from oxidation of a primary alcohol, not a secondary alcohol.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In multistep organic questions, track the carbon skeleton carefully.
      • Here:
        • propene \(\rightarrow\) propyl bromide \(\rightarrow\) propyl Grignard
        • propyl Grignard + propanal \(\rightarrow\) \(3 + 3 = 6\) carbon alcohol
      • Then ask:
        • Is the alcohol primary, secondary, or tertiary?
      • That immediately tells the oxidation product.
    • Final result:
      • The major product formed is 3-hexanone.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Subtopic: Peroxide effect, Grignard reagent, aldehyde addition and oxidation of alcohols
    • Correct answer: (d) p-bromophenol
    • Explanation: Cumene on oxidation with oxygen followed by acidic hydrolysis gives phenol and acetone. Phenol is an ortho/para directing group. Bromination in non-polar solvent \((CHCl_3)\) at low temperature gives mainly mono-substitution, and para substitution dominates over ortho because the para product is less hindered. Hence the major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step (i) and (ii): Oxidation of cumene followed by acidic treatment
        • Cumene \((isopropylbenzene)\) reacts with oxygen to form cumene hydroperoxide.
        • On acidic hydrolysis, cumene hydroperoxide cleaves to give:
          • phenol
          • acetone
        • This is the well-known cumene process.
      • So after the first two steps, the aromatic compound of interest is phenol.
      • Step (iii): Bromination with \(Br_2\) in \(CHCl_3\) at \(273\ \text{K}\)
        • The \(-OH\) group in phenol is strongly activating and ortho/para directing.
        • However, the conditions matter a lot.
        • In a non-polar solvent like \(CHCl_3\) and at low temperature, bromination is controlled and mainly mono-bromination occurs.
        • Both ortho and para products are formed.
        • But para product is major because ortho substitution suffers more steric hindrance near the \(-OH\) group.
      • Therefore, the major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines:
        • cumene process for phenol manufacture
        • directive influence of phenol in electrophilic substitution
        • effect of solvent and conditions on bromination
      • Phenol is strongly activating due to resonance donation from oxygen.
      • So it directs incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions.
      • Under aqueous bromine conditions, phenol can give 2,4,6-tribromophenol easily.
      • But under controlled non-polar solvent conditions, mono-bromination predominates, and para is major.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because phenol formed in the earlier step undergoes bromination in \(CHCl_3\) at low temperature to give mainly p-bromophenol.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) 2,4,6-tribromophenol is formed typically with bromine water, not under these controlled non-polar conditions.
      • Option (b) 2,4-dibromophenol is not the major product under the given conditions.
      • Option (c) o-bromophenol is formed only as minor product because of steric hindrance.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • If you see cumene + \(O_2\) + acid, immediately think:
        • phenol + acetone
      • Then for phenol bromination:
        • bromine water \(\rightarrow\) tribromophenol
        • \(Br_2\) in non-polar solvent at low temperature \(\rightarrow\) mono-bromination, para major
    • Final result:
      • The major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Phenols
      • Subtopic: Cumene process and bromination of phenol
    • Correct answer: (c) \(I > IV > II > III\)
    • Explanation: In acid-catalysed esterification, the alcohol acts as a nucleophile and attacks the protonated carboxylic acid. As steric bulk around the attacking \(-OH\) containing carbon chain increases, nucleophilicity and ease of attack decrease. Therefore, the least hindered alcohol reacts fastest and the most hindered alcohol reacts slowest. Hence the decreasing reactivity order is \(I > IV > II > III\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • In Fischer esterification, acetic acid first gets protonated.
      • Then the alcohol oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
      • So the alcohol behaves as a nucleophile in the key step.
      • Therefore, anything that makes the alcohol oxygen approach the carbonyl carbon more difficult will reduce the rate.
      • Now compare the alcohols:
        • \(I = CH_3CH_2OH\) (ethanol)
        • \(IV = CH_3CH_2CH_2OH\) (1-propanol)
        • \(II = (CH_3)_2CHCH_2OH\)
        • \(III\) is the most bulky among the given alcohols
      • The more bulky the alkyl group near the reacting \(-OH\), the lower the nucleophilic reactivity towards esterification.
      • So:
        • \(I\) is least hindered and reacts fastest.
        • \(IV\) is slightly bulkier than \(I\), so next.
        • \(II\) has branching and is more hindered, so slower.
        • \(III\) is the most hindered, so slowest.
      • Hence the order is:
        • \(I > IV > II > III\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Esterification of a carboxylic acid with an alcohol is a nucleophilic acyl substitution process under acidic conditions.
      • The alcohol must approach and attack the protonated carbonyl carbon.
      • If the alcohol is bulky, this approach becomes more difficult.
      • So steric hindrance lowers the reactivity of alcohol towards esterification.
      • This is why straight-chain, less hindered alcohols generally react more easily than branched bulky alcohols.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because steric hindrance increases in the order \(I < IV < II < III\), so reactivity decreases in the order \(I > IV > II > III\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) incorrectly places \(II\) above \(IV\), even though \(II\) is more hindered.
      • Option (b) is the reverse of the actual order.
      • Option (d) incorrectly places \(III\) above \(II\), though \(III\) is more bulky and therefore less reactive.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For esterification, think:
        • alcohol is the nucleophile
        • more bulk \(\rightarrow\) harder attack \(\rightarrow\) lower reactivity
      • So among isomeric alcohols, the least hindered one usually reacts fastest.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of reactivity towards esterification with acetic acid is \(I > IV > II > III\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Subtopic: Esterification and effect of steric hindrance on alcohol reactivity
    • Correct answer: (d) \(-CN\) to \(-CH_3\) and \(sp\) to \(sp^3\)
    • Explanation: The nitrile group \(( -CN )\) first undergoes Stephen reduction to give the corresponding aldehyde after hydrolysis. Then the aldehyde is further reduced by \(HI/Red\ P\) to an alkane. So overall, the \(-CN\) group gets converted into \(-CH_3\). Since carbon in nitrile is \(sp\)-hybridised and carbon in methyl group is \(sp^3\)-hybridised, the hybridisation changes from \(sp\) to \(sp^3\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is acetonitrile:
        • \(CH_3C \equiv N\)
      • Step (i): \(SnCl_2/HCl\)
        • This is Stephen reduction condition for nitriles.
        • Nitrile is partially reduced to an iminium salt intermediate.
      • Step (ii): \(H_3O^+\)
        • Hydrolysis of that intermediate gives the corresponding aldehyde.
        • So:
          • \(CH_3CN \rightarrow CH_3CHO\)
      • Step (iii): \(HI + Red\ P\)
        • This is a strong reducing system.
        • It reduces aldehydes further to hydrocarbons.
        • So:
          • \(CH_3CHO \rightarrow CH_3CH_3\)
      • Therefore, looking only at the changing functional part:
        • \(-CN \rightarrow -CH_3\)
      • Now hybridisation:
        • Carbon of nitrile \((C \equiv N)\) is \(sp\)-hybridised.
        • Carbon of methyl group \(( -CH_3 )\) is \(sp^3\)-hybridised.
      • Hence the required change is:
        • \(sp \rightarrow sp^3\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines two standard ideas:
        • Stephen reaction of nitriles
        • strong reduction by \(HI/Red\ P\)
      • Stephen reaction converts:
        • \(R-CN \rightarrow R-CHO\)
      • Then \(HI/Red\ P\) can reduce oxygen-containing groups further to hydrocarbon level.
      • Nitrile carbon is linear and \(sp\)-hybridised because of triple bond.
      • Methyl carbon is tetrahedral and \(sp^3\)-hybridised.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because the nitrile group finally becomes a methyl group, and the hybridisation changes from \(sp\) to \(sp^3\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) is wrong because no alcohol is formed finally.
      • Option (b) is wrong because amide is not the final product here.
      • Option (c) is wrong because amine is also not the final product under these conditions.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Remember:
        • Stephen reduction: nitrile \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • \(HI/Red\ P\): aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) alkane
      • So overall in this sequence:
        • \(-CN \rightarrow -CH_3\)
    • Final result:
      • The correct change is \(-CN\) to \(-CH_3\) and \(sp\) to \(sp^3\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Amines / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Subtopic: Stephen reduction and functional group interconversion
    • Correct answer: (c) A and C only
    • Explanation: Benzophenone is obtained from benzoyl chloride by Friedel–Crafts acylation with benzene, and also by reaction with diphenyl cadmium, which stops at ketone formation. But benzoyl chloride with excess phenylmagnesium bromide does not stop at ketone stage; the ketone formed first reacts further with the excess Grignard reagent to give a tertiary alcohol. So only A and C give benzophenone as major product.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us examine each reaction separately.
      • (A) Benzoyl chloride + benzene + \(AlCl_3\)
        • This is Friedel–Crafts acylation.
        • Benzoyl chloride generates the benzoyl electrophile.
        • Benzene attacks it and forms:
          • \(C_6H_5COC_6H_5\)
        • That product is benzophenone.
      • (B) Benzoyl chloride + phenylmagnesium bromide (excess)
        • First addition can indeed give ketone-like intermediate.
        • But Grignard reagent is very reactive.
        • Since it is present in excess, it attacks the ketone again.
        • After hydrolysis, the final major product becomes a tertiary alcohol, not benzophenone.
        • So benzophenone is not the major final product here.
      • (C) Benzoyl chloride + diphenyl cadmium
        • Organocadmium reagents are less reactive than Grignard reagents.
        • They react with acid chlorides and stop at ketone stage.
        • Hence benzophenone is formed as major product.
      • Therefore, the correct reactions are:
        • A and C only
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question is about methods of ketone preparation.
      • Three important reactions must be distinguished:
        • Friedel–Crafts acylation gives aryl ketones.
        • Grignard reagents usually do not stop at ketone stage with acid chlorides if used in excess.
        • Organocadmium reagents are milder and therefore are useful for ketone synthesis from acid chlorides.
      • This is a classic exam trap because many students think any aryl reagent with acid chloride gives ketone directly.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because reaction A and reaction C both give benzophenone, while reaction B proceeds further to tertiary alcohol due to excess Grignard reagent.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) is wrong because B does not give benzophenone as major final product.
      • Option (b) is wrong because B is again incorrect, while A is actually correct.
      • Option (d) is wrong because all three do not give benzophenone as major product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Remember:
        • acid chloride + benzene/\(AlCl_3\) \(\rightarrow\) ketone
