        • acid chloride + organocadmium \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • acid chloride + excess Grignard \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol
      • That rule alone solves this question fast.
    • Final result:
      • Benzophenone is formed as major product in reactions A and C only.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Preparation of ketones from acid chlorides and Friedel–Crafts acylation
    • Correct answer: (b) \(B_2H_6 / H_3O^+\)
    • Explanation: Carboxylic acids are reduced to primary alcohols by diborane \((B_2H_6)\), which acts through \(BH_3\) in ether or THF medium. After acidic workup, the corresponding alcohol is obtained. The other reagents listed either do not reduce carboxylic acids or are meant for different types of reductions.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The functional group given is carboxylic acid:
        • \(RCOOH\)
      • The required product is corresponding alcohol:
        • \(RCH_2OH\)
      • Now check each reagent.
      • (a) \(NaBH_4 / H_3O^+\)
        • \(NaBH_4\) is a mild reducing agent.
        • It reduces aldehydes and ketones easily.
        • But it does not normally reduce carboxylic acids.
      • (b) \(B_2H_6 / H_3O^+\)
        • Diborane provides \(BH_3\), which can reduce carboxylic acids efficiently.
        • After hydrolysis/workup, the product becomes the corresponding primary alcohol.
      • (c) \(Zn-Hg / conc.\ HCl\)
        • This is Clemmensen reduction condition.
        • It reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons.
        • It is not used to reduce carboxylic acids to alcohols.
      • (d) \(H_2, Pd/C\)
        • This is catalytic hydrogenation, more commonly used for multiple bonds such as \(C=C\), \(C \equiv C\), and related reducible groups under suitable conditions.
        • It is not the standard reagent here for converting carboxylic acid to alcohol.
      • Therefore, the correct reagent is \(B_2H_6 / H_3O^+\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is a reagent-recognition question.
      • For carboxylic acids, strong hydride-type reducing agents are needed.
      • Diborane is especially useful because \(BH_3\) complexes well with the oxygen atoms of the carboxylic acid and enables reduction to alcohol.
      • This is a very standard distinction from \(NaBH_4\), which is too mild for carboxylic acids.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because diborane reduces carboxylic acids to the corresponding primary alcohols after acidic workup.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) is wrong because \(NaBH_4\) does not reduce carboxylic acids under normal conditions.
      • Option (c) is wrong because Clemmensen reduction is for aldehydes and ketones, not carboxylic acids.
      • Option (d) is not the standard reagent for this transformation in this syllabus context.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Quick memory rule:
        • \(NaBH_4\): aldehydes, ketones
        • \(B_2H_6\) or \(BH_3\): carboxylic acids to alcohols
      • So if carboxylic acid appears in options, diborane should stand out immediately.
    • Final result:
      • The reagent is \(B_2H_6 / H_3O^+\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Reduction of carboxylic acids
    • Correct answer: (d) \(C_6H_{10}O\)
    • Explanation: Propyne on hydration with \(HgSO_4\) / dilute \(H_2SO_4\) gives acetone \((P)\). Acetone, when heated with \(Ba(OH)_2\), undergoes aldol condensation to form an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone. The final product \(Q\) has molecular formula \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Hydration of propyne
        • Propyne is:
          • \(CH_3-C \equiv CH\)
        • With \(HgSO_4\) / dilute \(H_2SO_4\), terminal alkynes undergo Markovnikov hydration.
        • The enol formed tautomerises to ketone.
        • So product \(P\) is:
          • acetone, \(CH_3COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Reaction of acetone with \(Ba(OH)_2\)
        • \(Ba(OH)_2\) acts as a base and promotes aldol reaction.
        • Two molecules of acetone combine first to form diacetone alcohol.
        • On heating, dehydration occurs and an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone is formed.
        • The product is mesityl oxide:
          • \(CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2\)
      • Now calculate molecular formula of this product:
        • Number of carbons \(= 6\)
        • Number of hydrogens \(= 10\)
        • Number of oxygens \(= 1\)
      • Therefore:
        • \(Q = C_6H_{10}O\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines:
        • hydration of terminal alkyne
        • keto-enol tautomerism
        • aldol condensation of ketone
      • Terminal alkyne with \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\) generally gives a ketone after tautomerism.
      • Acetone under basic conditions undergoes self-aldol condensation, and on heating gives an unsaturated ketone.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because acetone formed in the first step gives an aldol condensation product having formula \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • \(C_3H_6O\) corresponds to acetone itself, which is only intermediate \(P\), not final \(Q\).
      • \(C_3H_8O\) is an alcohol formula, not correct here.
      • \(C_6H_{12}O_2\) would correspond to the addition product before dehydration, not the heated condensation product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Remember:
        • propyne + \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\) \(\rightarrow\) acetone
      • Then:
        • acetone + base + heat \(\rightarrow\) aldol condensation product (unsaturated ketone)
      • If the question asks final product after heating, do not stop at the aldol addition stage.
    • Final result:
      • The molecular formula of product \(Q\) is \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Topic: Hydration of alkynes and aldol condensation
      • Subtopic: Conversion of propyne to acetone and self-condensation of acetone
    • Correct answer: (b)
    • Explanation: The starting compound is salicylic acid, that is, \(o\)-hydroxy benzoic acid. With soda lime it undergoes decarboxylation to give phenol. Phenol on heating with zinc dust gives benzene. Salicylic acid with bromine water undergoes bromination at positions activated by the \(-OH\) group to give 3,5-dibromosalicylic acid. On heating around \(200{-}230^\circ\text{C}\), salicylic acid decarboxylates to phenol. So the set in option (b) is correct.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound shown is salicylic acid:
        • \(o\)-hydroxy benzoic acid
      • Reaction towards \([A]\): soda lime, \(\Delta\)
        • Soda lime causes decarboxylation of aromatic carboxylic acids.
        • So the \(-COOH\) group is removed.
        • Salicylic acid therefore gives phenol.
        • Hence, \([A] =\) phenol.
      • Reaction from \([A]\) to \([B]\): zinc dust
        • Phenol on distillation with zinc dust loses oxygen functionality.
        • It is converted into benzene.
        • Hence, \([B] =\) benzene.
      • Reaction towards \([C]\): \(\mathrm{Br_2/H_2O}\)
        • The \(-OH\) group strongly activates the benzene ring and directs substitution to ortho and para positions.
        • But one ortho position already contains \(-COOH\).
        • The \(-COOH\) group is deactivating and meta-directing.
        • The positions that become most suitable are 3 and 5 relative to the given structure.
        • So bromination gives 3,5-dibromosalicylic acid.
        • Hence, \([C] =\) 3,5-dibromosalicylic acid.
      • Reaction towards \([D]\): \(200{-}230^\circ\text{C}\)
        • Salicylic acid undergoes decarboxylation on heating in this range.
        • So the product again is phenol.
        • Hence, \([D] =\) phenol.
      • Therefore:
        • \([A] =\) phenol
        • \([B] =\) benzene
        • \([C] =\) 3,5-dibromosalicylic acid
        • \([D] =\) phenol
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines several standard reactions of phenols and aromatic carboxylic acids:
        • decarboxylation by soda lime
        • reduction of phenol to benzene by zinc dust
        • electrophilic bromination of activated aromatic rings
        • thermal decarboxylation of salicylic acid
      • Salicylic acid is a very common exam compound because it contains both:
        • an activating \(-OH\) group
        • a deactivating \(-COOH\) group
      • So orientation of substitution becomes a good test of directing effects.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) correctly matches all four products from the given reaction conditions.
      • Every transformation in that option follows standard organic chemistry reactions.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) is wrong because \([C]\) is not 2,4,6-tribromophenol. The starting compound is salicylic acid, not plain phenol.
      • (c) is wrong because \([D]\) is not the alternative ring structure shown there; heating salicylic acid gives phenol.
      • (d) is wrong because \([A]\) and \([B]\) are incorrectly assigned. Soda lime gives phenol, and zinc dust converts phenol to benzene.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize these standard pairs:
        • aromatic \(-COOH\) + soda lime \(\rightarrow\) ring minus \(-COOH\)
        • phenol + zinc dust \(\rightarrow\) benzene
        • phenolic ring + bromine water \(\rightarrow\) easy bromination
      • If the starting compound already has \(-COOH\), do not jump to tribromophenol directly.
    • Final result:
      • The correct set is:
        • \([A] =\) phenol
        • \([B] =\) benzene
        • \([C] =\) 3,5-dibromosalicylic acid
        • \([D] =\) phenol
      • Hence, the correct option is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aromatic carboxylic acid reactions
      • Topic: Reactions of phenol and salicylic acid
      • Subtopic: Decarboxylation, bromination and zinc dust reduction
    • Correct answer: (d) A and C
    • Explanation: Statement (A) is true because phenol is more acidic than alcohol due to resonance stabilization of phenoxide ion. Statement (C) is also true because phenol gives a violet colour with neutral ferric chloride. Statement (B) is false because phenol is used in bakelite, not melamine plastic. Statement (D) is false because phenol reacts with acetyl chloride to form phenyl ethanoate, not phenetole.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us check each statement one by one.
      • (A) In general, phenol is more acidic than alcohol.
        • This is true.
        • When phenol loses \(H^+\), it forms phenoxide ion.
        • The negative charge on phenoxide ion is stabilized by resonance over the aromatic ring.
        • Ordinary alkoxide ions from alcohols do not get this kind of resonance stabilization.
        • So phenol is more acidic than alcohol.
      • (B) Phenol is used in the production of melamine plastic.
        • This is false.
        • Phenol is used for making bakelite, which is a phenol-formaldehyde resin.
        • Melamine plastic is based on melamine-formaldehyde, not phenol.
      • (C) Phenol gives violet colour with neutral ferric chloride solution.
        • This is true.
        • Phenols give characteristic coloured complexes with neutral \(\mathrm{FeCl_3}\).
        • For phenol, the colour is violet.
      • (D) Phenol when heated with acetyl chloride gives phenetole.
        • This is false.
        • Phenol reacts with acetyl chloride by acylation, not by ethylation.
        • The product formed is phenyl ethanoate:
          • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OH + CH_3COCl \rightarrow C_6H_5OCOCH_3 + HCl}\)
        • Phenetole is ethoxybenzene, which is a different compound.
      • Hence the correct statements are A and C only.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question mixes physical property, qualitative test, industrial use, and reaction chemistry of phenol.
      • Important facts about phenol:
        • more acidic than alcohol due to resonance stabilization
        • gives violet colour with neutral \(\mathrm{FeCl_3}\)
        • used in phenol-formaldehyde resin, that is bakelite
        • undergoes acylation to form esters such as phenyl ethanoate
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Only option (d) contains the two correct statements, A and C.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) C and D is wrong because D is false.
      • (b) A and D is wrong because D is false.
      • (c) B and C is wrong because B is false.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize this quick phenol set:
        • phenol \(>\) alcohol in acidity
        • neutral \(\mathrm{FeCl_3}\) test gives violet colour
        • phenol + formaldehyde \(\rightarrow\) bakelite
        • phenol + acetyl chloride \(\rightarrow\) ester, not ether
      • That is enough to solve this whole question in under a minute.
    • Final result:
      • The correct statements are A and C.
      • Hence, the correct option is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Properties and reactions of phenol
      • Subtopic: Acidity, ferric chloride test, uses and acylation
    • Correct answer: (d)
    • Explanation: Dialkyl cadmium reagents are mild organometallic reagents. They react with acid chlorides to give ketones, but they do not usually attack the ketone further like very reactive Grignard reagents do. So only the acid chloride group is converted, while the already present ketone group remains unchanged.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The given compound contains two important carbonyl functionalities:
        • one acid chloride group \((\mathrm{-COCl})\)
        • one ketone group \((\mathrm{-COCH_3})\)
      • The reagent used is \(\mathrm{(C_2H_5)_2Cd}\), that is dialkyl cadmium.
      • Dialkyl cadmium is known for one very specific reaction:
        • \(\mathrm{RCOCl \rightarrow RCOR'}\)
      • So here, the chlorine of the acid chloride is replaced by an ethyl group.
      • That means the left-side \(\mathrm{-COCl}\) becomes \(\mathrm{-COC_2H_5}\).
      • The ketone already present on the right side does not get converted into alcohol under these conditions.
      • Therefore, the product is the compound where:
        • acid chloride has changed into ketone
        • the original ketone remains unchanged
      • This matches option (d).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Organometallic reagents behave differently depending on their reactivity.
      • Very reactive reagents like Grignard reagents often add twice to acid chlorides, finally giving tertiary alcohols after hydrolysis.
      • But dialkyl cadmium is much milder.
      • It stops at the ketone stage.
      • This makes it a standard reagent for converting acid chlorides selectively into ketones.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) alone shows replacement of \(\mathrm{Cl}\) in the acid chloride by \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\), producing a ketone.
      • It also correctly leaves the other ketone group untouched.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) and (b) show over-addition to carbonyl groups to form alcohols, which is not the characteristic behaviour of dialkyl cadmium here.
      • (c) changes the acid chloride side too far while leaving the wrong functionality pattern.
      • These are more like outcomes expected with stronger organometallic reagents, not \(\mathrm{R_2Cd}\).
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize this direct rule:
        • acid chloride + \(\mathrm{R_2Cd}\) \(\rightarrow\) ketone
      • If you see alcohol formation in the options, be cautious. That usually suggests over-addition, which dialkyl cadmium generally does not do.
    • Final result:
      • The major product is the ketone formed by replacing \(\mathrm{Cl}\) of the acid chloride with \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\).
      • Hence, the correct option is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Reactions of acid chlorides
      • Subtopic: Selective preparation of ketones using organocadmium reagents
    • Correct answer: (a)
    • Explanation: The reagent sequence \((i)\ \mathrm{NH_2NH_2}\) and \((ii)\ \mathrm{KOH,\ ethylene\ glycol,\ \Delta}\) is the Wolff-Kishner reduction. It reduces the carbonyl group \((\mathrm{C=O})\) of a ketone to a methylene group \((\mathrm{-CH_2-})\). So the cyclic ketone becomes the corresponding cycloalkane derivative, giving option (a).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is a cyclopentanone derivative with two methyl groups on the same ring carbon.
      • Step 1: Reaction with hydrazine \((\mathrm{NH_2NH_2})\)
        • The ketone first forms a hydrazone.
      • Step 2: Heating with \(\mathrm{KOH}\) in ethylene glycol
        • This is the Wolff-Kishner reduction condition.
        • The hydrazone loses nitrogen and finally the carbonyl carbon becomes \(\mathrm{CH_2}\).
      • So:
        • \(\mathrm{C=O}\) of the cyclopentanone is reduced to \(\mathrm{CH_2}\)
        • the ring remains intact
        • the two methyl groups remain where they are
      • Therefore the product is dimethyl-substituted cyclopentane shown in option (a).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Wolff-Kishner reduction is a standard method to reduce:
        • aldehydes \(\rightarrow\) alkanes
        • ketones \(\rightarrow\) alkanes
      • It proceeds through hydrazone formation and then removal of nitrogen under strongly basic high-temperature conditions.
      • It is essentially the opposite functional idea of oxidation of alcohol to carbonyl, but here we go all the way from carbonyl to hydrocarbon.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) correctly shows complete reduction of the ketone carbonyl into a methylene group without changing the ring framework or the methyl substituents.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (b) shows an alkene, but Wolff-Kishner reduction does not create a double bond here.
      • (c) shows an alcohol, but these reagents do not reduce ketone to alcohol.
      • (d) shows a dimeric diol-type product, which is unrelated to Wolff-Kishner conditions.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(\mathrm{NH_2NH_2}\), then \(\mathrm{KOH}\), heat
      • immediately think:
        • Wolff-Kishner reduction
        • \(\mathrm{C=O \rightarrow CH_2}\)
      • So eliminate alcohol and alkene options first.
    • Final result:
      • The major product is the reduced cycloalkane shown in option (a).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Reduction of carbonyl compounds
      • Subtopic: Wolff-Kishner reduction
    • Correct answer: (c) butanoic acid
    • Explanation: \(n\)-Propanol first converts into \(n\)-propyl bromide with concentrated HBr. Then KCN substitutes bromine to form a nitrile, which adds one extra carbon atom. Finally, acidic hydrolysis of the nitrile gives the corresponding carboxylic acid. So the final product is butanoic acid.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: \(n\)-propanol with concentrated HBr
        • Alcohols react with hydrogen halides to form alkyl halides.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow{HBr} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\)
        • This product is \(P\), that is \(n\)-propyl bromide.
      • Step 2: Reaction of \(P\) with KCN
        • \(\mathrm{CN^-}\) substitutes bromine.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br \xrightarrow{KCN} CH_3CH_2CH_2CN}\)
        • This product is butanenitrile.
        • Notice that carbon count increases by one because the carbon of \(\mathrm{-CN}\) becomes part of the chain.
      • Step 3: Acidic hydrolysis of nitrile
        • Nitriles on heating with aqueous acidic solution hydrolyse to carboxylic acids.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CN \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ aq.\ acidic} CH_3CH_2CH_2COOH}\)
        • This is butanoic acid.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is a classic chain-extension sequence in organic chemistry.
      • Main ideas:
        • alcohol \(\rightarrow\) alkyl halide
        • alkyl halide + KCN \(\rightarrow\) nitrile
        • nitrile hydrolysis \(\rightarrow\) carboxylic acid
      • The most important memory point is:
        • KCN increases the carbon chain by one
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Starting from 3-carbon \(n\)-propanol, the nitrile step gives a 4-carbon compound, and hydrolysis converts it into the 4-carbon acid, that is butanoic acid.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) propanoic acid would have no carbon chain increase, so it is wrong.
      • (b) propanamide is neither the correct chain length nor the final acidic hydrolysis product here.
      • (d) butanamide can appear as an intermediate idea during controlled hydrolysis, but final heating with aqueous acidic solution gives the acid, not the amide.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • alkyl halide \(\rightarrow\) KCN \(\rightarrow\) hydrolysis
      • think:
        • add one carbon
        • final product = carboxylic acid
      • So 3-carbon alcohol finally gives 4-carbon acid.
    • Final result:
      • The product \(R\) is butanoic acid.
      • Hence, the correct option is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Organic conversions
      • Topic: Cyanide substitution and hydrolysis
      • Subtopic: One-carbon chain extension using KCN
    • Correct answer: (d) III > IV > II > I
    • Explanation: Acid-mediated dehydration of alcohols generally proceeds through carbocation formation for secondary and tertiary alcohols. So the rate depends on carbocation stability. Since tertiary carbocation is most stable, followed by secondary, then primary, the dehydration rate order becomes III > IV > II > I.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The given alcohols are:
        • I = primary alcohol
        • II = branched primary alcohol
        • III = tertiary alcohol
        • IV = secondary alcohol
      • In acid-mediated dehydration:
        • the alcohol first gets protonated
        • water leaves
        • a carbocation is formed in the key slow step for most secondary and tertiary cases
      • So the faster the carbocation forms, the faster the dehydration.
      • Carbocation stability order is:
        • tertiary \(>\) secondary \(>\) primary
      • Now compare the given compounds:
        • III gives a tertiary carbocation, so it is fastest.
        • IV gives a secondary carbocation, so it is next.
        • II and I are both primary, but II is more substituted in the nearby carbon framework and is more favorable than I.
        • I is the least favorable, so it is slowest.
      • Hence the order is:
        • III > IV > II > I
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Dehydration of alcohol under acidic conditions often follows:
        • \(E1\) pathway for secondary and tertiary alcohols
        • rate depends strongly on carbocation stability
      • More stable carbocation means lower activation energy and faster reaction.
      • This is why tertiary alcohols dehydrate most easily.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) exactly follows the carbocation stability order from the given structures:
        • III \(>\) IV \(>\) II \(>\) I
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) and (c) wrongly place II ahead of III.
      • (b) wrongly places IV ahead of III, but tertiary alcohol should dehydrate faster than secondary alcohol.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For acid-catalyzed dehydration, first identify alcohol type:
        • \(3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ\)
      • That alone usually solves most such questions quickly.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of rate is III > IV > II > I.
      • Hence, the correct option is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Dehydration of alcohols
      • Subtopic: Effect of carbocation stability on dehydration rate
    • Correct answer: (b)
    • Explanation:
      • We must follow the reaction sequence step by step. Benzene first gets brominated, then methylation occurs para to bromine, then the aryl bromide forms a Grignard reagent, and finally that Grignard reagent adds to acetaldehyde. On hydrolysis, the product is \(p\)-methyl phenyl ethanol derivative, which matches option (b).
    • Reaction sequence in the question
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Bromination of benzene
        • \(Br_2/FeBr_3\) causes electrophilic aromatic substitution.
        • Benzene gives bromobenzene.
      • Step 2: Friedel-Crafts alkylation
        • Now bromobenzene reacts with \(CH_3Cl/AlCl_3\).
        • Bromine is deactivating but ortho-para directing.
        • So the incoming \(CH_3\) group goes mainly at the para position because para product is less sterically hindered than ortho.
        • This gives mainly para-bromotoluene.
      • Step 3: Formation of Grignard reagent
        • With \(Mg\) in dry ether, the \(C-Br\) bond converts into \(C-MgBr\).
        • So para-bromotoluene becomes \(p\)-tolyl magnesium bromide.
      • Step 4: Reaction with acetaldehyde
        • Grignard reagents attack the carbonyl carbon of aldehydes and ketones.
        • Here the reagent attacks \(CH_3CHO\).
        • This forms an alkoxide intermediate:
          • \(p-CH_3C_6H_4-CH(OMgBr)-CH_3\)
      • Step 5: Hydrolysis
        • On adding water, the alkoxide becomes alcohol.
        • So the final product is:
          • \(p-CH_3C_6H_4-CH(OH)-CH_3\)
      • This is a secondary alcohol attached to a para-methyl substituted benzene ring.
      • That matches option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines several standard organic reactions in one chain:
        • electrophilic aromatic substitution
        • directive influence of substituents
        • Grignard reagent formation
        • nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds
      • Important theory points:
        • halogens are deactivating but ortho-para directing
        • Grignard reagent behaves like a carbon nucleophile
        • reaction of \(RMgX\) with an aldehyde other than formaldehyde gives a secondary alcohol after hydrolysis
      • Since the carbonyl compound here is acetaldehyde, the product must be a secondary alcohol, not a tertiary alcohol.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because it shows the para-methyl substituted ring attached to \(CH(OH)CH_3\).
      • That is exactly what comes from the reaction of \(p\)-tolyl magnesium bromide with acetaldehyde followed by hydrolysis.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options (a) and (c) show a tertiary alcohol type carbon center, which would require reaction with a ketone, not acetaldehyde.
      • Option (c) also places methyl substitution ortho, whereas para substitution is the major product in the Friedel-Crafts step.
      • Option (d) has the ortho-methyl arrangement, which is not the major product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In long reaction chains, do not panic. Track one step at a time:
        • ring substitution position
        • what Grignard becomes
        • what type of alcohol aldehyde or ketone will give
      • Quick memory:
        • Grignard + aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) secondary alcohol
        • Grignard + ketone \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol
      • That alone eliminates options (a) and (c).
    • Final result:
      • The major product is the para-substituted secondary alcohol shown in option (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Electrophilic substitution and Grignard reagents
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts alkylation, aryl Grignard formation, and addition to aldehydes
    • Correct answer: (c) \(C_9H_{12}O\)
    • Explanation:
      • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) to form acetophenone by Friedel-Crafts acylation. Then methylmagnesium bromide adds a methyl group to the carbonyl carbon of acetophenone. After hydrolysis, a tertiary alcohol is formed whose molecular formula is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Formation of acetophenone and its Grignard reaction
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Friedel-Crafts acylation
        • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride \((CH_3COCl)\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\).
        • This introduces the acetyl group \((COCH_3)\) onto benzene.
        • So product \(P\) is acetophenone:
          • \(C_6H_5COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Reaction with methylmagnesium bromide
        • \(CH_3MgBr\) is a Grignard reagent.
        • It attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetophenone.
        • A methyl group gets added to the carbonyl carbon.
      • Step 3: Hydrolysis
        • On treatment with water, the alkoxide becomes alcohol.
        • The product is:
          • \(C_6H_5C(OH)(CH_3)_2\)
      • Now count atoms carefully:
        • Phenyl group = \(C_6H_5\)
        • Two methyl groups = \(C_2H_6\)
        • One \(OH\) group = \(OH\)
      • Total:
        • Carbon = \(6 + 2 + 1 = 9\)
        • Hydrogen = \(5 + 6 + 1 = 12\)
        • Oxygen = 1
      • So the molecular formula is:
        • \(C_9H_{12}O\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Friedel-Crafts acylation converts benzene into an aryl ketone.
      • Grignard reagents add to carbonyl compounds:
        • with aldehydes they give primary or secondary alcohols depending on the aldehyde
        • with ketones they give tertiary alcohols
      • Since acetophenone is a ketone, adding \(CH_3MgBr\) must give a tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because the final product \(Q\) is \(C_6H_5C(OH)(CH_3)_2\), whose formula is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options (a) and (b) have only 8 carbons, but the final product clearly contains 9 carbons.
      • Option (d) has the wrong hydrogen count.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Quick route:
        • benzene + acetyl chloride \(\rightarrow\) acetophenone
        • ketone + Grignard reagent \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol
      • Then just write the structure and count atoms. That avoids confusion from formula guessing.
    • Final result:
      • The molecular formula of \(Q\) is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Friedel-Crafts acylation and Grignard reagents
      • Subtopic: Reaction of ketones with Grignard reagent and molecular formula determination
    • Correct answer: (b)
    • Explanation:
      • In the first reaction, tertiary butoxide ion reacts with methyl bromide through \(S_N2\) because methyl bromide cannot undergo elimination and is excellent for backside attack. So \(P\) is tert-butyl methyl ether. In the second reaction, methoxide reacts with tertiary butyl bromide, but tertiary alkyl halides do not undergo \(S_N2\) easily due to steric hindrance, so elimination occurs and \(Q\) becomes 2-methylpropene. Hence the correct pair is option (b).
    • Formation of ether in the first reaction
      Elimination in the second reaction
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • First reaction: sodium tert-butoxide + methyl bromide
      • The reacting species is tert-butoxide ion:
        • \((CH_3)_3CO^-\)
      • Methyl bromide is a methyl halide, so it is highly suitable for \(S_N2\) reaction.
      • There is no \(\beta\)-carbon on methyl bromide for elimination, so \(E2\) is not possible here.
      • Therefore the nucleophile attacks the methyl carbon and forms the ether:
        • \((CH_3)_3C-O-CH_3\)
      • So \(P\) is tert-butyl methyl ether.
      • Second reaction: sodium methoxide + tert-butyl bromide
      • Now the substrate is tertiary butyl bromide, a tertiary alkyl halide.
      • Tertiary alkyl halides are too sterically crowded for easy \(S_N2\) attack.
      • With a strong base like methoxide, elimination becomes favored.
      • So \(E2\) occurs, removing \(HBr\) and forming the alkene:
        • \((CH_3)_2C=CH_2\)
      • This alkene is 2-methylpropene.
      • Hence:
        • \(P =\) tert-butyl methyl ether
        • \(Q =\) 2-methylpropene
      • That matches option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question tests the competition between substitution and elimination.
      • Main principles:
        • methyl halides strongly favor \(S_N2\)
        • tertiary alkyl halides do not favor \(S_N2\) because of steric hindrance
        • strong bases with tertiary halides usually give \(E2\)
      • The first reaction is essentially Williamson ether synthesis:
        • alkoxide + primary or methyl halide \(\rightarrow\) ether
      • The second reaction is a classic elimination of tertiary butyl bromide.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) correctly shows:
        • \(P\) as tert-butyl methyl ether
        • \(Q\) as 2-methylpropene
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) wrongly shows an \(OBr\) type product, which is not formed here.
      • Option (c) gives alcohol or ether products inconsistent with the actual reaction pathways.
      • Option (d) wrongly shows ether formation in both cases, but the second reaction does not proceed by \(S_N2\) due to strong steric hindrance.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Use this fast rule:
        • methyl halide + alkoxide \(\rightarrow\) ether by \(S_N2\)
        • tertiary halide + strong base \(\rightarrow\) alkene by \(E2\)
      • So without drawing the full mechanism, you can often predict:
        • first gives ether
        • second gives alkene
    • Final result:
      • \(P\) is tert-butyl methyl ether and \(Q\) is 2-methylpropene. So the correct option is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Haloalkanes and ethers
      • Subtopic: \(S_N2\), \(E2\), Williamson ether synthesis, and steric effect
    • Correct answer: (d) A - IV, B - II, C - III, D - I
    • Explanation:
      • This is a direct named-reaction matching question. Each transformation is associated with one standard reagent set. Once those famous conversions are remembered, the answer becomes straightforward.
    • Standard conversions used in the match
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • (A) Acid chloride to aldehyde
        • Acid chlorides are converted to aldehydes by Rosenmund reduction.
        • The reagent is:
          • \(H_2\), Pd - \(BaSO_4\)
        • So, \(A \rightarrow IV\).
      • (B) Benzene to benzaldehyde
        • Benzene is converted to benzaldehyde by Gattermann-Koch reaction.
        • The reagents are:
          • CO, HCl, anhyd. \(AlCl_3\)
        • So, \(B \rightarrow II\).
      • (C) Acetylene to aldehyde
        • Acetylene on hydration in presence of \(HgSO_4\) and \(H_2SO_4\) gives enol first, which tautomerizes to acetaldehyde.
        • So, \(C \rightarrow III\).
      • (D) Ester to aldehyde
        • Esters are selectively reduced to aldehydes by DIBAL-H under controlled conditions.
        • So, \(D \rightarrow I\).
      • Hence the correct full match is:
        • \(A - IV\)
        • \(B - II\)
        • \(C - III\)
        • \(D - I\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question is built on very standard organic name-reaction memory points.
      • Important facts:
        • Rosenmund reduction: acid chloride \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • Gattermann-Koch reaction: benzene \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde
        • \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\): hydration of alkyne
        • DIBAL-H: controlled reduction of esters or nitriles to aldehydes
      • These are highly exam-relevant conversion shortcuts and often come as matching questions.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is the only option that correctly combines all four standard conversions.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • The other options wrongly interchange DIBAL-H and Rosenmund reduction, or confuse alkyne hydration with other conversions.
      • A very common trap is to forget that DIBAL-H is used for ester to aldehyde, not acid chloride to aldehyde.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize these four in one line:
        • acid chloride \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde: Rosenmund
        • benzene \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde: Gattermann-Koch
        • acetylene \(\rightarrow\) acetaldehyde: \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\)
        • ester \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde: DIBAL-H
      • That one memory set is enough to solve this question in seconds.
    • Final result:
      • The correct match is A - IV, B - II, C - III, D - I.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Name reactions and functional group interconversions
      • Subtopic: Rosenmund reduction, Gattermann-Koch reaction, alkyne hydration, and DIBAL-H reduction
    • Correct answer: (d) II, IV and VI
    • Explanation:
      • Iodoform test is given by compounds containing the \(CH_3CO-\) group or those alcohols which can be oxidized to that group, namely \(CH_3CH(OH)-\). Among the given compounds, only II, IV, and VI contain these required structural units.
    • Iodoform-positive structural units in the given set
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The iodoform test is positive for:
        • methyl ketones containing \(CH_3CO-\)
        • secondary alcohols containing \(CH_3CH(OH)-\), because they oxidize to methyl ketones
      • Now check each compound one by one.
      • (I) \(CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3\)
        • This is not a methyl ketone.
        • The carbonyl carbon is attached to ethyl groups on both sides.
        • So it does not give iodoform test.
      • (II) \(CH_3CH(OH)CH_3\)
        • This is isopropyl alcohol, a secondary alcohol of type \(CH_3CH(OH)-\).
        • It gives positive iodoform test.
      • (III)
        • The cyclic ketone shown does not contain the required \(CH_3CO-\) unit.
        • So it does not give the test.
      • (IV) \(CH_3CH_2COCH_3\)
        • This is a methyl ketone.
        • It contains the \(CH_3CO-\) group.
        • So it gives positive iodoform test.
      • (V) PhCOPh
        • This is benzophenone.
        • There is no \(CH_3CO-\) group here.
        • So it does not give the test.
      • (VI)
        • This is acetophenone, \(C_6H_5COCH_3\).
        • It contains the methyl ketone group \(CH_3CO-\).
        • So it gives positive iodoform test.
      • Hence the correct set is:
        • II, IV and VI
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Iodoform test identifies compounds that can produce \(CHI_3\), the yellow precipitate of iodoform.
      • The most important structures to remember are:
        • \(CH_3CO-\)
        • \(CH_3CH(OH)-\)
      • The second type gives the test because it first oxidizes to a methyl ketone under the reaction conditions.
      • This is one of the most frequently tested qualitative organic analysis reactions.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because only II, IV, and VI possess the required methyl ketone or related alcohol pattern.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options containing I, III, or V are wrong because those compounds do not contain the required \(CH_3CO-\) or \(CH_3CH(OH)-\) unit.
      • A frequent trap is benzophenone, which is a ketone but not a methyl ketone.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Do not ask only, “Is it a ketone?”
      • Ask specifically:
        • Is it a methyl ketone?
        • Or can it oxidize to a methyl ketone?
      • That single check solves most iodoform questions quickly.
    • Final result:
      • The compounds giving positive iodoform test are II, IV and VI.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Qualitative analysis of organic compounds
      • Subtopic: Iodoform test
    • Correct answer: (c) III, IV only
    • Explanation:
      • \(NaHCO_3\) reacts with acids stronger than carbonic acid, so ordinary carboxylic acids react with it and release \(CO_2\), but phenols do not. In the given set, III and IV are carboxylic acids, whereas I and II are phenolic compounds. Therefore, only III and IV react with \(NaHCO_3\).
    • Compounds that do and do not react with sodium bicarbonate
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The test with \(NaHCO_3\) is mainly used to identify carboxylic acids.
      • Carboxylic acids react as:
        • \(RCOOH + NaHCO_3 \rightarrow RCOONa + H_2O + CO_2 \uparrow\)
      • The brisk effervescence of \(CO_2\) is the key observation.
      • Now examine the given compounds:
        • I is a substituted phenol, not a carboxylic acid.
        • II is phenol, also not a carboxylic acid.
        • III is benzoic acid, so it reacts with \(NaHCO_3\).
        • IV is nitro-substituted benzoic acid, so it also reacts with \(NaHCO_3\).
      • Therefore, only III and IV react.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Phenols are acidic, but they are weaker acids than carboxylic acids.
      • \(NaHCO_3\) is not strong enough to deprotonate ordinary phenols effectively.
      • But carboxylic acids are sufficiently acidic and therefore react with sodium bicarbonate.
      • This distinction is a very important laboratory separation principle:
        • carboxylic acids react with \(NaHCO_3\)
        • phenols usually do not
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because only III and IV contain the \(COOH\) group and can react with \(NaHCO_3\) to release carbon dioxide.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Any option containing I or II is wrong because phenolic \(OH\) does not normally react with sodium bicarbonate.
      • A common trap is to think that because phenol is acidic, it must react with \(NaHCO_3\), but its acidity is not strong enough for that test.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Use this quick rule:
        • \(COOH \Rightarrow\) reacts with \(NaHCO_3\)
        • phenolic \(OH \Rightarrow\) does not react with \(NaHCO_3\)
      • If the group is carboxyl, expect effervescence. If it is just phenol, no effervescence.
    • Final result:
      • The compounds that react with \(NaHCO_3\) are III and IV only.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Acidic character of organic compounds
      • Subtopic: Reaction of carboxylic acids and phenols with sodium bicarbonate
    • Correct answer: (*) Chlorobenzene
    • Explanation:
      • The first step converts phenol into benzene by heating with zinc dust. In the second step, benzene reacts with chlorine in the presence of anhydrous \(FeCl_3\) to give chlorobenzene by electrophilic aromatic substitution. So the major product is chlorobenzene.
    • Stepwise conversion shown in the reaction
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Phenol with \(Zn, \Delta\)
        • Phenol on heating with zinc dust gets reduced to benzene.
        • The \(OH\) group is removed in this reduction.
        • So:
          • \(\text{Phenol} \xrightarrow{Zn,\Delta} \text{Benzene}\)
      • Step 2: Benzene with \(Cl_2/anhyd.\,FeCl_3\)
        • This is chlorination of benzene.
        • \(FeCl_3\) acts as Lewis acid catalyst and helps generate the electrophile.
        • Benzene undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution to form chlorobenzene.
      • So the final major product is:
        • chlorobenzene
      • This matches the structure shown at the top-right in the question image, marked in the solution as the correct one.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Phenol can be reduced to benzene by zinc dust.
      • This is a standard conversion used in aromatic chemistry.
      • Chlorination of benzene in presence of \(FeCl_3\) is a classic electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.
      • The catalyst polarizes \(Cl_2\), helping formation of the effective electrophile for ring substitution.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The correct product must come from benzene after the first step, not from phenol directly.
      • So chlorination happens on benzene, giving only chlorobenzene, not chlorophenol.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Any chlorophenol option is wrong because phenol is first converted to benzene before chlorination.
      • The unchanged phenol option is also wrong because both steps are reactive and lead to a new product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In multistep conversions, always finish step 1 completely before thinking about step 2.
      • Here the key trap is forgetting that phenol becomes benzene first.
      • Once you see that, the second step is simply benzene chlorination.
    • Final result:
      • The major product is chlorobenzene.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aromatic compounds
      • Subtopic: Reduction of phenol to benzene and electrophilic chlorination of benzene
    • Correct answer: (c)
    • Explanation:
      • Toluene first undergoes benzylic bromination to form benzyl bromide. That benzyl bromide gives a Grignard reagent with magnesium, which reacts with formaldehyde to form 2-phenylethanol after hydrolysis. Finally, \(PBr_3\) converts the alcohol into the corresponding alkyl bromide, giving \(C_6H_5CH_2CH_2Br\).
    • Stepwise conversion from toluene to phenethyl bromide
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: \(Br_2\), UV light
        • Toluene under UV light undergoes free-radical side-chain bromination, not ring bromination.
        • So the benzylic hydrogen is replaced.
        • Product:
          • \(C_6H_5CH_2Br\) (benzyl bromide)
      • Step 2: \(Mg\)
        • Benzyl bromide reacts with magnesium to form the Grignard reagent:
          • \(C_6H_5CH_2MgBr\)
      • Step 3 and 4: \(CH_2O\) followed by \(H_3O^+\)
        • Grignard reagent adds to formaldehyde.
        • Reaction with formaldehyde always increases the carbon chain by one carbon and gives a primary alcohol after hydrolysis.
        • So the product becomes:
          • \(C_6H_5CH_2CH_2OH\)
      • Step 5: \(PBr_3\)
        • \(PBr_3\) converts alcohols into alkyl bromides.
        • So:
          • \(C_6H_5CH_2CH_2OH \rightarrow C_6H_5CH_2CH_2Br\)
      • This matches option (c).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Important reaction ideas involved here:
        • \(Br_2/h\nu\) on toluene gives benzylic bromination
        • \(RBr + Mg \rightarrow RMgBr\)
        • Grignard reagent + formaldehyde \(\rightarrow\) primary alcohol after hydrolysis
        • \(PBr_3\) converts alcohol to alkyl bromide
      • This is a classic multistep organic-conversion question where each step is individually standard.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because it shows the final product phenethyl bromide, \(C_6H_5CH_2CH_2Br\), obtained after one-carbon chain extension and conversion of alcohol to bromide.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options showing bromination directly on the aromatic ring are wrong because \(Br_2/h\nu\) here causes side-chain bromination.
      • Options retaining only one-carbon side chain miss the formaldehyde chain-extension step.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Two fast clues solve this:
        • \(Br_2/h\nu\) on toluene \(\Rightarrow\) benzylic bromide
        • Grignard + formaldehyde \(\Rightarrow\) one-carbon homologation to primary alcohol
      • Then \(PBr_3\) simply changes \(OH\) to \(Br\).
    • Final result:
      • The major product is \(C_6H_5CH_2CH_2Br\), so the correct option is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Haloalkanes and Grignard reagents
      • Subtopic: Benzylic bromination, Grignard reaction with formaldehyde, and alcohol to bromide conversion
    • Correct answer: (b) A > C > B > D
    • Explanation:
      • \(pK_a\) is inversely related to acidity. So the stronger the acid, the smaller the \(pK_a\). Nitro group increases acidity by withdrawing electron density. At para and ortho positions it works through both \(-I\) and \(-R\) effects, while at meta position it mainly shows \(-I\) effect. Hence nitrophenols are more acidic than phenol, and therefore have smaller \(pK_a\) values. This gives the decreasing \(pK_a\) order: phenol > meta-nitrophenol > ortho-nitrophenol > para-nitrophenol.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The question asks for decreasing order of \(pK_a\), not decreasing acidity.
      • Remember:
        • higher acidity \(\Rightarrow\) lower \(pK_a\)
        • lower acidity \(\Rightarrow\) higher \(pK_a\)
      • Phenol (A)
        • It has no strongly electron-withdrawing nitro group.
        • So it is the least acidic among these four.
        • Therefore it has the highest \(pK_a\).
      • Meta-nitrophenol (C)
        • At meta position, nitro group cannot stabilize the phenoxide ion by resonance effectively.
        • It mainly acts through \(-I\) effect.
        • So it is more acidic than phenol, but less acidic than ortho- and para-nitrophenol.
      • Ortho-nitrophenol (B) and para-nitrophenol (D)
        • At ortho and para positions, the nitro group can withdraw electron density by both \(-I\) and \(-R\) effects.
        • This stabilizes the phenoxide ion strongly and increases acidity.
      • Between ortho and para:
        • para-nitrophenol is more acidic than ortho-nitrophenol
        • because ortho-nitrophenol shows intramolecular hydrogen bonding, which stabilizes the neutral molecule and makes proton loss slightly less favorable
      • So acidity order is:
        • \(D > B > C > A\)
      • Hence decreasing \(pK_a\) order is the reverse:
        • \(A > C > B > D\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Acidity of phenols depends on stability of the phenoxide ion formed after loss of \(H^+\).
      • Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity because they stabilize the negative charge in phenoxide ion.
      • Nitro group is strongly withdrawing:
        • meta position: mainly \(-I\) effect
        • ortho/para positions: both \(-I\) and \(-R\) effects
      • Intramolecular hydrogen bonding in ortho-nitrophenol affects its acidity and its physical behavior.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because it gives the decreasing \(pK_a\) order, not decreasing acidity order.
      • That is:
        • Phenol > meta-nitrophenol > ortho-nitrophenol > para-nitrophenol
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Some options mix up acidity order with \(pK_a\) order.
      • The most common trap is to forget that stronger acid means smaller \(pK_a\).
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Always do this in two steps:
        • first find acidity order
        • then reverse it if the question asks for decreasing \(pK_a\)
      • That avoids the most common mistake in acidity-\(pK_a\) questions.
    • Final result:
      • The decreasing order of \(pK_a\) is A > C > B > D.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Phenols
      • Subtopic: Effect of nitro group on acidity and \(pK_a\)
    • Correct answer: (d) (ii) > (iii) > (iv) > (i)
    • Explanation:
      • Williamson’s ether synthesis proceeds through an \(S_N2\) mechanism. So the less hindered and more electron-deficient the carbon attached to halogen, the faster the reaction. Allylic halide (ii) is the most reactive, the straight-chain primary halide (iii) comes next, the branched primary halide (iv) is slower, and the highly hindered neopentyl-type halide (i) is the least reactive.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Williamson ether synthesis involves attack of an alkoxide ion on an alkyl halide through:
        • \(S_N2\) mechanism
      • In \(S_N2\), reactivity is higher when:
        • steric hindrance is lower
        • the carbon attached to halogen is more accessible
      • Now compare the given alkyl chlorides.
      • (ii) Allyl chloride
        • \(Cl-CH_2-CH=CH_2\)
        • Allylic halides are especially reactive in \(S_N2\).
        • The adjacent double bond helps and the \(CH_2\) attached to chlorine is highly accessible.
        • So (ii) is the most reactive.
      • (iii) n-Propyl chloride
        • This is an unbranched primary alkyl chloride.
        • So it reacts readily by \(S_N2\).
      • (iv) Isobutyl chloride
        • It is still primary, but more branched than (iii).
        • So it is less reactive than (iii) because of greater steric hindrance.
      • (i)
        • This is a highly hindered primary halide of neopentyl type.
        • Although the carbon bearing chlorine is primary, the adjacent carbon is heavily substituted.
        • This causes severe steric hindrance and makes \(S_N2\) attack very difficult.
        • So (i) is the least reactive.
      • Hence the decreasing order is:
        • \((ii) > (iii) > (iv) > (i)\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Williamson ether synthesis is best with primary alkyl halides because it proceeds by \(S_N2\).
      • General reactivity in \(S_N2\):
        • allyl, benzyl > primary > secondary \(\gg\) tertiary
      • Also, branching near the reactive carbon strongly slows \(S_N2\).
      • Neopentyl halides are famous for being unusually slow even though they are primary.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because it follows the true \(S_N2\) reactivity trend:
        • allylic primary \(>\) straight primary \(>\) branched primary \(>\) neopentyl-type
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Any option putting the neopentyl-type halide too high is wrong because its steric hindrance is severe.
      • Any option placing isobutyl chloride above n-propyl chloride is also wrong for \(S_N2\) reasoning.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For Williamson synthesis, think only one thing first:
        • Which alkyl halide is best for \(S_N2\)?
      • Then rank by:
        • allylic activation
        • least branching
        • avoid neopentyl-type crowding
    • Final result:
      • The decreasing order of reactivity is (ii) > (iii) > (iv) > (i).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
      • Topic: Williamson ether synthesis
      • Subtopic: \(S_N2\) reactivity order of alkyl halides
    • Correct answer: (b) \(CrO_2Cl_2\) ; Cu, 573 K
    • Explanation:
      • In reaction (A), toluene is converted to benzaldehyde. This is the classic Etard reaction, which uses chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\) in \(CS_2\) followed by hydrolysis. In reaction (B), benzyl alcohol is converted to benzaldehyde by passing its vapours over heated copper at 573 K. So \(X = CrO_2Cl_2\) and \(Y = Cu, 573 K\).
    • Etard oxidation of toluene to benzaldehyde
      Oxidation of benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde over heated copper
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Reaction (A): toluene \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde
        • This is not complete side-chain oxidation to benzoic acid.
        • The product is specifically benzaldehyde, so we need a reagent that selectively oxidizes the benzylic methyl group only up to the aldehyde stage.
        • The standard reagent for this is:
          • \(CrO_2Cl_2\) in \(CS_2\), followed by hydrolysis
        • This reaction is called the Etard reaction.
        • So:
          • \(X = CrO_2Cl_2\)
      • Reaction (B): benzyl alcohol \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde
        • Primary alcohols can be converted to aldehydes by controlled oxidation or by catalytic dehydrogenation.
        • Here the reaction shown corresponds to passing benzyl alcohol over heated copper.
        • Copper at 573 K removes hydrogen from the alcohol and gives the aldehyde.
        • So:
          • \(Y = Cu,\;573\,K\)
      • Hence the correct pair is:
        • \(X = CrO_2Cl_2\)
        • \(Y = Cu,\;573\,K\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • The Etard reaction is a named reaction used to convert the side-chain methyl group of aromatic hydrocarbons into an aldehyde using chromyl chloride.
      • Important point:
        • strong oxidants like \(KMnO_4\) usually oxidize side chains fully to carboxylic acids
        • \(CrO_2Cl_2\) gives aldehyde selectively
      • Benzyl alcohol on heated copper undergoes catalytic dehydrogenation:
        • \(C_6H_5CH_2OH \rightarrow C_6H_5CHO\)
      • This is a standard conversion of primary alcohol to aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because it gives exactly the two reagents needed:
        • \(CrO_2Cl_2\) for Etard oxidation of toluene
        • Cu, 573 K for converting benzyl alcohol to benzaldehyde
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • \(KMnO_4\) generally over-oxidizes the side chain to benzoic acid rather than stopping at benzaldehyde.
      • \(CrO_3/H_2SO_4\) is too strong and would also push oxidation further.
      • \(AlCl_3\) and \(H_2O_2\) do not fit the specific transformations shown.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For toluene \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde, immediately think:
        • Etard reaction = \(CrO_2Cl_2\)
      • For benzyl alcohol \(\rightarrow\) benzaldehyde, a standard school-level clue is:
        • Cu / 573 K
    • Final result:
      • \(X = CrO_2Cl_2\) and \(Y = Cu,\;573\,K\). So the correct option is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Preparation of aldehydes
      • Subtopic: Etard reaction and oxidation/dehydrogenation of benzyl alcohol
    • Correct answer: (d) carboxylate anion
    • Explanation:
      • PCC oxidizes a primary alcohol to an aldehyde. Then ammoniacal silver nitrate solution, which is Tollens’ reagent, oxidizes that aldehyde further to a carboxylate anion in basic medium, while silver is reduced and a silver mirror is formed.
    • Primary alcohol to aldehyde, then aldehyde to carboxylate ion
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Action of PCC on a primary alcohol
        • PCC is a mild oxidizing agent.
        • It converts a primary alcohol \(RCH_2OH\) into an aldehyde \(RCHO\).
        • So the product \(P\) is:
          • an aldehyde
      • Step 2: Action of ammoniacal silver nitrate on aldehyde
        • Ammoniacal silver nitrate is Tollens’ reagent.
        • Tollens’ reagent oxidizes aldehydes in alkaline medium.
        • The aldehyde is converted into:
          • carboxylate ion
        • At the same time, silver ion is reduced to metallic silver, giving the silver mirror test.
      • Therefore, product \(P\) finally gives:
        • carboxylate anion
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • PCC is used when we want controlled oxidation:
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
      • Tollens’ reagent is an oxidizing reagent for aldehydes.
      • Because it works in alkaline solution, the oxidation product is first obtained as the carboxylate ion, not directly as free carboxylic acid.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because PCC gives an aldehyde, and Tollens’ reagent oxidizes that aldehyde to the corresponding carboxylate anion.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Anhydride is not formed in this oxidation sequence.
      • Aldehyde is only the intermediate \(P\), not the final product after Tollens’ reagent.
      • Amide formation does not occur here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Remember this two-step chain:
        • primary alcohol + PCC \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • aldehyde + Tollens’ reagent \(\rightarrow\) carboxylate ion
      • That directly gives the answer without any long mechanism.
    • Final result:
      • The product formed is carboxylate anion.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Oxidation reactions
      • Subtopic: PCC oxidation and Tollens’ test
    • Correct answer: (c) \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CHBrCO_2H\)
    • Explanation:
      • The starting compound is a methyl ketone, 2-hexanone. With \(NaOBr\), it undergoes the haloform reaction to give a carboxylate with one carbon less on the carbonyl side. After acidification, pentanoic acid is formed. Then pentanoic acid undergoes Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky bromination with \(Br_2/Red\,P\), giving the \(\alpha\)-bromo acid \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CHBrCO_2H\).
    • Haloform cleavage followed by Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky bromination
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is:
        • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2COCH_3\)
      • This is 2-hexanone, a methyl ketone.
      • Step 1: \(NaOBr\)
        • Methyl ketones give the haloform reaction.
        • The \(-COCH_3\) part is cleaved to form:
          • a carboxylate ion
          • haloform \((CHBr_3)\)
        • Here the carboxylate formed is:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2COO^-\)
      • Step 2: \(H_3O^+\)
        • Acidification converts the carboxylate ion into:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2COOH\)
        • This is pentanoic acid.
      • Step 3 and 4: \(Br_2/Red\,P\), then \(H_2O\)
        • This is the Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction.
        • HVZ brominates the \(\alpha\)-carbon of a carboxylic acid.
        • So pentanoic acid becomes:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CHBrCO_2H\)
      • Therefore the major final product is option (c).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Two standard named reactions are involved:
        • Haloform reaction: methyl ketone \(\rightarrow\) carboxylate + haloform
        • Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction: carboxylic acid \(\rightarrow\) \(\alpha\)-bromo acid
      • The haloform reaction shortens the carbon chain attached to the methyl ketone end by removing the \(CH_3CO-\) terminal methyl as haloform.
      • HVZ always introduces bromine at the carbon next to \(COOH\).
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because the sequence gives pentanoic acid first, and then \(\alpha\)-bromination of pentanoic acid produces \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CHBrCO_2H\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) is too short by one carbon.
      • Option (b) places bromine at the wrong carbon.
      • Option (d) is an acyl bromide type structure, not the hydrolyzed final HVZ product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • methyl ketone + hypobromite \(\Rightarrow\) think haloform
        • \(Br_2/Red\,P\) on acid \(\Rightarrow\) think HVZ, alpha-bromination
      • That lets you break the problem into two easy standard reactions.
    • Final result:
      • The major product is \(CH_3CH_2CH_2CHBrCO_2H\), so the correct option is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Methyl ketone reactions and carboxylic acid reactions
      • Subtopic: Haloform reaction and Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky reaction
    • Correct answer: (c) \(Ca(OH)_2\), \(NaOH\), \(C_6H_5COOH\)
    • Explanation: The inorganic compound used for water softening is \(Ca(OH)_2\). It reacts with \(Na_2CO_3\) to give \(NaOH\), which is strongly alkaline with pH \(= 14\). Also, \(Ca(OH)_2\) gives a cloudy precipitate with \(CO_2\) due to formation of \(CaCO_3\). Finally, \(NaOH\) and \(CaO\) together act as soda lime, which decarboxylates benzoic acid \((C_6H_5COOH)\) to give benzene \((C_6H_6)\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • We identify each unknown one clue at a time.
      • First clue: compound A is used for water softening.
      • \((Ca(OH)_2)\), that is slaked lime, is used in the lime-soda process of softening hard water. So A = \(Ca(OH)_2\).
      • Second clue: \(A\) reacts with \(Na_2CO_3\) to generate an alkaline compound \(B\) whose pH is 14.
      • The reaction is:
        • \((Ca(OH)_2 + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + 2NaOH)\)
      • The strongly alkaline product here is \((NaOH)\), whose pH is about 14 for a strong concentrated basic solution.
      • So B = \(NaOH\).
      • Third clue: \(A\) reacts with \(CO_2\) to give a cloudy precipitate.
      • This is the familiar lime water test:
        • \((Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 \downarrow + H_2O)\)
      • The white precipitate of \((CaCO_3)\) makes the solution cloudy.
      • Fourth clue: \(B + CaO\) reacts with organic compound \(C\) to give \(C_6H_6\).
      • \((NaOH + CaO)\) is soda lime.
      • Soda lime causes decarboxylation of sodium salts of carboxylic acids or corresponding acids in suitable question logic.
      • To obtain benzene \((C_6H_6)\), the required acid is benzoic acid, \((C_6H_5COOH)\).
      • Thus C = \(C_6H_5COOH\).
      • So the correct set is \(A = Ca(OH)_2\), \(B = NaOH\), \(C = C_6H_5COOH\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Lime-soda process: \((Ca(OH)_2)\) and \((Na_2CO_3)\) are used in water softening to remove temporary and permanent hardness.
      • Lime water test: \((Ca(OH)_2)\) turns milky with \((CO_2)\) because of \((CaCO_3)\) formation.
      • Soda lime: a mixture of \((NaOH)\) and \((CaO)\). It is used to remove the \((COOH)\) group from carboxylic acids or their salts during decarboxylation.
      • General decarboxylation pattern:
        • \((RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow{CaO,\ \Delta} RH + Na_2CO_3)\)
      • For benzoic acid or sodium benzoate, the hydrocarbon formed is benzene:
        • \((C_6H_5COONa + NaOH \xrightarrow{CaO,\ \Delta} C_6H_6 + Na_2CO_3)\)
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Only option (c) correctly satisfies all three clues:
        • water-softening compound = \((Ca(OH)_2)\)
        • strong alkali formed = \((NaOH)\)
        • organic acid giving benzene on soda lime decarboxylation = \((C_6H_5COOH)\)
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) is wrong because \(B\) is not \((Na_2CO_3)\); the strongly alkaline product formed is \((NaOH)\).
      • (b) is wrong because \((C_6H_5CH_2COOH)\) on decarboxylation would not give benzene directly; it would give a different hydrocarbon.
      • (d) is wrong because \(A\) is not calcium metal \((Ca)\); the water-softening reagent here is calcium hydroxide \((Ca(OH)_2)\).
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • The moment you see:
        • used for water softening + gives cloudy ppt with \(CO_2\)
      • you should immediately think of lime water / slaked lime, that is \((Ca(OH)_2)\).
      • And when you see:
        • \(NaOH + CaO\)
      • immediately think soda lime decarboxylation.
      • To get benzene after decarboxylation, the parent acid must be benzoic acid.
    • Final result
      • \(A = Ca(OH)_2\)
      • \(B = NaOH\)
      • \(C = C_6H_5COOH\)
      • So, the correct answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrogen / Purification of water and Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Lime-soda process and decarboxylation
      • Subtopic: Uses of \(Ca(OH)_2\), soda lime, benzoic acid to benzene conversion
    • Correct answer: (c)
    • Explanation: In the presence of peroxide, HBr adds to the double bond by the anti-Markovnikov mechanism. So methylenecyclohexane first forms bromomethylcyclohexane. Treatment with \(KCN\) replaces bromine by \((CN)\), giving a nitrile, and acidic hydrolysis of the nitrile gives the corresponding carboxylic acid \(({-}CH_2COOH)\). This matches option (c).
    • Reaction sequence leading to the product
      • The sequence clearly converts the exocyclic double bond into a \(({-}CH_2COOH)\) side chain attached to cyclohexane.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is methylenecyclohexane, which has an exocyclic double bond.
      • Step 1: \(HBr\) in presence of peroxide \((C_6H_5CO)_2O_2\)
      • This means the reaction follows the peroxide effect, so \(HBr\) adds by anti-Markovnikov addition.
      • Therefore:
        • \(Br\) goes to the terminal \(CH_2\) carbon
        • \(H\) goes to the ring carbon of the double bond
      • So the product after step 1 is cyclohexylmethyl bromide, that is, ring-\((CH_2Br)\).
      • Step 2: \(KCN\)
      • Cyanide ion performs nucleophilic substitution on the alkyl bromide:
        • \(({-}CH_2Br \rightarrow {-}CH_2CN)\)
      • Step 3: \((H_3O^+, \Delta)\)
      • Acidic hydrolysis of nitrile converts \(({-}CN)\) into carboxylic acid:
        • \(({-}CH_2CN \rightarrow {-}CH_2COOH)\)
      • Hence the final product is cyclohexylacetic acid, which matches option (c).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • The peroxide effect is shown only by HBr and leads to anti-Markovnikov addition through a free-radical mechanism.
      • This effect is not shown by HCl or HI under normal exam conditions.
      • \(KCN\) is an ambident nucleophile, but in simple nucleophilic substitution with alkyl halides it generally forms nitriles through carbon attack:
        • \((RBr + KCN \rightarrow RCN)\)
      • Nitriles on acidic hydrolysis give carboxylic acids:
        • \((RCN \xrightarrow{H_3O^+,\Delta} RCOOH)\)
      • So this question is really a three-step concept chain:
        • anti-Markovnikov addition
        • cyanide substitution
        • nitrile hydrolysis
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) contains the product with a \(({-}CH_2COOH)\) side chain attached to cyclohexane.
      • That is exactly what is obtained after the three-step sequence.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) and (b) incorrectly place a methyl group and carboxyl group directly on the ring carbons, which does not follow from the reaction sequence.
      • (d) has a longer side chain than required; only one extra carbon is introduced by cyanide before hydrolysis, giving \(({-}CH_2COOH)\), not a longer chain.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(HBr/peroxide\)
        • then \(KCN\)
        • then hydrolysis
      • think:
        • anti-Markovnikov bromide formation
        • \(Br \rightarrow CN\)
        • \(CN \rightarrow COOH\)
      • This is a very common stepwise-conversion pattern in exams.
    • Final result
      • The major product is the structure shown in option (c).
      • So, the correct answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Organic Conversions
      • Topic: Peroxide effect, nucleophilic substitution and nitrile hydrolysis
      • Subtopic: Anti-Markovnikov addition of HBr followed by \(CN\) substitution and hydrolysis
    • Correct answer: (d) 2-hydroxybenzoic acid
    • Explanation: Intramolecular hydrogen bonding occurs when the donor and acceptor groups are present within the same molecule at suitable positions. In 2-hydroxybenzoic acid \((o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid or salicylic acid), the \((OH)\) group and \((COOH)\) group are ortho to each other, so they can form a stable intramolecular hydrogen bond.
    • Intramolecular hydrogen bonding in \(o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid
      • The image shows the hydrogen bond forming within the same molecule in 2-hydroxybenzoic acid.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • To get intramolecular hydrogen bonding, the molecule must have:
        • a hydrogen-bond donor like \((OH)\)
        • a hydrogen-bond acceptor nearby in the same molecule
        • proper spatial arrangement, usually at ortho position in aromatic compounds
      • Now examine the options:
        • Phenol: it has an \((OH)\) group but no suitably placed acceptor group within the same molecule for intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
        • Benzoic acid: it mainly forms intermolecular hydrogen-bonded dimers, not intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
        • \(p\)-Nitrophenol: the \((OH)\) and \((NO_2)\) groups are para to each other, so they are too far apart for intramolecular hydrogen bonding. It prefers intermolecular hydrogen bonding.
        • 2-hydroxybenzoic acid: the \((OH)\) and \((COOH)\) groups are ortho to each other, so a six-membered chelate-like hydrogen-bonded ring forms within the same molecule.
      • Hence, the correct answer is 2-hydroxybenzoic acid.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Hydrogen bonding can be:
        • intermolecular = between different molecules
        • intramolecular = within the same molecule
      • Intramolecular hydrogen bonding is common when donor and acceptor groups are positioned so that a stable 5-membered or 6-membered ring can form.
      • In aromatic compounds, ortho substitution often favours intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
      • That is why:
        • \(o\)-nitrophenol shows intramolecular hydrogen bonding
        • \(p\)-nitrophenol usually shows intermolecular hydrogen bonding
        • salicylic acid \((o\)-hydroxybenzoic acid) shows intramolecular hydrogen bonding
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) is correct because the ortho arrangement of \((OH)\) and \((COOH)\) allows hydrogen bond formation within the same molecule.
      • This gives stable intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) lacks the necessary nearby acceptor group.
      • (b) forms intermolecular dimeric hydrogen bonding instead.
      • (c) has para substitution, so the groups are too far apart for intramolecular hydrogen bonding.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • In aromatic hydrogen-bonding questions, check the ortho position first.
      • If donor and acceptor are ortho and can make a 5- or 6-membered ring, intramolecular hydrogen bonding is very likely.
      • Memory cue:
        • salicylic acid = intramolecular H-bonding
    • Final result
      • Intramolecular hydrogen bonding is present in 2-hydroxybenzoic acid.
      • So, the correct answer is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrogen bonding
      • Subtopic: Intramolecular hydrogen bonding in aromatic compounds
    • Correct answer: (c) (A) is true but (R) is false
    • Explanation: Tertiary alcohols do produce turbidity immediately with Lucas reagent because they react fastest to form insoluble alkyl chlorides. But the Reason is false because Lucas reagent is not a mixture of conc. \(HNO_3\) and \(ZnCl_2\). It is a mixture of conc. \(HCl\) and anhydrous \(ZnCl_2\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First test the Assertion.
      • Assertion (A): “Tertiary alcohols produce turbidity immediately with Lucas reagent.”
      • This is true.
      • Lucas reagent is used to distinguish primary, secondary and tertiary alcohols based on the rate of formation of alkyl chlorides.
      • Tertiary alcohols react fastest because they form the most stable carbocations, so substitution occurs very quickly.
      • The alkyl chloride formed is insoluble in the reaction medium, so turbidity appears immediately.
      • Now test the Reason.
      • Reason (R): “Lucas reagent is a \(1:1\) mixture of conc. \(HNO_3\) and anhydrous \(ZnCl_2\).”
      • This is false.
      • Lucas reagent is actually a mixture of:
        • conc. \(HCl\)
        • anhydrous \(ZnCl_2\)
      • Since the Assertion is true but the Reason is false, the correct option is (c).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Lucas reagent is used for classifying alcohols by their reaction rate with concentrated hydrochloric acid in presence of \(ZnCl_2\).
      • \(ZnCl_2\) acts as a Lewis acid and helps the substitution reaction.
      • The order of reactivity is:
        • \(3^\circ\) alcohol \(>\) \(2^\circ\) alcohol \(>\) \(1^\circ\) alcohol
      • Observed result:
        • tertiary alcohol: immediate turbidity
        • secondary alcohol: turbidity in a few minutes
        • primary alcohol: no turbidity at room temperature
      • This trend is mainly due to carbocation stability in the substitution process.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) matches the actual facts:
        • Assertion is true
        • Reason is false
      • Tertiary alcohols do react immediately, but the reagent has been stated incorrectly in the Reason.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) and (b) are wrong because the Reason is not true.
      • (d) is wrong because the Assertion is not false.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Always remember the composition of Lucas reagent exactly:
        • conc. \(HCl\) + anhydrous \(ZnCl_2\)
      • And remember the turbidity order:
        • \(3^\circ\): immediate
        • \(2^\circ\): few minutes
        • \(1^\circ\): no turbidity at room temperature
      • The wrong acid in the reagent is a classic trap.
    • Final result
      • Assertion is true.
      • Reason is false.
      • So, the correct answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Distinction between alcohols
      • Subtopic: Lucas reagent and Lucas test
    • Correct answer: (d) \(CH_3COCH_3\)
    • Explanation: The given alcohol is propan-2-ol, which is a secondary alcohol. Secondary alcohols on heating with copper at \(573\,K\) undergo dehydrogenation to give ketones. Therefore, propan-2-ol forms propanone \((CH_3COCH_3)\).
    • Dehydrogenation of secondary alcohol with copper
      • The image shows the direct conversion of the secondary alcohol into a ketone under these conditions.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify the type of alcohol.
      • The structure shown is:
        • \((CH_3)_2CHOH\)
      • This is propan-2-ol, a secondary alcohol.
      • Now recall the rule for alcohol vapours passed over heated copper at \(573\,K\):
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) dehydration product
      • So here propan-2-ol loses hydrogen and becomes:
        • \((CH_3)_2CHOH \xrightarrow{Cu,\,573\,K} (CH_3)_2CO\)
      • The product \((CH_3)_2CO\) is propanone or acetone.
      • Hence option (d) is correct.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Heated copper acts as a dehydrogenating agent for primary and secondary alcohols.
      • The general patterns are:
        • \((RCH_2OH \xrightarrow{Cu,\,573\,K} RCHO + H_2)\)
        • \((R_2CHOH \xrightarrow{Cu,\,573\,K} R_2CO + H_2)\)
      • But tertiary alcohols do not have a hydrogen on the carbon carrying the \((OH)\) group, so they cannot undergo dehydrogenation in the same way. They mainly undergo dehydration.
      • This is a very standard conversion from the chapter on alcohols.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) is correct because the starting alcohol is secondary, and secondary alcohols give ketones on heating with copper at \(573\,K\).
      • The ketone formed here is propanone \((CH_3COCH_3)\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) propane is not formed under these conditions.
